Recapping how to find the reciprocal of a number can be helpful before reviewing the procedures for multiplying and dividing fractions. Pictorial representations, such as area models or partitioned shapes, remain useful for deepening understanding of the differences between these procedures. Encourage students to determine whether a problem requires multiplication or division, initially starting with similar problems involving integers before moving onto fractions. Students can also improve their calculator proficiency by checking their solutions.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Autumn_Higher_Work with fractions_3_Solve problems with fractions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students focus on identifying the correct procedure to solve problems involving fractions. They will select and perform the appropriate operations needed to solve each problem. Pictorial representations can support students in visualising the operations and verifying their reasoning. Initially, showing similar problems involving integers before introducing fractions can help students develop a solid understand of the correct calculation process. Encourage students to identify key words and phrases in word problems, such as “shared equally” for division, to guide their choice of operation. Include solving equations within this step to allow students to practise using their understanding of operations with fractions within abstract structures. Checking their answers with a calculator will help build both confidence and calculator proficiency.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Autumn_Higher_Work with fractions_4_Add and subtract algebraic fractions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn to apply the generalisations for adding and subtracting fractions to algebraic fractions with single terms. Some students may have encountered addition and subtraction of simple algebraic fractions as an extend step in Key Stage 3. Students may need reminding of simplifying algebraic terms, for example, x × x­ æ x2 and 2y + 3y æ 5y. Start with examples that have a common denominator, both numerical and algebraic, to build familiarity and confidence. As students’ confidence increases, progress to examples without common denominators, where students rewrite the fractions by finding equivalent algebraic fractions. Encourage students to simplify their final answers where possible.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Autumn_Higher_Work with fractions_5_Multiply algebraic fractions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn how to apply the generalisations for multiplying fractions with algebraic fractions. Some students may have encountered multiplying and dividing simple algebraic fractions as an extend step in Year 8. Students may need reminding of simplifying algebraic terms, for example, a × c æ ac and 2x × x æ 2x2 Begin by finding the product of a numerical fraction and integer, for example, 2 3 × 4 = 8 3 and compare with an algebraic example, such as x 3 × 4 æ 4 3 x . Use a similar approach with the product of two fractions, for example, 2 3 × 4 5 = (2 × 4) (3 × 5) = 8 15 and a b × c d æ ( × ) ( × ) a c b d æ ac bd . As students’ confidence develops, introduce examples that can be simplified. It can be helpful to remind students to check for common factors in the numerators and denominators before multiplying as this can make the process more efficient.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Autumn_Higher_Work with fractions_6_Divide algebraic fractions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply the generalisations for dividing fractions to algebraic fractions with single terms. Some students may have been introduced to dividing simple algebraic fractions as an extend step in Year 8. Students may need a reminder of writing the reciprocal of a number before making comparisons with writing the reciprocal of an algebraic expression. Begin by dividing a fraction by an integer and explore the similarities and differences with an algebraic case, for example, 2 3 ÷ 5 = 2 15 and x 3 ÷ 5 = x 15 . Apply the generalisations further with the division of two fractions, for example, 2 5 ÷ 3 4 = 2 5 × 4 3 = 8 15 and a b ÷ c d = a b × d c = ad bc . As students’ confidence develops, introduce terms that require simplifying. It can be helpful to remind students to check for common factors in the numerators and denominators before calculating as this can make the process more efficient.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Autumn_Higher_Work with fractions_7_Simplify algebraic fractions by factorising
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students simplify algebraic fractions where one or both the numerator and denominator require factorising. You may wish to discuss the use of the term “cancelling” when simplifying algebraic fractions, as it implies that factors are simply erased rather than simplified through division. Begin with fractions expressed in a factorised form such as 3( + 2) 2( + 2) x x and ( + 1)( + 2) ( + 2) x x x and encourage students to identify common factors that can be simplified through division. It can be helpful to recap factorising linear and quadratic expressions before introducing fractions that require factorising such as x 8 – 4 12 and 3 + 6 9 + 3 2 x x . As students develop confidence, progress to more complex algebraic fractions such as x x x 2 + 3 + 2 + 2 and x x x 2 2 – 4 – 5 + 6 before showing algebraic fractions with varying coefficients of x2

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Autumn_Higher_Work with fractions_8_Add and subtract more complex algebraic fractions (E)
small_step_name: Add and subtract more complex algebraic fractions (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of adding and subtracting simple algebraic fractions to more complex expressions such as x 2 – 2 + 3 + 3 x Ensure students are confident with expanding and simplifying brackets. It can be helpful to first recap addition and subtraction of numerical fractions with common denominators, before introducing algebraic fractions with the same denominator, such as x x 3 – 5 – x x – 5, to reinforce the process of combining like terms in the numerators. As students’ confidence develops, introduce examples that require finding a common denominator, such as 1 x + x 1 – 1 and x x 2 – 2 + 3 + 3 x x Highlight the importance of rewriting each fraction using equivalent fractions before combining them. Encourage students to look for opportunities to factorise and simplify further after combining fractions.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Autumn_Higher_Work with fractions_9_Multiply and divide more complex algebraic fractions (E)
small_step_name: Multiply and divide more complex algebraic fractions (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students extend their understanding of multiplying and dividing algebraic fractions to more complex problems, such as 5 + 3 2 x x ÷ x2 15 – 9 Begin with examples such as a 3 × a + 4 2 before introducing examples with common factors such as 8 3 × a – 5 4 . Encourage students to check for common factors in the numerators/ denominators before multiplying to make the process more efficient. When dividing, reinforce the concept of multiplying by the reciprocal. As students’ confidence develops, introduce examples such as x x 2 2 – 4 – 16 × x x 3 – 12 + 2 , highlighting how factorisation helps simplify both before and after multiplication.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Interpret and represent data_1_Averages and range
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit their Year 8 knowledge by calculating averages and the range from a small data set. Start by reviewing how to calculate the mean, median, mode and range. Draw students’ attention to the fact that the range measures the spread of the data and is not an average. Emphasise that each type of average represents the data differently and encourage students to choose the most appropriate average based on the context, such as selecting the mode for categorical data. Highlight the importance of considering outliers and their impact on the averages. Once students are confident in calculating averages and the range, introduce more complex problems, such as finding an unknown value in a data set when given the mean, median, mode or range, or combinations of these.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Interpret and represent data_2_Averages from an ungrouped frequency table
small_step_name: Averages from an ungrouped frequency table
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students practise calculating the mean, median and mode from ungrouped frequency tables. Students have already covered calculating the mean from a frequency table in Year 8, but this may be their first time calculating the mode and median. Begin by helping students interpret the table, identifying the values and their frequencies. For the mean, guide them to multiply each value by its frequency, total these products and divide by the overall number of data points. When finding the mode, prompt students to identify the most frequent value. For the median, guide students to explore the cumulative frequency and find the middle value either through a cumulative frequency column or an ordered list.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Interpret and represent data_3_Mean from a grouped frequency table
small_step_name: Mean from a grouped frequency table
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students estimate the mean from grouped frequency tables. This may have previously been covered in Year 8 as an extend step. Begin by reviewing how to calculate the mean from raw data to highlight the difference when working with grouped data. Emphasise that when data is grouped, individual values are unknown, so the mean can only be estimated. Explain that the midpoint of each interval is used as a representative value, assuming an even distribution within each group. Model the process step by step, showing how to identify midpoints, multiply them by their corresponding frequencies, sum these products, and divide by the total frequency to find an estimated mean.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Interpret and represent data_4_Averages from a grouped frequency table
small_step_name: Averages from a grouped frequency table
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students identify the median and modal class for grouped data, and estimate the mean. This could be combined with the previous step. Begin by helping students rewrite the data from the frequency table as a list to strengthen their understanding of the median. Highlight the link between finding the median in a list and locating it in the table before introducing the use of cumulative frequency. Model how to calculate the median position using n + 1 2 , and guide students in creating a cumulative frequency column to identify the class interval containing the median. Emphasise that the median is an estimate representing a range of values rather than an exact value. For the modal class, prompt students to identify the class interval with the highest frequency, explaining that this represents the most common range of values, not a single mode.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Interpret and represent data_5_Use data to compare distributions (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students build on their understanding of comparing distributions using averages and range from Year 8. Students who have exclusively followed the support scheme may not have previously encountered comparing distributions. Remind students that the range is a measure of spread, showing how consistent or varied a set of data is, while averages describe a typical value within the data. Ask students to compare two sets of data by commenting on both the average and the range, encouraging them to describe what the values mean in context. Prompt students to notice that a smaller range suggests greater consistency, and that differences in averages can highlight which group performs better or worse overall.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Interpret and represent data_6_Types of data
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit their Year 8 learning on discrete, continuous, quantitative and qualitative data, and explore the distinction between primary and secondary data. Those following the support scheme may not have previously encountered these data types, so it’s important to model clear examples and discuss each one to build understanding. Once students are confident, they can collect primary data through a simple survey or experiment, for example, measuring classmates’ heights or gathering information about their hobbies. Ask students to find secondary data on a related topic, such as national statistics or published survey results. Encourage them to discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each data source: primary data is usually more relevant but takes longer to collect, while secondary data is quicker to obtain but may be less specific or reliable.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Interpret and represent data_7_Sampling
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of data collection by exploring sampling, a method used to gather information from part of a population when it isn’t practical to survey everyone. Begin by defining the population as the entire group being studied, such as all students in a school, and a sample as a smaller group representing it. Introduce random sampling, where each individual has an equal chance of being selected, and systematic sampling, where selections are made at regular intervals. Once students are confident, explore stratified sampling, where the population is divided into groups (strata) and a proportional sample is taken from each. Encourage students to apply proportional reasoning to calculate sample sizes and discuss which method is most reliable in different situations. Emphasise the importance of avoiding bias when selecting samples.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Interpret and represent data_8_Scatter graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students review scatter graphs, building on their prior knowledge from Key Stage 3. The focus is on reinforcing key vocabulary such as “positive correlation”, “negative correlation” and “no correlation”, and developing confidence in interpreting the relationship between two variables from the graph. Draw students’ attention to how the direction and strength of the pattern suggest different types of correlation. Students should also practise drawing a line of best fit and using it to make simple estimates. It is worth noting that, while lines of best fit in maths are straight lines, they may be curved in science depending on the nature of the data. It is valuable to remind students that correlation does not imply causality, and that the absence of a clear linear relationship does not necessarily mean the variables are unconnected.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Interpret and represent data_9_Interpolation and extrapolation (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use scatter graphs to make predictions, with particular emphasis on understanding the difference between interpolation and extrapolation. Encourage discussion about when it is or isn’t appropriate to extrapolate beyond the given data range. Use examples where extrapolation leads to unrealistic or impractical results, such as predicting negative values for height or mass, to highlight the limitations of mathematical models. Make cross-curricular links with science, such as exploring the relationship between the length of a spring and the force applied. While this relationship may appear linear within a certain range, it no longer holds once the spring is overstretched. This helps students appreciate that extrapolation relies on assumptions and reinforces the importance of applying mathematical models carefully and critically.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-calculator methods_1_Place value for integers and decimals
small_step_name: Place value for integers and decimals
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of place value from Key Stage 3. Ensure students can represent integers and decimals on place value charts, highlighting the significance of the columns that represent a digit’s place value. Encourage the use of vocabulary such as “tens”, “tenths” and “hundredths” throughout, as well as their numerical equivalents: “10”, “ 1 10 ”, “ 1 100 ” and so on. This can be useful when highlighting the place value of specific digits in a given number, for example understanding that the number 4032 has 3 tens or a value of 30 Encourage students to focus on the structure of a number, for example saying 2.45 as “two point four five” rather than “two point forty-five”, which can lead to misconceptions such as 2.45 > 2.5

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-calculator methods_2_Compare and order numbers
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use their understanding of place value to compare and order numbers. Encourage students to use inequality notation when comparing two numbers, for example 2.7 > 2.07. Writing digits of numbers in a place value chart can be useful to help students order numbers. Model how to systematically check each place value column, starting from the left with the greatest place value, to compare and order numbers where there are many digits in common, for example 4.317 and 4.173. Once students are confident ordering positive integers and decimals, they could order negative numbers or a combination of fractions, decimals or percentages. Using numbers in context, such as lengths, costs and masses, may be useful.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-calculator methods_3_Add and subtract integers and decimals
small_step_name: Add and subtract integers and decimals
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit strategies to add and subtract integers and decimals covered in Key Stage 3. Remind students of situations where an exchange is needed, for example, when the sum of two digits in the same place value column is greater than 9 Comparing strategies to add or subtract integers and decimals with the same digits can support depth of understanding. For example, comparing 234 – 19 with 23.4 – 1.9 To develop fluency, students should explore different methods depending on the problem, such as rewriting 1000 – 317 as 999 – 316, where the latter calculation does not need exchanges but it gives the same result. If appropriate, students could be encouraged to apply their understanding to solving one-step equations without the use of a calculator, for example, x + 2.92 = 5.8

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-calculator methods_4_Multiply and divide integers and decimals
small_step_name: Multiply and divide integers and decimals
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit strategies to multiply and divide integers and decimals from Key Stage 3. Explore different strategies for multiplying numbers, for example, using an area model or a column method. Begin with multiplying numbers by a single digit and, as confidence develops, move onto multiplying by two-digit numbers and greater. Encourage students to represent remainders as decimals when dividing integers. Compare similar calculations when multiplying or dividing with decimals, such as comparing 928 × 4 and 92.8 × 4 or 327 ÷ 8 and 32.7 ÷ 8, and encourage students to estimate an answer before calculating.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-calculator methods_5_Four operations with directed number
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit adding, subtracting, multiplying and dividing with directed number covered in Key Stage 3. Number lines are a useful representation to help students complete calculations that cross zero, for example, 4 – 9 or –2 + 5. Double sided counters could be used to model addition or subtraction of directed number, ensuring students are reminded of the concept of a “zero pair”. Arrays of counters could also be used to establish multiplication and division facts involving negative numbers, for example, if –12 ÷ 3 = –4 then –12 ÷ –4 = 3 If appropriate, students can also explore using the four operations with negative decimals or solving equations involving negative solutions and/or operations.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-calculator methods_6_Order of operations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of how to priortise operations in calculations, including those with directed number. Acronyms such as BIDMAS or BODMAS may be misleading as they imply that each operation has its own place in priority. Instead, highlight that some operations have equal priority, for example multiplication and division. Draw students’ attention to examples where operations should be calculated from left to right, for example, when calculating 20 – 5 + 3, 20 – 5 should be calculated first. Ensure students have opportunities to explore calculations involving all four operations, as well as powers and brackets throughout the step. Most calculators use order of operations correctly, but comparing and checking answers is useful.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-calculator methods_7_Related calculations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use known numerical facts to derive other related facts. Students will make links between similar calculations, for example, understanding that if 12 × 9 = 108 then 1.2 × 0.9 = 1.08. Attention should be drawn to how the numbers in a calculation have been manipulated and the effect this will have on a given answer. For example, if both numbers in a multiplication are multiplied by 10, then the new product will be 100 times greater than the original product. Students must also understand that this logic does not apply to division. For example, if both numbers in a division are multiplied by 10, then the new product will be the same as the original product. For division, this can be linked to students’ understanding of equivalent fractions, for example, 45 ÷ 0.5 = 45 0.5 = 90 1 = 90

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-calculator methods_8_Solve multi-step problems
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate the learning from this block so far, applying their knowledge to reasoning and problem- solving contexts. Students interpret questions and identify an appropriate approach to solve a variety of multi-step problems. Students may need to be reminded of key vocabulary used in different contexts, for example, “deposit” or “instalments” in financial contexts. It may also be appropriate to scaffold problems initially, so that students can gain confidence and construct a strategy or plan to solve the rest of the problem. Bar models, among other representations, can be used to help students visualise the problem and support their decisions around which calculations to perform. Consider the use of “goal-free” or “slow reveal” problems to build students’ confidence further. As a non-calculator step, this step offers students a valuable opportunity to reinforce and practise their arithmetic skills.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-linear graphs_1_Quadratic graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students plot and interpret quadratic graphs. This step builds on understanding from Key Stage 3, introducing equations with negative coefficients of x2 It may be helpful to provide additional practice in substituting values, especially negative numbers, into quadratic expressions. Ensure students have opportunities to practise substitution both with and without a calculator. When plotting, emphasise the characteristic “U” shape of a quadratic curve, explaining that this shape is called a parabola. Remind students that the points should be joined smoothly, not with straight-line segments, and highlight the importance of correctly shaping the curve around the turning point. Dynamic graphing software can be used to support understanding. Once students are confident in plotting, progress to estimating values directly from the graph.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-linear graphs_2_Intercepts and roots of quadratic graphs (E)
small_step_name: Intercepts and roots of quadratic graphs (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students identify and interpret the roots and intercepts of quadratic graphs, reinforcing connections to their previous work on solving quadratics. Begin by exploring examples where the roots and y-intercept can be identified through inspection before introducing algebraic methods. Emphasise that the roots of the equation y = ax2 + bx + c correspond to the solutions of ax2 + bx + c = 0 and should be written in the form x = d. Remind students that the y-intercept can be found by substituting x = 0 into the equation to identify the value of y before writing as a coordinate in the form (0, y). Provide opportunities for students to explore graphs that have 0, 1 or 2 roots, helping them recognise how the number of solutions relates to the graph’s intersection with the x-axis. Dynamic graphing software can be used to support understanding.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-linear graphs_3_Cubic graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students plot and interpret cubic graphs. This has previously been covered as an extend step in Key Stage 3, so it may be new content for some students. It may be helpful to provide additional practice in substituting values, especially negative numbers, into cubic expressions. When plotting, draw attention to the characteristic shape of a cubic curve. Emphasise to students that, as with quadratics, the points should be joined smoothly, not with straight-line segments, and highlight the importance of correctly shaping the curve around the turning points. Dynamic graphing software can be used to support understanding. Once students are confident in plotting, progress to estimating values directly from the graph.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Non-linear graphs_4_Approximate solutions to equations using graphs (E)
small_step_name: Approximate solutions to equations using graphs (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students plot additional lines on quadratic graphs to find solutions to equations, building on their understanding of finding roots of quadratics from graphs. This step reinforces the equivalence of roots and solutions of equations in the form ax2 + bx + c = 0. Build complexity slowly by initially asking students to add a given straight line of the form y = d to the graph of y = ax2 + bx + c to find solutions to ax2 + bx + c = d. For example, the solutions of the equation x2 + 5x + 6 = 1 can be found by identifying the x-coordinates of the points where the graphs y = x2 + 5x + 6 and y = 1 intersect.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Perimeter area and volume_10_Surface area of a prism
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding of nets and 2-D area to calculate the surface area of 3-D prisms. Begin by encouraging students to sketch the net of a given prism, identify each face and calculate the area of each one before finding the total surface area. Students should clearly label dimensions on their nets to support accurate calculations. After practising with cubes, cuboids and triangular prisms, extend learning to include prisms with different 2-D cross sections, such as parallelograms and trapeziums. Some examples may require the use of Pythagoras’ theorem to determine unknown side lengths. Challenge students to work backwards to calculate an unknown side length when the total surface area is given.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Perimeter area and volume_1_Name 2-D and 3-D shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the properties of 2-D and 3-D shapes to accurately identify and name them. Ensure that students develop fluency with key geometric vocabulary and notation, for example, using hatch marks to show equal lengths and arrows to indicate parallel lines. Emphasise how prefixes relate to shape properties, such as “-gon” meaning “angled” and “oct-” meaning “eight”, so an octagon has eight interior angles and therefore eight sides. When exploring 3-D shapes, provide physical models and discuss the 2-D shapes that form their surfaces. Remind students how to recognise prisms by highlighting their constant cross-sectional area along their length. Include examples of shapes with curved surfaces, such as cones, cylinders and spheres.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Perimeter area and volume_2_Perimeter of a 2-D shape
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit how to work out the perimeter of a 2-D shape. Students have previously covered perimeter of simple 2-D shapes and compound shapes at Key Stage 3. This step provides an opportunity to deepen understanding and use their knowledge of shape properties to solve problems in context and with more complex shapes. It may be useful to start with simple shapes that use geometric notation to depict properties to solidify this learning. For example, hatch marks for equal side lengths and arrows for parallel sides. Move on to compound shapes where students may need to find unknown side lengths before working out the perimeter. Students can be challenged to calculate the unknown lengths of sides when given the total perimeter of the shape.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Perimeter area and volume_3_Area of a 2-D shape
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate the area of 2-D shapes. They may need reminders of the formulae introduced at Key Stage 3. Begin with individual shapes such as rectangles, triangles, parallelograms and trapezia before progressing to mixed examples. Ensure students can confidently identify and apply the correct formula for each shape. As in the previous step, include geometric notation in diagrams and discuss its meaning, for example, hatch marks to indicate equal side lengths and arrows to show parallel sides. Encourage students to determine unknown side lengths when the area is provided. This step does not include the area or circumference of circles, or the area of compound shapes, as these topics will be explored in a later step. If appropriate, challenge students to find the areas of shapes that require unit conversions.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Perimeter area and volume_4_Area of a compound shape
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this step, students calculate the area of compound shapes by splitting into their component shapes. Encourage students to divide each compound shape into simpler shapes and find the area of each one separately before combining the results to determine the total area. This step focuses on rectilinear compound shapes, as well as those formed from trapezia, triangles and parallelograms. Ensure students are confident in selecting and applying the formula to calculate the area for each shape. Remind students to label side lengths clearly on their diagrams and to choose the most efficient way to split up the shape. Discuss when it may be more appropriate to use subtraction instead of addition, such as when a section of the shape is missing.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Perimeter area and volume_5_Recognise and label parts of a circle
small_step_name: Recognise and label parts of a circle
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore new vocabulary related to circles and their components. While they may already recognise key parts such as the radius and diameter, many of the terms introduced in this step may be unfamiliar. Visual aids can support understanding, so encourage students to label diagrams alongside written definitions and commit these terms to memory. Using a Frayer model can help students explore examples and non-examples of each definition, and short memory quizzes can reinforce recall. Emphasise the relationships between key parts, such as the radius always being half the diameter. A strong understanding of vocabulary will support students when calculating the area and circumference of circles later in the block.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Perimeter area and volume_6_Circumference of a circle
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use pi (π) to calculate the circumference of a circle. It may be useful to begin by asking students to measure both the circumference (C) and diameter (d) of various circles to investigate the relationship between the two, leading to the understanding that C d ≈ 3.14 This then leads to the discovery of the formula C = π × d. Students should practise applying this formula to find the circumference when given either the diameter or the radius. Students can also be challenged to work backwards, using a given circumference to determine the length of the diameter or radius. Encourage the use of the π button on calculators, rather than 3.14, to ensure greater accuracy. Although calculators may be used throughout this step, ensure students also practise expressing their answers in terms of π.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Perimeter area and volume_7_Area of a circle
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their prior understanding of calculating the area of a circle. Remind students of the formula A = πr2 and provide plenty of opportunities for fluency practice to ensure confidence in using it correctly. Emphasise that the radius is half the diameter and that all answers should include appropriate units such as cm2 or mm2 Although calculators can be used, students should also practise leaving their answers in terms of pi (π). Extend students’ learning by challenging them to calculate the area of semicircles and quarter circles.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Perimeter area and volume_8_Volume of a prism
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit calculating the volume of a prism, building on their prior learning from Key Stage 3. Begin with prisms that have cross sections of familiar 2-D shapes, such as rectangles, triangles and parallelograms, before progressing to more complex examples like trapezia and compound shapes. Ensure students apply the correct formula: volume of a prism = area of cross section × length. Highlight the difference with the cuboid formula (length × width × height). Challenge students to find unknown side lengths when the volume is given and remind them to include the correct units of measurement in all their answers.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Perimeter area and volume_9_Nets
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their prior knowledge from Key Stage 3 to explore the nets of 3-D shapes. They explore how 3-D shapes are formed and how each face corresponds to a familiar 2-D shape. Throughout this step, encourage students to use precise vocabulary such as face, edge and vertex. Begin with physical examples of nets for cubes and cuboids to show how the 2-D shapes fold to form a 3-D object. Use examples and non-examples to highlight that the faces must be arranged correctly for the net to fold together correctly. Matching activities can help students identify a 3-D shape from its net and vice versa. Challenge students to draw accurate nets of 3-D shapes when given specific dimensions.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Probability_10_Tree diagrams for dependent events (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This extend step builds on the previous small step by introducing probabilities for dependent events. Highlight to students that each scenario must be considered to calculate the probabilities on the second set of branches. A common example is “without replacement”, where modelling helps students see how one event affects another. For example, selecting a red counter from a bag and not replacing it changes both the total number of counters and the number of red counters for the next branch. Once the tree diagram is constructed, students should recognise that the calculations are carried out in the same way as in the previous step, using both the multiplication rule and the addition rule.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Probability_1_Probability of a single event
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit prior learning from Key Stage 3, focusing on calculating the probability of a single event. Emphasise that probability measures the likelihood of an outcome, and it is always expressed as a value between 0 and 1, where 0 represents an impossible event and 1 represents a certain event. Remind students that probabilities can be written as fractions, decimals or percentages. Students should also be familiar with probability notation, such as P(red), which represents the probability of selecting a red counter in a given context. Mutually exclusive events, such as rolling a 2 or a 6 on a dice should also be discussed.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Probability_2_Use the property that probabilities sum to 1
small_step_name: Use the property that probabilities sum to 1
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Building on the previous small step, students apply the idea that the sum of probabilities for all mutually exclusive events sums to 1 Begin with examples where the total is given before moving on to examples where the probability of the event occurring is given. Reinforce that if the probability of an event is known, the probability of it not occurring can be found by subtracting this value from 1 Students should practise applying this property when working with probabilities expressed as fractions, decimals or percentages and they should be confident in converting between the forms. Highlight to students that this relationship can be generalised by P(A) + P(not A) = 1 Students should use calculators to reduce cognitive load if required.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Probability_3_List outcomes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students work systematically to list all possible outcomes in a given situation. Start with simple examples, such as flipping a coin or rolling a dice, before progressing to more complex examples. Encourage students to explain how they know their list is complete and that no outcomes have been missed. Model systematic approaches, such as changing one element at a time to ensure accuracy. Encourage students to discuss whether the order of outcomes matters in the given context and remind them to check that their lists do not include repeated outcomes. At this stage, sample space diagrams are not required for organising outcomes, as they will be explored later in the block.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Probability_4_Relative frequency
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding from Key Stage 3, to consider relative frequency as a form of experimental probability. Students should recognise that probability is often considered in theoretical terms, where predictions are made about events that have not yet happened, while relative frequency is based on data from experiments or past events. Comparing theoretical probability with relative frequency can strengthen students’ understanding. A key discussion point is the effect of the number of trials on accuracy. Students should see that as the number of trials increases, the relative frequency tends to approach the theoretical probability. Students will also estimate the expected number of outcomes of an event using the relative frequency.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Probability_5_Sample spaces for 1 or more events
small_step_name: Sample spaces for 1 or more events
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students construct sample space diagrams for one or more events and use these sample spaces to calculate theoretical probabilities. This step revisits learning from Key Stage 3 and provides an opportunity to incorporate their skills from earlier in the block, using a systematic method when setting up a sample space diagram. Discuss with students the pros and cons of using a list or a grid, and how a grid can organise work. When calculating probabilities, explore the importance of the order of outcomes and whether the different options are equally likely.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Probability_6_Two-way tables
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This small step offers an opportunity to revisit and extend learning from Key Stage 3 to complete and interpret. Students should be able to design and construct two-way tables to represent data. They begin with partially completed tables before progressing to filling in or creating a full table from given information. When working with two-way tables, students also interpret probabilities from them. Particular attention should be paid to the wording of probability questions, so that students can recognise whether their probability is calculated from the whole group or from a specific subgroup. Reinforce accurate probability notation and remind students to express their answers as fractions, decimals or percentages.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Probability_7_Frequency trees
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit and extend learning from Key Stage 3 to use frequency trees to calculate probabilities. They should be able to construct a frequency tree from given data, though some time may be needed to consolidate this skill before moving on to calculating probabilities. When working with probabilities, it is useful to discuss the similarities and differences between frequency trees and sample space diagrams in how they represent and support problem- solving. Be clear about the distinction between finding the probability from the whole group versus from a given condition, such as “given that the person is in Year 10”.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Probability_8_Independent events
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit independent events, first introduced in Key Stage 3. Distinguishing between independent and dependent events can be a challenging concept, so it is important to spend time ensuring students can recognise the difference. Independent events are those where the occurrence of one event does not affect the probability of the other. Once confident in identifying independent events, students apply the multiplication rule, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B), to calculate the probability of two or more independent events occurring. This can then be extended by using the rule to test whether two events are independent.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Probability_9_Tree diagrams for independent events
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to tree diagrams for the first time, with a focus on independent events, building on the previous step. Explain that tree diagrams are another way of representing probability problems, alongside frequency trees and sample space diagrams, but that tree diagrams use probabilities on the branches rather than frequencies or totals. Students apply the multiplication rule, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) to calculate probabilities along the branches, and will also be introduced to the addition rule, P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B), to find the probability of one event or another occurring.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Rounding and estimation_1_Round to decimal places
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and build on Key Stage 3 learning, rounding to a specified number of decimal places. Begin by checking students are confident with rounding to the nearest integer, ten, hundred, thousand, etc. Number lines are useful in supporting students’ conceptual understanding of rounding. Remind students that we round to the greater value when the original number is halfway between two values, for example 4.75 rounds to 4.8, to 1 decimal place. Discuss that if a value rounds to an integer, it should still be written to the required number of decimal places, for example, 3.02 rounds to 3.0 to 1 decimal place.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Rounding and estimation_2_Round to significant figures
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their learning from Key Stage 3 and round numbers to a given number of significant figures. Remind students that the first significant figure is defined as the value of the first non-zero digit. It can be helpful for students to first identify the place value of the defined significant figure, and note that this place value varies between numbers. For example, in 435 and 0.0435 it is the hundreds and hundredths, respectively. Students should also be aware that zeros are classed as significant when they serve as a placeholder within a number. For example, in 5031, the second significant figure is 0

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Rounding and estimation_3_Estimate answers to calculations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of rounding to estimate answers to calculations. In most calculations, rounding to 1 significant figure is enough for an estimate, but students could explore examples needing more accuracy. By using calculations in context, students can determine what makes an estimate appropriate, and assess whether a calculation is reasonable. Highlight to students that they may see different vocabulary used, such as “approximate” or “estimate”, within estimation questions. Students should also consider whether their rounding will lead to an overestimate or an underestimate.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Rounding and estimation_4_Use of a calculator
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Building on the previous steps, students use a calculator to perform calculations and round their answers to the required degree of accuracy. Encourage students to write down all the figures on their calculator display before rounding their answers. Ensure students are confident inputting complex calculations into the calculator, drawing students’ attention to key features on their calculator such as roots, powers and fractions. Discuss that if their calculator displays the answer in exact form, they can use the S ⇔ D or FORMAT button (or equivalent) to convert it to a decimal.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Rounding and estimation_5_Error intervals (including truncation) (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend small step, students write error intervals for values that have been rounded or truncated to a specified degree of accuracy. This step provides a good opportunity to consolidate prior knowledge of inequality notation. Number lines can help students understand the range in which a true value lies. Begin by writing error intervals for values rounded to powers of 10 before exploring decimals and significant figures. Draw students’ attention to how the notation for error intervals, a ≤ x < b, includes values up to, but not including, the upper bound b. Once students are confident in writing error intervals for rounded values, introduce values that have been truncated. It is important to note that truncating is not a form of rounding.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Straight line graphs_1_Draw straight line graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of plotting straight line graphs from Key Stage 3. Remind students how to draw the lines y = x, y = –x and lines that are parallel to the axes. Draw attention to important features, for example, the x- and y-coordinates are equal for any point that lies on the line y = x. Model how to complete a table of values to generate pairs of coordinates before progressing to questions where a table of values is not given. If appropriate, students should have opportunities to explore forms other than y = mx + c, such as x + y = 10 Encourage students to discuss whether a point lies on a given line by substituting the coordinates into the equation.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Straight line graphs_2_Find solutions to equations using straight lines graphs
small_step_name: Find solutions to equations using straight lines graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use straight-line graphs to solve related equations. Remind students how to identify points that lie on pairs of lines, for example, how to verify the point (2, 6) lies on the lines y = 3x and y = 6. Build on this to show students how to solve an equation graphically. For example, solving 2x – 7 = 8 by drawing the graphs of y = 2x – 7 and y = 8 and identifying the x-coordinate of the point of intersection as the solution to the equation. As students’ confidence builds, provide more-complex equations to solve graphically, such as 2x + 3 = x + 1 Dynamic graphing software is recommended to support understanding.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Straight line graphs_3_Explore gradients
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the gradients of straight line graphs and learn that the value of m in the equation y = mx + c represents the gradient. For students previously following the support scheme, this may be their first formal introduction. Explain that gradient measures the steepness of a line, using the formula gradient = change in change in y x Start with lines on a grid, then progress to coordinate axes. Begin with positive gradients before introducing negative and fractional gradients. Encourage students to observe how changing the gradient affects steepness and highlight that lines with the same gradient are parallel. Dynamic graphing software can effectively support visual understanding.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Straight line graphs_4_y = mx + c
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on learning from previous steps and Key Stage 3 to interpret equations of a line given in the form y = mx + c, identifying both the gradient, m, and the coordinates of the y-intercept, (0, c). Students who have exclusively followed the support scheme in Key Stage 3 may not be familiar with the y-intercept. Students also build on their understanding that lines with the same gradient are parallel and use this knowledge to write equations of parallel lines. Once students can identify the gradient and y-intercept, introduce non-standard examples such as y = 3 + 5x or 2x + 1 = y. Students could also explore equations of lines that need to be rearranged into the form of y = mx + c, such as 2y = 4x + 6

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Straight line graphs_5_Find the equation of a line from a graph (E)
small_step_name: Find the equation of a line from a graph (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students identify gradients and y-intercepts from equations, before writing the equation of a straight line from a graph. Begin by giving students structured practice in identifying gradients and y-intercepts separately, before combining these to write equations of straight lines. Remind students that m represents the gradient and c represents the y-intercept within y = mx + c. Introduce non-standard forms such as y = 6 – 2x, and support students in rearranging these into the form y = mx + c. Use examples with different axis scales to develop fluency. Once confident, students should find the equation of a line from a graph by calculating the gradient from two points and identifying the y-intercept. Challenge students by working with fractional and negative values.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Straight line graphs_6_Find the midpoint of a line segment
small_step_name: Find the midpoint of a line segment
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of coordinates to find the midpoint of a line segment. Students may have previously explored this in Year 8 as an extend step. Begin by encouraging students to find the midpoint of two numbers on a number line, before transferring this skill to working in two dimensions. Students will learn that the mean of the x- and y-coordinates from a pair of points will give the x- and y-coordinates of the midpoint. Begin by modelling examples with line segments presented on a grid before students progress to working abstractly with two coordinates. If appropriate, students could work out the coordinates of the start or end point of a line segment given the midpoint. Challenge students to solve more complex problems involving midpoints and 2-D shapes.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Straight line graphs_7_Equation of a straight-line graph given one point and a gradient
small_step_name: Equation of a straight-line graph given one point and a gradient
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students combine their knowledge from previous steps to find the equation of a line given one point and a gradient. Begin with examples where the point given is the y-intercept, such as (0, 4). It may be helpful to spend time recapping with students how the x- and y-coordinates relate to an equation of a line. Once confidence is built, introduce questions where a point other than the y-intercept is given. Encourage the understanding that, by substituting the given values of m, x and y into the equation y = mx + c, then the value of c can be calculated. Some students may benefit from a reminder of methods for solving two-step equations before this step. If appropriate, students could find equations of parallel lines passing through a specific point.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Straight line graphs_8_Equation of a straight-line graph given two points (E)
small_step_name: Equation of a straight-line graph given two points (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of straight line graphs to find the equation of a line given two points. Begin by calculating the gradient of a line given the coordinates of two points it passes through. Once students are confident with this, make links to the previous step where students used the gradient and single point to calculate the y-intercept. Initially, use examples where the gradients and coordinates are positive, before introducing more difficult examples where the gradient is negative. Discuss that either of the given points can be chosen for the substitution stage of working. Sketching graphs can be a helpful scaffold for students who require further support.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Foundation_Straight line graphs_9_Real-life straight-line graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding of gradients and intercepts to real-life linear graphs. This step focuses on graphs that do not show direct proportion. Encourage students to interpret the intercept of the vertical axis, for example, in showing a fixed cost for a taxi fare. Discuss with students how the gradient represents the rate of increase or decrease per unit on the horizontal axis, such as cost per mile. Where appropriate, support students in linking contextual graphs to equations, allowing them to explore how changes to the gradient or intercept of the horizontal axis affect the relationship represented.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Interpret and represent data_10_Interpolation and extrapolation
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use scatter graphs to make predictions, with particular emphasis on understanding the difference between interpolation and extrapolation. Encourage discussion about when it is or isn’t appropriate to extrapolate beyond the given data range. Use examples where extrapolation leads to unrealistic or impractical results, such as predicting negative values for height or mass, to highlight the limitations of mathematical models. Make cross-curricular links with science, such as exploring the relationship between the length of a spring and the force applied. While this relationship may appear linear within a certain range, it no longer holds once the spring is overstretched. This helps students appreciate that extrapolation relies on assumptions and reinforces the importance of applying mathematical models carefully and critically.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Interpret and represent data_1_Averages and range
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit their Year 8 knowledge by calculating averages and the range from a small data set. Start by reviewing how to calculate the mean, median, mode and range. Draw students’ attention to the fact that the range measures the spread of the data and is not an average. Emphasise that each type of average represents the data differently and encourage students to choose the most appropriate average based on the context, such as selecting the mode for categorical data. Highlight the importance of considering outliers and their impact on the averages. Once students are confident in calculating averages and the range, introduce more complex problems, such as finding an unknown value in a data set when given the mean, median, mode or range, or combinations of these.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Interpret and represent data_2_Averages from an ungrouped frequency table
small_step_name: Averages from an ungrouped frequency table
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students practise calculating the mean, median and mode from ungrouped frequency tables. Students have already covered calculating the mean from a frequency table in Year 8, but this may be their first time calculating the mode and median. Begin by helping students interpret the table, identifying the values and their frequencies. For the mean, guide them to multiply each value by its frequency, total these products and divide by the overall number of data points. When finding the mode, prompt students to identify the most frequent value. For the median, guide students to explore the cumulative frequency and find the middle value either through a cumulative frequency column or an ordered list.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Interpret and represent data_3_Mean from a grouped frequency table
small_step_name: Mean from a grouped frequency table
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students estimate the mean from grouped frequency tables. This may have previously been covered in Year 8 as an extend step. Begin by reviewing how to calculate the mean from raw data to highlight the difference when working with grouped data. Emphasise that when data is grouped, individual values are unknown, so the mean can only be estimated. Explain that the midpoint of each interval is used as a representative value, assuming an even distribution within each group. Model the process step by step, showing how to identify midpoints, multiply them by their corresponding frequencies, sum these products, and divide by the total frequency to find an estimated mean.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Interpret and represent data_4_Averages from a grouped frequency table
small_step_name: Averages from a grouped frequency table
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students identify the median and modal class for grouped data, and estimate the mean. This could be combined with the previous step. Begin by helping students rewrite the data from the frequency table as a list to strengthen their understanding of the median. Highlight the link between finding the median in a list and locating it in the table before introducing the use of cumulative frequency. Model how to calculate the median position using n + 12, and guide students in creating a cumulative frequency column to identify the class interval containing the median. Emphasise that the median is an estimate representing a range of values rather than an exact value. For the modal class, prompt students to identify the class interval with the highest frequency, explaining that this represents the most common range of values, not a single mode.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Interpret and represent data_5_Use data to compare distributions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of comparing distributions using averages and range from Year 8. Students who have exclusively followed the support scheme may not have previously encountered comparing distributions. Remind students that the range is a measure of spread, showing how consistent or varied a set of data is, while averages describe a typical value within the data. Ask students to compare two sets of data by commenting on both the average and the range, encouraging them to describe what the values mean in context. Prompt students to notice that a smaller range suggests greater consistency, and that differences in averages can highlight which group performs better or worse overall.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Interpret and represent data_6_Types of data
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit their Year 8 learning on discrete, continuous, quantitative and qualitative data, and explore the distinction between primary and secondary data. Those following the support scheme may not have previously encountered these data types, so it’s important to model clear examples and discuss each one to build understanding. Once students are confident, they can collect primary data through a simple survey or experiment, for example, measuring classmates’ heights or gathering information about their hobbies. Ask students to find secondary data on a related topic, such as national statistics or published survey results. Encourage them to discuss the advantages and disadvantages of each data source: primary data is usually more relevant but takes longer to collect, while secondary data is quicker to obtain but may be less specific or reliable.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Interpret and represent data_7_Sampling
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of data collection by exploring sampling, a method used to gather information from part of a population when it isn’t practical to survey everyone. Begin by defining the population as the entire group being studied, such as all students in a school, and a sample as a smaller group representing it. Introduce random sampling, where each individual has an equal chance of being selected, and systematic sampling, where selections are made at regular intervals. Once students are confident, explore stratified sampling, where the population is divided into groups (strata) and a proportional sample is taken from each. Encourage students to apply proportional reasoning to calculate sample sizes and discuss which method is most reliable in different situations. Emphasise the importance of avoiding bias when selecting samples.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Interpret and represent data_8_Capture and recapture
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use proportional reasoning to estimate a population with the capture–recapture method. Using familiar examples, such as estimating the number of fish in a pond, helps make the idea relatable and emphasises why estimation is needed when counting every individual is impractical. Begin by discussing the key assumption that the proportion of marked individuals in the second sample reflects the proportion in the entire population. For instance, if 10 fish are marked initially and, in a second sample of 20 fish, 2 are marked, then 10 N = 2 20 . Have students solve to calculate N to see how the population estimate arises from proportional reasoning. Conclude by discussing when estimates may be unreliable, such as when marked fish don’t mix evenly, highlighting the importance of the method’s assumptions.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Interpret and represent data_9_Scatter graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students review scatter graphs, building on their prior knowledge from Key Stage 3. The focus is on reinforcing key vocabulary such as “positive correlation”, “negative correlation” and “no correlation”, and developing confidence in interpreting the relationship between two variables from the graph. Draw students’ attention to how the direction and strength of the pattern suggest different types of correlation. Students should also practise drawing a line of best fit and using it to make simple estimates. It is worth noting that, while lines of best fit in maths are straight lines, they may be curved in science depending on the nature of the data. It is valuable to remind students that correlation does not imply causality, and that the absence of a clear linear relationship does not necessarily mean the variables are unconnected.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-calculator methods_1_Order of operations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of how to priortise operations in calculations, including those with directed number. Acronyms such as BIDMAS or BODMAS may be misleading as they imply that each operation has its own place in priority. Instead, highlight that some operations have equal priority, for example multiplication and division. Draw students’ attention to examples where operations should be calculated from left to right, for example, when calculating 20 – 5 + 3, 20 – 5 should be calculated first. Ensure students have opportunities to explore calculations involving all four operations, as well as powers and brackets throughout the step. Most calculators use order of operations correctly, but comparing and checking answers is useful.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-calculator methods_2_Related calculations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use known numerical facts to derive other related facts. Students will make links between similar calculations, for example, understanding that if 12 × 9 = 108 then 1.2 × 0.9 = 1.08. Attention should be drawn to how the numbers in a calculation have been manipulated and the effect this will have on a given answer. For example, if both numbers in a multiplication are multiplied by 10, then the new product will be 100 times greater than the original product. Students must also understand that this logic does not apply to division. For example, if both numbers in a division are multiplied by 10, then the new product will be the same as the original product. For division, this can be linked to students’ understanding of equivalent fractions, for example, 45 ÷ 0.5 = 45 0.5 = 90 1 = 90

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-calculator methods_3_Solve multi-step problems
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate the learning from this block so far, applying their knowledge to reasoning and problem- solving contexts. Students interpret questions and identify an appropriate approach to solve a variety of multi-step problems. Students may need to be reminded of key vocabulary used in different contexts, for example, “deposit” or “instalments” in financial contexts. It may also be appropriate to scaffold problems initially, so that students can gain confidence and construct a strategy or plan to solve the rest of the problem. Bar models, among other representations, can be used to help students visualise the problem and support their decisions around which calculations to perform. Consider the use of “goal-free” or “slow reveal” problems to build students’ confidence further. As a non-calculator step, this step offers students a valuable opportunity to reinforce and practise their arithmetic skills.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-calculator methods_4_Convert recurring decimals to fractions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students convert recurring decimals to fractions. In this step, students will only work with decimals with a single repeating digit. Build understanding by scaling known equivalences, for example, dividing 0.3 = 1 3 by 3 to show that 0.1 = 1 9 , and hence generalise that 0.a = a 9 where a is an integer between 1 and 9. Dividing 0.1 by 10 can show how 0.01 = 1 90 Algebraic approaches can also be used. Introduce attributing a decimal to an unknown and multiplying this by powers of 10 until two numbers with the same sequence of digits after the decimal point can be subtracted to give an integer. Students should be able to identify fractions that convert to a recurring decimal as well as use short division to complete conversions.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-calculator methods_5_Convert more complex recurring decimals to fractions (E)
small_step_name: Convert more complex recurring decimals to fractions (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students convert decimals with two or more repeating digits into fractions. Highlight that the first digit with a recurring dot marks the start of a repeating sequence, which extends to the next digit with a recurring dot and then repeats indefinitely. Initially, it may be useful to model finding consecutive powers of ten of a decimal, for example, x, 10x, 100x, 1000x and so on, highlighting when two numbers have the same sequence of digits after the decimal point and how these could be subtracted to then form an equation then a fraction. As students build confidence, they may develop more efficient ways to solve problems through pattern spotting. This can link to the non- algebraic approaches explored in the previous step.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-linear graphs_1_Quadratic graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students plot and interpret quadratic graphs. This step builds on understanding from Key Stage 3, introducing equations with negative coefficients of x2 It may be helpful to provide additional practice in substituting values, especially negative numbers, into quadratic expressions. Ensure students have opportunities to practise substitution both with and without a calculator. When plotting, emphasise the characteristic “U” shape of a quadratic curve, explaining that this shape is called a parabola. Remind students that the points should be joined smoothly, not with straight-line segments, and highlight the importance of correctly shaping the curve around the turning point. Dynamic graphing software can be used to support understanding. Once students are confident in plotting, progress to estimating values directly from the graph.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-linear graphs_2_Intercepts and roots of quadratic graphs
small_step_name: Intercepts and roots of quadratic graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students identify and interpret the roots and intercepts of quadratic graphs, reinforcing connections to their previous work on solving quadratics. Begin by exploring examples where the roots and y-intercept can be identified through inspection before introducing algebraic methods. Emphasise that the roots of the equation y = ax2 + bx + c correspond to the solutions of ax2 + bx + c = 0 and should be written in the form x = d. Remind students that the y-intercept can be found by substituting x = 0 into the equation to identify the value of y before writing as a coordinate in the form (0, y). Provide opportunities for students to explore graphs that have 0, 1 or 2 roots, helping them recognise how the number of solutions relates to the graph’s intersection with the x-axis. Dynamic graphing software can be used to support understanding.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-linear graphs_3_Turning points
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of quadratic graphs and completing the square to explore turning points. Students can begin by identifying the coordinates of a turning point by inspection. Highlight that equations with a positive coefficient of x2 have a minimum turning point and that those with a negative coefficient of x2 have a maximum turning point. Draw attention to the link between the completed square form of the quadratic, y = (x + c)2 + d, and the coordinates of the turning point (–c, d). Once confident, students can complete the square to find turning points of graphs whose equations have negative x2 coefficients and those with x2 coefficients that are greater than 1. Dynamic graphing software can be used to support student understanding.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-linear graphs_4_Cubic graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students plot and interpret cubic graphs. This has previously been covered as an extend step in Key Stage 3, so it may be new content for some students. It may be helpful to provide additional practice in substituting values, especially negative numbers, into cubic expressions. When plotting, draw attention to the characteristic shape of a cubic curve. Emphasise to students that, as with quadratics, the points should be joined smoothly, not with straight-line segments, and highlight the importance of correctly shaping the curve around the turning points. Dynamic graphing software can be used to support understanding. Once students are confident in plotting, progress to estimating values directly from the graph.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-linear graphs_5_Approximate solutions to equations using graphs
small_step_name: Approximate solutions to equations using graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students plot additional lines on graphs to find solutions to equations, building on their understanding of finding roots of quadratics from graphs. This step reinforces the equivalence of roots and solutions of equations in the form ax2 + bx + c = 0 Start by asking them to draw a given horizontal line in the form y = d alongside a graph of the form y = ax2 + bx + c to solve ax2 + bx + c = d. For example, the solutions of the equation x2 + 5x + 6 = 1 can be found by identifying the x-coordinates of the points where the graphs y = x2 + 5x + 6 and y = 1 intersect. Once students are confident, introduce further examples requiring lines of the form y = ax + b, supporting students to determine and plot the correct additional line. For example, adding a line of y = 2x + 4 to the graph of y = 2x2 + 3x + 1 to solve 2x2 + x – 3 = 0

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-linear graphs_6_Estimate the gradient of a curve at a given point
small_step_name: Estimate the gradient of a curve at a given point
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn to draw tangents to curves and calculate an estimate of the gradient at a given point. They are introduced to the concept of a tangent as a straight line that touches a curve at a single point, without crossing it. Dynamic graphing software can help illustrate how a tangent at a point represents the gradient at that point. Model the process of drawing a tangent, guiding students to position their line as close as possible to the point, while keeping it equidistant from the curve on both sides. Once the tangent is drawn, students can calculate its gradient. Remind students that the gradient of the curve is an estimate unless the tangent at the given point is provided. Allow students to explore tangents to both quadratic and cubic graphs within this step.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-linear graphs_7_Estimate the area under a curve (E)
small_step_name: Estimate the area under a curve (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use trapezia to estimate the area under a curve. Begin by reminding students of the formula for the area of a trapezium. Remind students that the area under a velocity–time graph (or speed–time graph) represents the distance travelled. Emphasise the importance of dividing the x-axis into equal intervals to create triangular and trapezium-shaped “strips”. Ensure students are confident working with examples with a variety of scales on the axis. Discuss for each example whether the total area calculated is an overestimate or an underestimate, and how that affects the accuracy of the distance travelled.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-linear graphs_8_Equation of a circle
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to the equation of a circle, focusing on circles centred at the origin. Emphasise that the radius represents the distance from the origin (0, 0) to any point on the circumference of the circle. Demonstrate to students how a right-angled triangle can be formed by drawing a line from the centre of the circle, the origin, to a given point (x, y) on the circumference. Students can use the coordinates to determine the side lengths of the triangle before applying Pythagoras’ Theorem to calculate the length of the hypotenuse, which represents the radius r. This establishes how the equation of a circle can be written as x2 + y2 = r2 Students should be able to calculate the length of the radius from a diagram as well as from a given equation, such as recognising that a circle with an equation x2 + y2 = 144 has a radius of 12.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Non-linear graphs_9_Equation of a tangent to a circle (E)
small_step_name: Equation of a tangent to a circle (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students determine the equation of a tangent to a circle. Students can apply their prior understanding of finding the equations of perpendicular lines from earlier in the year. Remind students that the angle between the radius and the tangent at the same point is 90°. This concept will be explored further when studying circle theorems later in Year 11. Encourage students to calculate the gradient of the radius first, then use this to determine the gradient of the tangent. Remind students that, because the lines are perpendicular, the gradient of the tangent is the negative reciprocal of the gradient of the radius. Model how the gradient and the coordinates of the given point form the equation of the tangent in the form y = mx + c

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Perimeter area and volume_10_Surface area of a cylinder
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding of nets and circles to calculate the surface area of cylinders. Begin by exploring the net of a cylinder using practical examples, such as unwrapping the label from a tin can, to demonstrate that the length of the curved rectangular face corresponds to the circumference of the circular base. Challenge students to calculate unknown dimensions of a cylinder when its total surface area is given. While calculators may be used, ensure students can also express their answers in terms of pi (π). Remind students to include the correct units of measurement in all their answers.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Perimeter area and volume_1_Perimeter of a 2-D shape
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit how to work out the perimeter of a 2-D shape. Students have previously covered perimeter of simple 2-D shapes and compound shapes at Key Stage 3. This step provides an opportunity to deepen understanding and use their knowledge of shape properties to solve problems in context and with more complex shapes. It may be useful to start with simple shapes that use geometric notation to depict properties to solidify this learning. For example, hatch marks for equal side lengths and arrows for parallel sides. Move on to compound shapes where students may need to find unknown side lengths before working out the perimeter.
