Students can be challenged to calculate the unknown lengths of sides when given the total perimeter of the shape.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Perimeter area and volume_2_Area of a 2-D shape
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate the area of 2-D shapes. They may need reminders of the formulae introduced at Key Stage 3. Begin with individual shapes such as rectangles, triangles, parallelograms and trapezia before progressing to mixed examples. Ensure students can confidently identify and apply the correct formula for each shape. As in the previous step, include geometric notation in diagrams and discuss its meaning, for example, hatch marks to indicate equal side lengths and arrows to show parallel sides. Encourage students to determine unknown side lengths when the area is provided. This step also includes calculating the area of compound shapes. If appropriate, challenge students to find the areas of shapes that require unit conversions.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Perimeter area and volume_3_Area and circumference of a circle
small_step_name: Area and circumference of a circle
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students find the area and circumference of a circle. Students may need reminding of the formulae Area = π × r2 and Circumference = π × d before embedding their learning through fluency practice. Emphasise that the radius is half the diameter and that all answers should include appropriate units such as cm2 or mm2 Students can also be challenged to work backwards, using a given circumference or area of the circle to determine the length of the diameter or radius. Encourage the use of the π button on calculators, rather than 3.14, to ensure greater accuracy. Although calculators may be used throughout this step, ensure students also practise expressing their answers in terms of π.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Perimeter area and volume_4_Arc length and perimeter
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students extend their understanding of circle circumference to calculate arc lengths and the perimeters of sectors. Emphasise that an arc represents the same fraction of the circumference as the sector’s angle represents of a full turn. Begin with simple fractional angles, such as halves, quarters and sixths, before introducing θ 360 × πd, where θ represents the angle subtended by the arc length. Ensure students explore examples with angles both greater than and less than 180°. When finding the perimeter of a sector, remind students to add the lengths of both radii to the arc length. Although calculators can be used, students should also practise expressing their answers in terms of pi (π).

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Perimeter area and volume_5_Area of a sector
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students extend their understanding of calculating the area of circles to calculate the area of sectors. Emphasise that a sector represents the same fraction of the area of the whole circle as the sector’s angle represents of a full turn. Begin with simple fractional angles, such as halves, quarters and sixths, before introducing θ 360 × πr2, where θ represents the angle subtended by the arc length. Ensure students explore examples with angles both greater than and less than 180°. Challenge students to find the area of compound shapes including parts of circles. Although calculators can be used, students should also practise expressing their answers in terms of pi (π).

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Perimeter area and volume_6_Volume of a prism
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit calculating the volume of a prism, building on their prior learning from Key Stage 3. Begin with prisms that have cross sections of familiar 2-D shapes, such as rectangles, triangles and parallelograms, before progressing to more complex examples like trapezia and compound shapes. Ensure students apply the correct formula: volume of a prism = area of cross section × length. Highlight the difference with the cuboid formula (length × width × height). Challenge students to find unknown side lengths when the volume is given and remind them to include the correct units of measurement in all their answers. Students can be challenged further by including examples where unknown dimensions need to be found through other means, such as Pythagoras’ theorem.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Perimeter area and volume_7_Volume of a cylinder
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of prism volume and circle area to calculate the volume of a cylinder. Emphasise that, although a cylinder is not a prism, the same formula applies: volume = area of cross-section × length. Students can deepen their understanding by using the volume to find unknown dimensions, such as the height or the radius of the circular base. While calculators may be used, ensure students can also express their answers in terms of pi (π). Extend learning by challenging students to find the volume of a cylinder in a variety of real-life problem-solving contexts.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Perimeter area and volume_8_Nets
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their prior knowledge from Key Stage 3 to explore the nets of 3-D shapes. They explore how 3-D shapes are formed and how each face corresponds to a familiar 2-D shape. Throughout this step, encourage students to use precise vocabulary such as face, edge and vertex. Begin with physical examples of nets for cubes and cuboids to show how the 2-D shapes fold to form a 3-D object. Use examples and non-examples to highlight that the faces must be arranged correctly for the net to fold together correctly. Matching activities can help students identify a 3-D shape from its net and vice versa. Challenge students to draw accurate nets of 3-D shapes when given specific dimensions.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Perimeter area and volume_9_Surface area of a prism
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding of nets and 2-D area to calculate the surface area of 3-D prisms. Begin by encouraging students to sketch the net of a given prism, identify each face and calculate the area of each one before finding the total surface area. Students should clearly label dimensions on their nets to support accurate calculations. After practising with cubes, cuboids and triangular prisms, extend learning to include prisms with different 2-D cross sections, such as parallelograms and trapeziums. Some examples may require the use of Pythagoras’ theorem to determine unknown side lengths. Challenge students to work backwards to calculate an unknown side length when the total surface area is given.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Probability_1_Probability of a single event
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit prior learning from Key Stage 3, focusing on calculating the probability of a single event. Emphasise that probability measures the likelihood of an outcome, and it is always expressed as a value between 0 and 1, where 0 represents an impossible event and 1 represents a certain event. Remind students that probabilities can be written as fractions, decimals or percentages. Students should also be familiar with probability notation, such as P(red), which represents the probability of selecting a red counter in a given context. Mutually exclusive events, such as rolling a 2 or a 6 on a dice should also be discussed.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Probability_2_Use the property that probabilities sum to 1
small_step_name: Use the property that probabilities sum to 1
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Building on the previous small step, students apply the idea that the sum of probabilities for all mutually exclusive events sums to 1 Begin with examples where the total is given before moving on to examples where the probability of the event occurring is given. Reinforce that if the probability of an event is known, the probability of it not occurring can be found by subtracting this value from 1 Students should practise applying this property when working with probabilities expressed as fractions, decimals or percentages and they should be confident in converting between the forms. Highlight to students that this relationship can be generalised by P(A) + P(not A) = 1 Students should use calculators to reduce cognitive load if required.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Probability_3_List and count outcomes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students work systematically to list all possible outcomes in a given situation. Start with simple examples, such as flipping a coin or rolling a dice, before progressing to more complex examples. Encourage students to explain how they know their list is complete and that no outcomes have been missed. Modelling a systematic approach can help students understand that the total number of arrangements equals the product of the number of choices for each part. Students also need to distinguish between listing outcomes with and without replacement, and understand contexts where repeats are not allowed, such as finding the total number of handshakes in a group if each pair shakes hands once.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Probability_4_Relative frequency
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding from Key Stage 3, to consider relative frequency as a form of experimental probability. Students should recognise that probability is often considered in theoretical terms, where predictions are made about events that have not yet happened, while relative frequency is based on data from experiments or past events. Comparing theoretical probability with relative frequency can strengthen students’ understanding. A key discussion point is the effect of the number of trials on accuracy. Students should see that as the number of trials increases, the relative frequency tends to approach the theoretical probability. Students will also estimate the expected number of outcomes of an event using the relative frequency.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Probability_5_Sample spaces for 1 or more events
small_step_name: Sample spaces for 1 or more events
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students construct sample space diagrams for one or more events and use these sample spaces to calculate theoretical probabilities. This step revisits learning from Key Stage 3 and provides an opportunity to incorporate their skills from earlier in the block, using a systematic method when setting up a sample space diagram. Discuss with students the pros and cons of using a list or a grid, and how a grid can organise work. When calculating probabilities, explore the importance of the order of outcomes and whether the different options are equally likely.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Probability_6_Two-way tables and frequency trees
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit and extend learning from Key Stage 3 to complete and interpret two-way tables and frequency trees. They should be able to construct and complete two- way tables from given information and use them to calculate probabilities, recognising whether the denominator comes from the whole group or from a subgroup. Students will also consolidate their ability to build frequency trees, using them to model multi-stage probability situations. It is important to highlight similarities and differences between frequency trees and sample space diagrams to support understanding of how different representations can aid problem solving.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Probability_7_Independent events
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit independent events, first introduced in Key Stage 3. Distinguishing between independent and dependent events can be a challenging concept, so it is important to spend time ensuring students can recognise the difference. Independent events are those where the occurrence of one event does not affect the probability of the other. Once confident in identifying independent events, students apply the multiplication rule, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B), to calculate the probability of two or more independent events occurring. This can then be extended by using the rule to test whether two events are independent.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Probability_8_Tree diagrams for independent events
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to tree diagrams for the first time, with a focus on independent events, building on the previous step. Explain that tree diagrams are another way of representing probability problems, alongside frequency trees and sample space diagrams, but that tree diagrams use probabilities on the branches rather than frequencies or totals. Students apply the multiplication rule, P(A and B) = P(A) × P(B) to calculate probabilities along the branches, and will also be introduced to the addition rule, P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B), to find the probability of one event or another occurring.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Probability_9_Tree diagrams for dependent events
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This small step builds on the previous small step by introducing probabilities for dependent events. Highlight to students that each scenario must be considered to calculate the probabilities on the second set of branches. A common example is “without replacement”, where modelling helps students see how one event affects another. For example, selecting a red counter from a bag and not replacing it changes both the total number of counters and the number of red counters for the next branch. Once the tree diagram is constructed, students should recognise that the calculations are carried out in the same way as in the previous step, using both the multiplication rule and the addition rule.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Rounding and estimation_1_Round to decimal places and significant figures
small_step_name: Round to decimal places and significant figures
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and build on their prior learning from Key Stage 3, rounding to a specified number of decimal places or significant figures. Remind students of the convention of rounding to the greater value when the original number is halfway between two values, for example 4.75 rounds to 4.8 to 1 decimal place. Rounded values should still be written to the required decimal place, for example 3.02 rounds to 3.0 (not 3) to 1 decimal place. Reinforce that the first significant figure is the first non-zero digit, but that zeros are classed as significant when they serve as a placeholder within a number. For example, in 5031, the second significant figure is 0

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Rounding and estimation_2_Estimate answers to calculations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of rounding to estimate answers to calculations. In most calculations, rounding to 1 significant figure is enough for an estimate, but students could explore examples needing more accuracy. By using calculations in context, students can determine what makes an estimate appropriate, and assess whether a calculation is reasonable. Highlight to students that they may see different vocabulary used, such as “approximate” or “estimate”, within estimation questions. Students should also consider whether their rounding will lead to an overestimate or an underestimate.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Rounding and estimation_3_Use of a calculator
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Building on the previous steps, in this small step students use a calculator to perform calculations and round their answers to the required degree of accuracy. Encourage students to write down all the figures on their calculator display before rounding their answers. Ensure students are confident inputting complex calculations into the calculator, drawing students’ attention to key features on their calculator such as roots, powers and fractions. Discuss that if their calculator displays the answer in exact form, they can use the S ⇔ D or FORMAT button (or equivalent) to convert it to a decimal.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Rounding and estimation_4_Error intervals (including truncation)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students write error intervals for values that have been rounded or truncated to a specified degree of accuracy. This step provides a good opportunity to consolidate prior knowledge of inequality notation. Number lines can help students understand the range in which a true value lies. Begin by writing error intervals for values rounded to powers of 10 before exploring decimals and significant figures. Draw students’ attention to how the notation for error intervals, a ≤ x < b, includes values up to, but not including, the upper bound, b. Once students are confident in writing error intervals for rounded values, introduce values that have been truncated. It is important to note that truncating is not a form of rounding.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Rounding and estimation_5_Upper and lower bounds
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on learning from the previous step and use limits of accuracy to find upper and lower bounds of calculations. Encourage students to write down the upper and lower bounds for each value before performing any calculations. Start with straightforward operations like addition or multiplication, where concepts like (a + b)max = amax + bmax are intuitive. Subtraction and division may need more careful exploration to establish, for example (a – b)max = amax – bmin Learning these formulae is not necessary here. Instead, prompt students to consider how they can make the value greater or smaller.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Straight line graphs_10_Real-life straight-line graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding of gradients and intercepts to real-life linear graphs. This step focuses on graphs that do not show direct proportion. Encourage students to interpret the intercept of the vertical axis, for example, in showing a fixed cost for a taxi fare. Discuss with students how the gradient represents the rate of increase or decrease per unit on the horizontal axis, such as cost per mile. Where appropriate, support students in linking contextual graphs to equations, allowing them to explore how changes to the gradient or intercept of the horizontal axis affect the relationship represented.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Straight line graphs_1_Draw straight line graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of plotting straight line graphs from Key Stage 3. Remind students how to draw the lines y = x, y = –x and lines that are parallel to the axes. Draw attention to important features, for example, the x- and y- coordinates are equal for any point which lies on the line y = x. Model how to complete a table of values to generate pairs of coordinates before progressing to questions where a table of values is not given. If appropriate, students should have opportunities to explore forms other than y = mx + c, such as x + y = 10 Encourage students to discuss whether a point lies on a given line by substituting the coordinates into the equation. This step also covers estimating solutions to equations by reading values off the graph and drawing two straight lines to solve an equation, for example, solving 2x = 5x – 7 by drawing y = 2x and y = 5x – 7

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Straight line graphs_2_y = mx + c
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on learning from previous steps and Key Stage 3 to interpret equations of a line given in the form y = mx + c, identifying both the gradient, m, and the coordinates of the y-intercept, (0, c). Students also build on their understanding that lines with the same gradient are parallel and use this knowledge to write equations of parallel lines. Once students can identify the gradient and y-intercept, introduce non-standard examples such as y = 3 + 5x or 2x + 1 = y. Students could also explore equations of lines that need to be rearranged into the form of y = mx + c, such as 2y = 4x + 6

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Straight line graphs_3_Find the equation of a line from a graph
small_step_name: Find the equation of a line from a graph
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students identify gradients and y-intercepts from equations, before writing the equation of a straight line from a graph. Begin by giving students structured practice in identifying gradients and y-intercepts separately, before combining these to write equations of straight lines. Remind students that m represents the gradient and c represents the y-intercept within y = mx + c. Introduce non-standard forms such as y = 6 – 2x, and support students in rearranging these into the form y = mx + c. Use examples with different axis scales to develop fluency. Once confident, students should find the equation of a line from a graph by calculating the gradient from two points and identifying the y-intercept. Challenge students by working with fractional and negative values.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Straight line graphs_4_Represent solutions to single inequalities in a graph
small_step_name: Represent solutions to single inequalities in a graph
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of straight line graphs to represent and interpret inequalities graphically. Some students may have encountered graphical inequalities as an extend step in Key Stage 3. Start with simple inequalities such as x > 1 or y ≤ 3, before progressing to more complex examples such as x + 5 > 7 Emphasise the correct use of solid lines and dashed lines when graphing the inequalities. When appropriate, introduce linear inequalities in two variables, such as y > 3x + 1, and support students in shading the correct region. Note that regions defined by multiple inequalities will be explored in the following step.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Straight line graphs_5_Represent solutions to multiple inequalities on a graph (E)
small_step_name: Represent solutions to multiple inequalities on a graph (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding from the previous step to identify and shade regions defined by multiple inequalities. Begin with simple cases, such as lines parallel to the axes and the lines y = x and y = –x, before progressing to inequalities in different forms such as y ≤ ax + b or x + y < a. Remind students of the correct use of solid lines or dashed lines when graphing the inequalities. Encourage students to test points by substituting x- and y-coordinates to identify regions to be shaded. Dynamic graphing software is a useful visual aid to explore this concept and develop students’ understanding.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Straight line graphs_6_Find the midpoint of a line segment
small_step_name: Find the midpoint of a line segment
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of coordinates to find the midpoint of a line segment. Students may have previously explored this in Year 8 as an extend step. Begin by encouraging students to find the midpoint of two numbers on a number line, before transferring this skill to working in two dimensions. Students will learn that the mean of the x- and y-coordinates from a pair of points will give the x- and y-coordinates of the midpoint. Begin by modelling examples with line segments presented on a grid before students progress to working abstractly with two coordinates. If appropriate, students could work out the coordinates of the start or end point of a line segment given the midpoint. Challenge students to solve more complex problems involving midpoints and 2-D shapes.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Straight line graphs_7_Equation of a straight-line graph given one point and a gradient
small_step_name: Equation of a straight-line graph given one point and a gradient
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students combine their knowledge from previous steps to find the equation of a line given one point and a gradient. Begin with examples where the point given is the y-intercept, such as (0, 4). It may be helpful to spend time recapping with students how the x- and y-coordinates relate to an equation of a line. Once confidence is built, introduce questions where a point other than the y-intercept is given. Encourage the understanding that, by substituting the given values of m, x and y into the equation y = mx + c, then the value of c can be calculated. Some students may benefit from a reminder of methods for solving two-step equations before this step. If appropriate, students could find equations of parallel lines passing through a specific point.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Straight line graphs_8_Equation of a straight-line graph given two points
small_step_name: Equation of a straight-line graph given two points
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of straight line graphs to find the equation of a line given two points. Begin by calculating the gradient of a line given the coordinates of two points it passes through. Once students are confident with this, make links to the previous step where students used the gradient and single point to calculate the y-intercept. Initially, use examples where the gradients and coordinates are positive, before introducing more difficult examples where the gradient is negative. Discuss that either of the given points can be chosen for the substitution stage of working. Sketching graphs can be a helpful scaffold for students who require further support.

small_step_id: Year 10_14-15_Spring_Higher_Straight line graphs_9_Equations of perpendicular lines (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to perpendicular lines on coordinate grids, building on their prior understanding of gradients and linear equations. Allow students time to explore the relationship between the gradients of perpendicular lines, highlighting that they are negative reciprocals of each other. Once students can confidently identify negative reciprocals, model how to apply this understanding to find the equation of a perpendicular line. Start with a given equation, using knowledge of the form y = mx + c to identify the gradient. Then progress to more complex concepts, such as finding the equation of a line through two given points, followed by determining the equation of a line perpendicular to it.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_10_Convert metric units of area (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students convert metric units of area. This may have been previously explored in the extend step in Year 9. Begin by exploring a square with a side length of 1 cm and comparing it to a square with a side length of 10 mm, highlighting that both lengths are equivalent. This leads to the key insight that 1 cm² is equal to 100 mm2 A similar approach can be used to compare other metric units, such as converting between m2 and cm2. Encourage students to recognise that the area scale factor is the square of the linear scale factor. Once students are confident, they can progress to converting between units in a single calculation for example, converting 3 cm2 to mm2

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_11_Convert metric units of volume (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students convert metric units of volume. This may have been previously explored as an extend step in Year 9. Similar to the previous step, students start by comparing the volume of a cube with a side length of 1 cm and another cube with a side length of 10 mm. This supports students in understanding that 1 cm3 is equal to 1000 mm3 Links between other measurements of volume, such as m3 and cm3, should also be explored. Encourage students to recognise that the volume scale factor is the cube of the linear scale factor. Students can then progress to converting between units in a single calculation.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_1_Isometric drawings
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students draw and interpret 3-D shapes on isometric paper. Students may be familiar with isometric paper when exploring volumes in cuboids in Key Stage 2. Ensure students know that adjacent dots on isometric paper are equidistant. Start by having students draw cubes and cuboids isometrically made from centimetre cubes, before drawing cubes and cuboids with labelled integer dimensions. Encourage students to start drawing the shape from its base towards the bottom of the isometric paper, so that they do not run out of room. When ready, have students practise drawing and interpreting triangular prisms isometrically. Having 3-D shapes available will help students to understand how the shape is represented using the isometric paper. Volume of cubes, cuboids and triangular prisms can be interleaved in this step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_2_Plans and elevations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will draw accurate and scaled plans as well as front and side elevations of 3-D shapes. They should begin by drawing plans and elevations of cubes and cuboids before moving onto more complex shapes such as prisms. Allowing students access to physical objects such as interlocking cubes to make shapes or dynamic geometry software may be useful to help students visualise objects before attempting to draw plans and elevations. If appropriate, have students draw or describe shapes when given the plan and elevation views. This includes being able to draw them on isometric paper, which was covered in the previous step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_3_Volume of a cylinder
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will build on prior learning from Year 10, combining their understanding of the area of a circle and the volume of prisms. Highlight that, although cylinders are not prisms, the volume of a cylinder is calculated in a similar way by multiplying the area of the constant cross-section by the length or height. Allow students the opportunity to practise giving answers exactly in terms of pi (π) and to various degrees of accuracy, for example, rounded to 1 decimal place. If appropriate, students could solve problems involving the volume of a cylinder, for example calculating an unknown length within the cylinder.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_4_Volume of a sphere
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use the formula to calculate the volume of a sphere. Tell students that the formula to work out the volume of a sphere with radius r is 4 3 πr3, and model how to input relevant calculations into a calculator, for example 4 3 × π × 53, to work out the volume of a sphere with a radius of 5 cm. As with other formulae involving pi (π), students should have the opportunity to provide exact answers in terms of pi (π), as well as giving approximate answers to varying degrees of accuracy. When students are comfortable with finding the volume of a sphere, they could begin to investigate the volume of hemispheres and compound shapes.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_5_Volume of cones and pyramids
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate the volume of cones and pyramids. Students may not have covered this before, as this is an extend step in Year 9. When introducing the volume of a cone, it may be useful to draw out similarities with the formula for the volume of a cylinder. That is, the volume of a cone is 1 3 of the volume of a cylinder with the same height and radius. Then further similarity may be shown between the formulae for cones and pyramids. For example, the volume of both can be calculated with 1 3 × area of base × height. If appropriate, students could form and solve equations involving volumes, such as calculating the length of a radius when given the height and volume of a cone.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_6_Surface area of a sphere
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will calculate the surface area of a sphere using the formula. Remind students that the surface area is the area that covers the outside of a 3-D shape, and introduce them to the formula Area = 4πr2. Although this formula is provided, it may be beneficial to investigate and compare the structure of area and volume formulae using dimensional analysis. For example, area is measured in square units and 4πr2 involves a length (a radius) being squared. If appropriate, students could also calculate the surface area of a hemisphere.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_7_Surface area of a cylinder (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will learn how to calculate the surface area of a cylinder, which they may have studied previously as an extension step in Year 9. Exploring the net of a cylinder allows students to see that the length of the curved surface area is equal to the circumference of the circular face. This then supports students to understand the calculations needed to work out the total surface area. As with previous steps, allow students the opportunity to practise giving answers exactly in terms of pi (π), and to various degrees of accuracy, such as rounded to 1 decimal place.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_8_Surface area of a pyramid (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will learn how to calculate the surface area of a pyramid. Note that students may have calculated the surface area of prisms in Year 10. Students will only need to consider pyramids where the lateral faces are congruent triangles and the apex of the pyramid is directly above the centre of the base. Nets can be a useful representation to highlight the calculations necessary to work out the surface area of a pyramid. Students need to pay particular attention to given dimensions, for example, ensuring they use the perpendicular height of a triangular face when calculating its area.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Area and volume_9_Surface area of a cone (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will use a given formula to work out the area of the curved surface of a cone, as well as finding the total surface area. This will normally be given as A = πrl where A is the area of the curved surface, r is the radius of the cone and l is the slant height of the cone. Ensure students understand that the net of a cone consists of a circular base as well as another curved surface, and therefore the total surface area will be the sum of these areas. If appropriate, students could solve other problems involving cones, such as calculating the length of a radius when given the surface area and slant height of a cone.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Ratio and proportion_1_Solve problems with ratio
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will revisit problems involving ratio covered in Year 10. Provide opportunities for students to share amounts in a ratio when given either a total, a part, or a difference. Bar models can help students to identify key differences between problems and to establish a suitable strategy to solve them, rather than relying on a procedural method, such as dividing an amount by the sum of the parts in a given ratio. Students should also explore links between fractions and ratios. Note that “combine a set of ratios” and “link ratio and algebra” are both Extend steps in Year 10 and may not have been covered previously. Time could be given to each of these steps if appropriate.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Ratio and proportion_2_Direct proportion
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will solve problems involving direct proportion, preparing them for subsequent steps in this block. Highlight that a direct proportion relationship is a multiplicative relationship between two values. Double number lines and ratio tables are useful to highlight direct proportion relationships. Begin by solving problems with unit cost, for example, working out the cost of 8 pens when each pen costs £1.50, and discuss related inverse operations, such as dividing the total cost of some pens by 1.5 to work out the number of pens. Students should also use scaling strategies to solve problems. For example, if 4 rulers cost £5 then the cost of 8 rulers can be found by multiplying the cost of 4 rulers by 2. Students will work algebraically with direct proportion later in this block.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Ratio and proportion_3_Best buy problems
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will solve financial problems, building on prior learning from Key Stage 3. Provide students opportunities to explore different strategies to compare items and cost, for example, scaling to compare the same amount of each item or working out the unit cost of each item. It is important to discuss when different strategies may be more efficient than others. Students should also be able to solve problems with common everyday life special offers, for example, “20% off’” or “3 for 2” offers. The use of a calculator is recommended for this step to place more emphasis on strategies to solve problems rather than mental arithmetic.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Ratio and proportion_4_Conversion graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students draw and interpret conversion graphs to complete approximate conversions. Explain to students that if a conversion graph forms a straight line that passes through the origin, it represents a direct proportion. Encourage students to draw a line segment to the graph when reading a value from the axes. Students can also use the learning from the previous step to work out values that are not in the range of the data represented on a graph. For example, if a graph shows that 5 cm ≈ 2 inches, then 50 cm ≈ 20 inches. Unfamiliar contexts, such as converting metric units to imperial units, are useful to encourage students to use a graph for their conversions.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Ratio and proportion_5_Exchange rates
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will revisit exchange rates, which were covered in Key Stage 3. Explain what an exchange rate implies, for example, £1 = €1.30 means every £1 is worth €1.30 Model strategies to highlight the directly proportional relationship between two values. For example, if the number of euros is found by multiplying the number of pounds by 1.30, then the number of pounds can be found by dividing the number of euros by 1.30 Double number lines can be a useful representation to support students. Ensure that students practise with a variety of currencies. Everyday life contexts are also recommended, for example, comparing prices of items in different countries.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Ratio and proportion_6_Inverse proportion
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the relationship between quantities that are inversely proportional. Students should understand that if two quantities are inversely proportional, then when one amount increases, the other amount decreases at the same rate. Students’ attention should be drawn to the fact that the product of the two quantities is constant as the quantities vary. It may be useful to begin with a scenario involving one person, such as 1 person takes 4 hours to paint a fence, before moving on to more challenging problems. Encourage students to think about whether a task will take more or less time with more people. Throughout the step, discuss any assumptions that have been made, such as assuming every person works at the same rate. Foundation students do not need to describe inverse proportion relationships algebraically, for example, y k x =

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Ratio and proportion_7_Direct proportion equations (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will use their understanding of direct proportion to form equations. Students will be introduced to the symbol ∝, which is used to denote when two variables are proportional. For example, y ∝ x means y is proportional to x, which implies direct proportion. Highlight that direct proportion equations are written in the form y = kx, where x and y are variables and k is the constant of proportionality. Encourage students to substitute known information into their formula to work out the constant of proportionality. They will then use this constant to work out different corresponding values of the variables. Students can also solve problems in context. For example, the cost, c, of pens is directly proportional to the number of pens, p.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Ratio and proportion_8_Direct inverse proportion graphs (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will plot and interpret graphical representations of directly and inversely proportional relationships, which they may have covered in Year 9. Remind students that direct proportion graphs form a straight line from the origin. Highlight that inversely proportional relationships produce a non-linear graph where the horizontal and vertical axes are asymptotes. When working with direct proportion graphs, ensure students can use proportional reasoning to complete conversions outside of the range of values of the graph. Provide students with plenty of opportunities to identify whether a graph represents a directly proportional or an inversely proportional relationship.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Sequences and proof_1_Describe and continue arithmetic and geometric sequences
small_step_name: Describe and continue arithmetic and geometric sequences
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of sequences from Key Stage 3 to describe and continue arithmetic and geometric sequences. Highlight to students that the terms “linear sequence” and “arithmetic sequence” are interchangeable. Remind students that an arithmetic sequence has a common difference whereas a geometric sequence has a common ratio. Students should have opportunities to use term-to-term rules to generate and describe sequences for both arithmetic and geometric sequences. Include questions where students find missing terms in sequences. Encourage students to look for patterns in a sequence to decide whether a number will appear later within it. It is not necessary to consider nth term rules in this step as this will be covered later in the block.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Sequences and proof_2_Explore other sequences
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of sequences from Key Stage 3 to explore less familiar sequences, including Fibonacci sequences and triangular numbers, in greater depth. Encourage students to pattern spot to find rules for sequences. Sequences with algebraic terms can be explored, such as 2a, 3a, 5a, 8a. Term-to-term rules will be given in words, and it is not required to look at algebraic rules for sequences within this step. Concrete manipulatives, such as multi-link cubes, can allow students to represent sequences and support their reasoning.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Sequences and proof_3_Generate a sequence given an algebraic rule
small_step_name: Generate a sequence given an algebraic rule
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students substitute values into expressions to generate sequences. The step focuses largely on arithmetic sequences, but some more complex types of sequence, such as quadratic and geometric, are introduced. Some students may need to recap substitution and order of operations prior to this step. Highlight to students that the value of n corresponds to the position of a term in the sequence. Substituting a value of n into an nth term rule gives the value of the corresponding term. Ensure students are confident in this as it will support the later step on deriving nth term rules of linear sequences. If appropriate, students can explore questions that require forming and solving equations to find the position of a particular value in a sequence.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Sequences and proof_4_nth term of a linear sequence
small_step_name: nth term of a linear sequence
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students review finding the nth term rule for a linear sequence from Key Stage 3. Note that this may be the first time students have found the nth term of a sequence, as this is not covered in the Key Stage 3 Support curriculum. Make explicit that the terms “arithmetic sequence” and “linear sequence” are interchangeable. Begin by exploring increasing sequences before moving on to decreasing sequences. Students who are comfortable with integer sequences can be extended with sequences with fractional and decimal terms. Ensure that students also have opportunity to explore questions that require using the nth term rule to determine whether a number exists in a sequence.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Sequences and proof_5_Represent numbers algebraically (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use algebra to represent types of numbers. Begin by guiding students to recognise that even numbers are all multiples of 2 and can be written as 2n, where n is an integer. Then encourage students to make connections between odd and even numbers, recognising that odd numbers are 1 more than even numbers and can be written as 2n + 1 Progress to exploring the results of performing the four operations on combinations of odd and even numbers, for example odd + odd = even. Using multilink cubes can support students to build and visualise number structures. Students will also be required to represent consecutive numbers algebraically, so it is vital they understand the concept of consecutive integers. Students should have the opportunity to discuss representations of multiples. For example, 6a (where a is an integer) being a multiple of 6 but also of 2 and 3

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Sequences and proof_6_Arguments and counterexamples (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of representing numbers algebraically to explore algebraic arguments and use counterexamples to disprove conjectures. It may be necessary to recap the terms prime number, square and cube. Begin by looking at counterexamples to disprove simple numerical conjectures such as “all multiples of 3 are odd” before progressing to algebraic conjectures such as “if x is an odd number, 8x will always be odd.” This foundation step does not require students to construct formal algebraic proofs.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Set notation and Venn diagrams_1_Venn diagrams
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students create and interpret Venn diagrams, consolidating learning from Year 9. Recap the key features of a Venn diagram, such as the circles each representing a set of items. It is important for students to understand that the large rectangle around the circles represents all possible elements, denoted by the universal set, ξ. By understanding the structure of a Venn diagram, students will be able to sort and interpret information efficiently, investigating whether sets intersect or whether they are mutually exclusive. Emphasise the difference between questions such as “Who plays hockey?” compared with “Who plays only hockey”? Students could explore Venn diagrams with three circles, if appropriate. Set notation is covered explicitly in the next step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Set notation and Venn diagrams_2_Set notation
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and build on learning from Year 9, identifying and representing sets using set notation. This content is new for students who have exclusively followed the support curriculum, so reviewing the Year 9 main curriculum may help reinforce understanding. Students need to be able to identify elements of a set given a description and describe a set given the elements. Having already explored the structure of a Venn diagram, students will also identify and interpret the part that represents the intersection or union of two or more sets, as well as the complement of a set. Students may need reminding that set A includes the intersection, A ∩ B, in a standard Venn diagram. Using colour or shading to highlight these areas is useful to support students’ understanding.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Set notation and Venn diagrams_3_Probabilities from Venn diagrams
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of Venn diagrams and set notation to work out probabilities. Remind students how to interpret different regions of a Venn diagram. Start with completed Venn diagrams with “countable” elements, for example, classifying shapes as “blue shapes” or “squares”. Model how to find the probability of choosing, for example, a blue square at random, by writing the number of blue squares as a fraction of the total number of shapes. Ensure that students practise writing probabilities of each region in the Venn diagram, to build understanding. As confidence develops, move onto Venn diagrams with numerical data, interleaving the use of set notation where appropriate.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Similarity and congruence_1_Identify similar shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will revisit their understanding of similar shapes from Key Stage 3. Students will build on their knowledge of enlargement to identify similar shapes. Begin by encouraging students to calculate the scale factor of enlargement for each corresponding length and highlight that if the scale factor is the same, the shapes must be similar. Remind students that angles in corresponding positions in similar shapes are equal. Once students are confident in identifying similar shapes using scale factors, they can begin to incorporate ratios to determine if shapes are similar.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Similarity and congruence_2_Find unknown lengths and angles in similar shapes
small_step_name: Find unknown lengths and angles in similar shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate unknown angles and lengths in similar shapes, building on learning from Key Stage 3. Ensure students identify corresponding sides correctly, drawing attention to examples where the shapes have different orientations. Model strategies to calculate the scale factor of enlargement between the shapes, ensuring students can work with both integer and fractional scale factors, for example the scale factor from shape A to B and shape B to A. Students should be aware that corresponding angles in similar shapes are equal. Encourage the use of geometric notation when labelling side lengths and angles.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Similarity and congruence_3_Identify similar triangles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their knowledge of angle facts to show that a pair of triangles are similar. Remind students of facts involving angles that are vertically opposite, angles that form a straight line and angles that exist within pairs of parallel lines. Ensure students see a variety of examples including triangles that share a vertex as well triangles embedded within each other, sharing a common angle. Support students to identify the corresponding vertices of two similar triangles. Highlight that if all three angles in one triangle are the same as the angles in another, then the triangles are similar. If necessary, it may be appropriate to encourage students to draw the triangles separately and in the same orientation before working out unknown lengths and angles. Students should also recognise that using side ratios is an equally valid method of establishing similarity.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Similarity and congruence_4_Solve problems with similar shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and extend their understanding of similar shapes. Begin with a mixture of questions from previous steps where students calculate scale factors between similar shapes, determining if two shapes are similar and calculating unknown lengths and angles. Once students are confident, include problems involving similar triangles. Demonstrate strategies to help visualise the problem, such as drawing any shapes with a common vertex or angle as two separate shapes in the same orientation. Students should be encouraged to discuss their approaches and reasoning when solving the problems. It may be appropriate to interleave other topic areas such as Pythagoras’ theorem or trigonometry.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Similarity and congruence_5_Prove a pair of triangles are similar (E)
small_step_name: Prove a pair of triangles are similar (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students explore general examples of similar triangles and use formal proofs to justify their similarity. They may need reminders about angle rules, such as those involving parallel lines, to help identify equal angles within a shape. Emphasise the importance of using correct geometric notation when labelling angles and lengths in their proofs. Demonstrate how to structure explanations clearly and precisely to strengthen their justifications. Reinforce that triangles are similar if all corresponding angles are equal or if their corresponding side lengths are proportional. Where appropriate, challenge students to apply Pythagoras’ theorem within similar right-angled triangles.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Similarity and congruence_6_Similarity and congruence
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the differences between similarity and congruence. Remind them that congruent shapes are identical, while similar shapes are proportional to each other. Students should compare different variations of a shape and categorise them to determine if they are congruent, similar or neither. Ensure students are exposed to shapes in different orientations to encourage them to consider corresponding lengths and angles. By reasoning in this way, students will have a better idea of where the concepts overlap and what characteristics are unique to each.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Similarity and congruence_7_Congruent triangles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will learn how to identify the properties of congruent triangles, which they may have covered in Year 9. It may be necessary to remind students of the conditions of congruence such as SSS, ASA, SAS and RHS. Begin by defining each condition of congruence, highlighting the key features and showing various examples and non-examples for each. Students should understand the minimum information needed to establish congruence between triangles. Ensure students see examples that are commonly mistaken as congruent but do not present enough information, for example, two triangles with three angles in common are similar, but more information would be needed to determine if the triangles are congruent.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Standard form_1_Numbers greater than 1 in standard form
small_step_name: Numbers greater than 1 in standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students recap and consolidate their knowledge of standard form from Key Stage 3. It may be appropriate to teach this step and the next step in tandem. They will revisit converting numbers to and from standard form, solving problems and comparing or ordering numbers in standard form. Students that have exclusively followed the support scheme will not have converted from standard form to ordinary numbers. Students may need reminding of the correct format A × 10n where 1 ≤ A < 10 and n is an integer. Students need to able to recognise when a number is in standard form; using non-examples such as 12.6 × 105 is encouraged. Place value charts can be used to support students’ deeper understanding of standard form. Using numbers in context, such as distances in the solar system, helps to highlight why standard form is an efficient way to represent very large numbers.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Standard form_2_Numbers between 0 and 1 in standard form
small_step_name: Numbers between 0 and 1 in standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students continue to recap and consolidate their knowledge of standard form, but for numbers between 0 and 1. This step may be taught in tandem with the previous step. Students will revisit both conversion to and from standard form as well as solving problems with standard form and comparing and ordering numbers in standard form. Students may need reminding that when expressing numbers between 0 and 1 in standard form, A × 10n, the value of n is a negative integer. Begin with numbers that only have one non-zero digit. Students need to able to recognise when a number is and is not in standard form; using non-examples such as 0.6 × 10–5 is encouraged. Place value charts can be used to support students to have deeper understanding of standard form. Using numbers in context, such as the size of an atom, helps to highlight why standard form is an efficient way to represent very small numbers.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Standard form_3_Adjust a number to standard form
small_step_name: Adjust a number to standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of standard form to rewrite numbers that are written in the form A × 10n, where A ≥ 10 or 0 < A < 1. The aim of this step is to help students adjust answers into correct standard form after performing calculations that result in numbers outside the standard form format. Students following the support scheme may not have encountered this concept before, however, this step allows teachers to focus specifically on refining this skill. It may be appropriate to teach this step alongside the next steps on performing the four operations with numbers in standard form. Place value grids are useful to support students’ understanding.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Standard form_4_Multiply and divide numbers in standard form (E)
small_step_name: Multiply and divide numbers in standard form (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students use their knowledge of standard form and laws of indices to multiply and divide numbers in standard form. Mental, written and calculator methods should all be introduced. Attention should be drawn to the commutative property of multiplication and the difference from division. Students who have exclusively followed the support scheme will not have previously calculated with numbers written in standard form. It may be helpful to recap adding and subtracting with directed numbers before starting this step. Begin with examples where a number in standard form is being multiplied or divided by a single value before exploring those where both numbers are in standard form, for example (5 × 102) ÷ (4 × 10–3). Students should be given the opportunity to use their knowledge from the previous step to adjust their answers to correct standard form.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Standard form_5_Add and subtract numbers in standard form (E)
small_step_name: Add and subtract numbers in standard form (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students add and subtract numbers in standard form. Students who have exclusively followed the support scheme will not have calculated with numbers written in standard form. Mental, written and calculator methods should be explored. Begin with examples where both numbers have the same power of 10 before progressing to those with different powers. Draw attention to the necessity of converting numbers in standard form to ordinary numbers as a first step if the powers in the standard form numbers are different. Students should be given the opportunity to use their knowledge from previous steps to convert answers to correct standard form.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Work with circles_1_Area and circumference of a circle
small_step_name: Area and circumference of a circle
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their prior learning from Year 10 on calculating the area and circumference of a circle. Remind students of key vocabulary related to circles such as radius, diameter, circumference and area, and how these relate to corresponding formulae for area and circumference. At this stage students’ focus should be on when each formula is appropriate to use. Provide practice involving a mixture of rounding answers to a specified degree of accuracy, for example, 3 significant figures and expressing answers in terms of pi (π). Challenge students to work out the radius or diameter of circles when given the area, making links to solving equations and rearranging formulae.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Work with circles_2_Fractional parts of a circle (area)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students broaden their understanding of the area of circles by exploring the area of fractional parts of circles, specifically split into sectors. Encourage students to calculate the area of a circle first, before focusing on what fraction of this area is needed. Discuss different strategies to work with fractions, for example, 1 5 of 50 can be calculated as 50 × 1 5 or 50 ÷ 5. Working with familiar fractions provides a solid foundation for upcoming extend steps on calculating arc lengths and sector area. This helps support that approaches such as multiplying the area of a circle by θ 360 are not always needed.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Work with circles_3_Area of a sector (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students build upon their understanding of finding the area of fractional parts of circles. Emphasise the connection to the area of a full circle, highlighting that the fraction of a circle's area taken up by a sector corresponds to its fraction of a full 360° turn. This supports students in understanding why, in general, the area of a sector with angle θ is found by θ 360 × πr2 Include examples where answers are left in terms of π as well as in decimal form to reinforce flexibility in representation. Ensure there is opportunity for students to work from a given area of a sector to calculate either the angle subtended by the arc or the radius of the sector.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Work with circles_4_Fractional parts of a circle (perimeter)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students extend their understanding of circumference by finding lengths of fractional parts of the circles, specifically sectors. This step reinforces basic fraction skills and introduces the concept of an arc, helping students see that the arc length represents a portion of the full circumference. Working with familiar fractions provides a solid foundation to prepare students to calculate arc lengths without using a formal approach, for example, θ 360 × πd. Model how to calculate arc lengths first, before solving problems involving the perimeter of a sector.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Work with circles_5_Arc length and perimeter (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding from the previous step to find the arc length and the perimeter of a sector. Ensure students are confident in calculating the circumference from a givin radius before calculating the arc length and perimeter of a sector. Draw students' attention to the size of the angle, θ, subtended by the arc length and how this informs what fraction of the whole circle is represented by the sector with the same radius. Emphasise that θ 360 × πd calculates only the arc length and not the perimeter of the sector. After practising arc length calculations, guide students to find the perimeter by adding the two radii to the arc length. Include examples with answers both in terms of π and decimal form to support flexibility in representation. If appropriate students could also discuss "minor" and "major" sectors and arcs.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Work with circles_6_Area and perimeter of compound shapes with circles
small_step_name: Area and perimeter of compound shapes with circles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the area and perimeter of compound shapes, building on their knowledge of fractional parts of circles. Encourage students to identify familiar 2-D shapes within a compound shape. Include a mixture of examples where compound shapes are adjoined, as well as where compound shapes are formed by removing a shape from another shape. Where relevant and appropriate, discuss efficient methods for problems such as those that include two congruent semicircles. Students may discuss whether they can use the formula for a whole circle, rather than performing separate calculations for each.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Foundation_Work with circles_7_Solve problems with circles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding from previous steps in the block to solve contextual problems involving circles. Goal-free prompts are effective to encourage exploration and build confidence with problem solving. Practice should include problems involving percentages and fractions, for example, determining what percentage of a shape is shaded. For multi-step problems, breaking down steps and organising work into tables can help students solve systematically and efficiently. Ensure students have opportunities to give their answer approximately (as a decimal) or in exact form (in terms of π).

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Area and volume_1_Plans and elevations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will draw accurate and scaled plans as well as front and side elevations of 3-D shapes. They should begin by drawing plans and elevations of cubes and cuboids before moving onto more complex shapes such as prisms. Allowing students access to physical objects such as interlocking cubes to make shapes or dynamic geometry software may be useful to help students visualise objects before attempting to draw plans and elevations. If appropriate, have students draw or describe shapes when given the plan and elevation views. This includes being able to draw them on isometric paper, which was covered in the previous step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Area and volume_2_Volume of a sphere
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use the formula to calculate the volume of a sphere. Tell students that the formula to work out the volume of a sphere with radius r is 4 3 πr3, and model how to input relevant calculations into a calculator, for example 4 3 × π × 53, to work out the volume of a sphere with a radius of 5 cm. As with other formulae involving pi (π), students should have the opportunity to provide exact answers in terms of pi (π), as well as giving approximate answers to varying degrees of accuracy. When students are comfortable with finding the volume of a sphere, they could begin to investigate the volume of hemispheres and compound shapes.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Area and volume_3_Volume of cones and pyramids
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate the volume of cones and pyramids. Students may not have covered this before, as this is an extend step in Year 9. When introducing the volume of a cone, it may be useful to draw out similarities with the formula for the volume of a cylinder. That is, the volume of a cone is 1 3 of the volume of a cylinder with the same height and radius. Then further similarity may be shown between the formulae for cones and pyramids. For example, the volume of both can be calculated with 1 3 × area of base × height. Provide opportunities for students to work backwards from a given volume to find dimensions of the cone or pyramid. If appropriate, explore problems where using Pythagoras’ theorem may be necessary to calculate the perpendicular height of the cone or pyramid.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Area and volume_4_Surface area of spheres and cones
small_step_name: Surface area of spheres and cones
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will use formulae to calculate the surface area of spheres and cones. The formulae for each of these are given, although it should be noted that the formula given for the surface area of a cone is often only for the curved surface area. As with previous steps, students should be encouraged to give answers to varied degrees of accuracy. They should also be given opportunities to rearrange formulae, such as calculating the radius of a cone when given the surface area and slant height. Once comfortable with spheres and cones, students can investigate the surface area of related shapes such as hemispheres or other 3-D solids made from these.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Area and volume_5_Volume and surface area of a frustrum (E)
small_step_name: Volume and surface area of a frustrum (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students work out volume and surface area of frustums made from cones or pyramids. Students may need reminding of strategies to work out unknown lengths in similar shapes, as this will often be needed to access frustum problems. It may also be useful to scaffold problems initially to build confidence, for example, working out the volume of a cone then “removing” a smaller and mathematically similar cone to create a frustum. Students should solve problems involving both volume and surface area, rounding answers to varied degrees of accuracy.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Area and volume_6_Convert metric units of area and volume
small_step_name: Convert metric units of area and volume
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students convert metric units of area and volume. This may have been previously studied in the extend step in Year 9. Initially, explore the area of a square 1 cm in length and compare it to the area of a square 10 mm in length, to emphasise how 1 cm² is equal to 100 mm². A similar approach can be used to convert units of volume using a 1 cm cube compared to the volume of a 10 mm cube. Students should recognise that the area scale factor is the square of the linear scale factor, and the volume scale factor is the cube of the linear scale factor. Students can then progress to converting between units in a single calculation. Links between other measurements of volume such as m3 and cm3 should also be explored.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Circle theorems_10_Solve problems with circle theorems
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students focus on solving problems involving circle theorems and angle facts. Begin by reviewing each theorem to ensure students are confident in identifying them. Provide diagrams of circles with features such as points on the circumference, chords, tangents and intersecting lines, and prompt students to determine which theorems can be used to find unknown angles or lengths. Encourage students mark known angles and lengths on diagrams and justify their reasoning for each step, reinforcing when and why each theorem applies. Guide students to document each angle calculation in a logical order, clearly justifying which facts they used, to help them develop a structured approach to problem-solving.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Circle theorems_1_Angles in circles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students focus on finding angles in circles, specifically using properties of isosceles triangles and angles on a straight line. Remind students how to work out unknown angles in isosceles triangles and on straight lines and ensure they can interpret angle notation. Model how to annotate diagrams to identify isosceles triangles, for example, marking the radii with hatch marks to indicate they are equal in length. Encourage students to look for instances where angles form a straight line, allowing them to form an equation equal to 180° to find the value of unknown angles. As students progress, introduce more complex circle diagrams that combine the angle properties of isosceles triangles and straight lines, helping them develop a multi-step approach to solving geometry problems.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Circle theorems_2_Circle theorem (angles at the centre and circumference)
small_step_name: Circle theorem (angles at the centre and circumference)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students begin learning the first of eight circle theorems. They explore how the angle at the centre of a circle subtended by an arc is twice the angle at the circumference subtended by the same arc. Students draw upon their prior knowledge of various angle facts from Key Stage 3, such as the sum of the interior angles of a triangle. Dynamic geometry software is an effective visual tool to investigate the relationship between the angle at the centre and the angle at the circumference, as it allows students to see how these angles change with different arrangements. It is also beneficial to include examples with chords and angles in various orientations, not just when the angle at the circumference is at the “top” of the circle.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Circle theorems_3_Circle theorem (angles in a semicircle)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem exploring how any angle inscribed in a semicircle (subtended by the diameter) is always 90°. Using dynamic geometry software can help students to see this is the case no matter where on the circumference the vertex of the angle is. Encourage students to apply their understanding of angles in a triangle to solve problems with triangles inscribed in a semicircle. They should annotate diagrams by labelling the right angle and discuss strategies to work out unknown angles. Additionally, explain that this theorem can be seen as a special case of the previous step, where the angle at the centre of the circle is 180°. If appropriate, previous topics involving right-angled triangles, such as Pythagoras’ theorem and trigonometry, can be included in circle problems for students to solve.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Circle theorems_4_Circle theorem (angles in the same segment)
small_step_name: Circle theorem (angles in the same segment)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem that states that angles in the same segment of a circle are equal. This theorem can be proven by using the theorem that states the angle at the centre of a circle is twice the angle at the circumference. Ensure students can identify major and minor segments in a circle. Drawing an auxiliary chord can support students to see that the angles are in the same segment. The use of dynamic geometry software is a useful visual to show students a variety of diagrams to help them recognise the relationship beyond the typical “bow-tie” shape. As they deepen their understanding, students can combine this theorem with the circle theorems covered earlier in the block to tackle more complex problems. Encourage students to justify each step of their working by explaining their reasoning clearly.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Circle theorems_5_Circle theorem (angles in a cyclic quadrilateral)
small_step_name: Circle theorem (angles in a cyclic quadrilateral)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem that opposite angles in a cyclic quadrilateral sum to 180°. Ensure students have a secure knowledge of the properties of different quadrilaterals and the sum of their interior angles. Emphasise that a quadrilateral is cyclic when all four vertices lie on the circumference of a circle. Encourage students to measure pairs of opposite angles and observe that they always add up to 180°. The use of dynamic geometry software to manipulate the shape can help reinforce this concept, showing that the relationship remains consistent regardless of where the vertices lie on the circumference. This relationship can also be proven by connecting it to the theorem that the angle at the centre is twice the angle at the circumference. As students’ understanding develops, encourage students to solve problems involving other circle theorems covered so far in the block.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Circle theorems_6_Circle theorem (perpendicular from the centre to a chord)
small_step_name: Circle theorem (perpendicular from the centre to a chord)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem that a perpendicular line segment from the centre of a circle to a chord, bisects the chord. Ensure students are confident in their understanding of what a chord is. Emphasise the fact that when a radius bisects a chord, the angle between the radius and the chord is always 90°. The use of dynamic geometry software allows students to manipulate the position of the chord and to observe that the perpendicular relationship remains consistent. If appropriate, relate this topic to construction and loci, modelling that a perpendicular bisector of a chord must pass through the centre of a circle, and doing this twice on separate chords in turn locates the centre of the circle.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Circle theorems_7_Circle theorem (angle between radius and tangent)
small_step_name: Circle theorem (angle between radius and tangent)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem that the angle between a radius and tangent of a circle, that meet at the same point on the circumference, is 90°. Ensure students understand that a tangent is a line that touches the circumference of the circle at only one point. Dynamic geometry software can help students to visualise how this remains true as the tangent moves or the circle’s size changes. Encourage students to annotate diagrams with angles, for example, labelling known right angles. As students’ understanding develops, explore problems where other right-angled related topics are present, for example, using Pythagoras’ theorem to find unknown lengths of line segments.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Circle theorems_8_Circle theorem (two tangents from a point)
small_step_name: Circle theorem (two tangents from a point)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the theorem that two tangents from a single external point to the circle are equal in length. Allow students to observe the lengths of the tangents in many orientations, using dynamic geometry software to move the external point around outside the circle, as well as towards and further away from the circumference.
