Emphasise that these tangents are of equal length and therefore form an isosceles triangle when a chord connects the tangents’ points of contact with the circumference. Additionally, drawing radii from the circle’s centre to the tangents, and a line segment to connect the external point to the centre, forms two congruent right-angled triangles. Encourage students to annotate diagrams with known angles and lengths, marking right angles where the radius meets the tangent and identifying equal tangent lengths. As their understanding develops, explore problems where they apply this relationship in other areas of maths, such as pairs of congruent triangles and properties of kites.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Circle theorems_9_Circle theorem (alternate segment theorem) (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students explore the alternate segment theorem, which states that the angle between a tangent and a chord at the point of contact is equal to the angle in the alternate segment. Students must understand that a segment is the region enclosed by a chord and its arc, with the larger area as the major segment and the smaller as the minor segment. Highlight the angle between the tangent and chord meeting on the circumference and the angle in the alternate segment. Measure both angles to emphasise their equality. Explore the relationship further by adjusting the chord or tangent using dynamic geometry software. As understanding develops, provide problems to apply this theorem with other circle theorems.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Ratio and proportion_1_Solve problems with ratio
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will revisit problems involving ratio covered in Year 10. Provide opportunities for students to share amounts in a ratio when given either a total, a part, or a difference. Bar models can help students to identify key differences between problems and to establish a suitable strategy to solve them, rather than relying on a procedural method, such as dividing an amount by the sum of the parts in a given ratio. Students should also explore links between fractions and ratios. Note that “combine a set of ratios” and “link ratio and algebra” are both Extend steps in Year 10 and may not have been covered previously. Time could be given to each of these steps if appropriate.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Ratio and proportion_2_Best buy problems
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will solve financial problems, building on prior learning from Key Stage 3. Provide students opportunities to explore different strategies to compare items and cost, for example, scaling to compare the same amount of each item or working out the unit cost of each item. It is important to discuss when different strategies may be more efficient than others. Students should also be able to solve problems with common everyday life special offers, for example, “20% off’” or “3 for 2” offers. The use of a calculator is recommended for this step to place more emphasis on strategies to solve problems rather than mental arithmetic.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Ratio and proportion_3_Conversion graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students draw and interpret conversion graphs to complete approximate conversions. Explain to students that if a conversion graph forms a straight line passing through the origin, it represents a direct proportion. Encourage students to draw a line segment to the graph when reading a value from the axes. Students can also use the learning from the previous step to work out values that are not in the range of the data represented on a graph. For example, if a graph shows that 5 cm ≈ 2 inches, then 50 cm ≈ 20 inches. Unfamiliar contexts, such as converting metric units to imperial units, are useful to encourage students to use a graph for their conversions.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Ratio and proportion_4_Exchange rates
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will revisit exchange rates, which were covered in Key Stage 3. Explain what an exchange rate implies, for example, £1 = €1.30 means every £1 is worth €1.30 Model strategies to highlight the directly proportional relationship between two values. For example, if the number of euros is found by multiplying the number of pounds by 1.30, then the number of pounds can be found by dividing the number of euros by 1.30 Double number lines can be a useful representation to support students. Ensure that students practise with a variety of currencies. Everyday life contexts are also recommended, for example, comparing prices of items in different countries.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Ratio and proportion_5_Direct proportion equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will use their understanding of direct proportion to form equations. Students will be introduced to the symbol ∝, which is used to denote when two variables are proportional. For example, y ∝ x means y is proportional to x, which implies direct proportion. Highlight that direct proportion equations are written in the form y = kx, where x and y are variables and k is the constant of proportionality. Encourage students to substitute known information into their formula to work out the constant of proportionality. They will then use this constant to work out different corresponding values of the variables. Students can also solve problems in context. For example, the cost, c, of pens is directly proportional to the number of pens, p.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Ratio and proportion_6_Complex direct proportion equations (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will build on their understanding from the previous step to solve direct proportion equations involving powers and roots. Highlight the differences between formulae of different forms, such as y = kx2 and y = k x , and ensure that students are familiar with vocabulary linked to powers and roots. For example, x2 means “the square of x” and x means “the square root of x. Students should be reminded that equations such as x2 = 25 have a positive and negative solution.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Ratio and proportion_7_Inverse proportion
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the relationship between quantities that are inversely proportional. Students should understand that if two quantities are inversely proportional, then when one amount increases, the other amount decreases at the same rate. Students’ attention should be drawn to the fact that the product of the two quantities is constant as the quantities vary. It may be useful to begin with a scenario involving one person, such as 1 person takes 4 hours to paint a fence, before moving on to more challenging problems. Encourage students to think about whether a task will take more or less time with more people. Throughout the step, discuss any assumptions that have been made, such as assuming every person works at the same rate.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Ratio and proportion_8_Inverse proportion equations (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will build on their understanding from the previous step to form and solve inverse proportion equations. Students will learn that “y is inversely proportional to x” is the same as “y is directly proportional to 1 x ”, which can be written as y ∝ 1 x . They will also learn that inverse proportion formulae are written in the form y k x = , where k is the constant of proportionality. Once students can create equations in this form, provide opportunities to practise with more complex formulae such as y = k x2 or y = k x 3 , highlighting key vocabulary such as “the square of” or “the root of”. If appropriate, students could solve a combination of problems using both direct and inverse proportion.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Ratio and proportion_9_Direct and indirect proportion graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will plot and interpret graphical representations of directly and inversely proportional relationships, which they may have covered in Year 9. Remind students that direct proportion graphs form a straight line from the origin. Highlight that inversely proportional relationships produce a non-linear graph where the horizontal and vertical axis are asymptotes. When working with direct proportion graphs, ensure students can use proportional reasoning to complete conversions outside of the range of values of the graph. Provide students with plenty of opportunities to identify whether a graph represents a directly proportional or an inversely proportional relationship.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Sequences and proof_1_Describe and continue sequences
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of sequences from Key Stage 3 to explore arithmetic and geometric sequences, as well as less familiar sequences including quadratic and Fibonacci sequences, and triangular numbers. Allow students to become familiar with the definitions of “arithmetic”, “geometric” and “Fibonacci”. Highlight to students that the terms “linear sequence” and “arithmetic sequence” are interchangeable. Students should have opportunities to use term-to-term rules to generate and describe sequences. Include questions where students find missing terms in sequences. Encourage students to pattern spot to find rules for sequences. Sequences with algebraic terms may be introduced, but keep in mind that this is specifically looked at in the next step. It is not necessary to consider nth term rules in this step, as this is covered later in the block.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Sequences and proof_2_Explore sequences with algebraic terms
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore arithmetic, geometric and Fibonacci sequences that have algebraic terms. Students will be expected to use their knowledge of common difference and common ratio and the structure of Fibonacci sequences within this step. Ensure students are confident in manipulating algebraic expressions, as this is essential for finding missing algebraic terms within sequences. Students may be required to form and solve simultaneous equations to find values of unknowns, or factorise expressions to find common ratios. This step does not involve students using or finding nth term rules.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Sequences and proof_3_Describe and continue sequences involving surds (E)
small_step_name: Describe and continue sequences involving surds (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge from previous steps to explore sequences where the terms, common differences or common ratios are surds. It may be necessary to recap manipulating expressions involving surds before introducing this step to students. Students will be required to use the four operations with surds as well as expand double brackets involving surds. This step includes arithmetic, geometric and Fibonacci sequences. Highlight that simplifying expressions can help with recognising patterns in sequences, for example, in a geometric sequence with 1st and 2nd terms 3 3 and 6 12 , it is easier to see the common ratio is 4 when the 2nd term is simplified to 12 3 . It is not necessary for students to use or work out nth term rules in this step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Sequences and proof_4_Generate a sequence given an algebraic rule
small_step_name: Generate a sequence given an algebraic rule
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students substitute values into expressions to generate sequences. The step focuses on a variety of sequences, including arithmetic, quadratic and geometric. Some students may need to recap substitution and order of operations prior to this step. Highlight to students that the value of n corresponds to the position of a term in the sequence and that substituting a value of n into an nth term rule gives the value of the corresponding term. Ensure students are confident in this, as it will support the later step on the nth term rule. If appropriate, students can explore questions that require forming and solving equations or inequalities to find the position of a particular value in a sequence.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Sequences and proof_5_nth term of a linear sequence
small_step_name: nth term of a linear sequence
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will review finding the nth term rule for a linear sequence from Key Stage 3. Make explicit that the terms “arithmetic sequence” and “linear sequence” are interchangeable. Begin by exploring increasing sequences before moving on to decreasing sequences. Students who are comfortable with integer sequences can be extended with sequences containing fractional and decimal terms. Ensure that students also have opportunity to explore questions that require using the nth term rule to determine whether a number exists in a sequence.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Sequences and proof_6_nth term of a quadratic sequence (E)
small_step_name: nth term of a quadratic sequence (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to finding the nth term of a quadratic sequence for the first time. Begin by looking at sequences of the form n2 + c. Focus students on the difference between these sequences and the sequence n2. Discuss the pattern of differences and second differences before introducing sequences of the form an2, drawing attention to the value of a being half the second difference. Once students are confident, move to finding nth terms of sequences of the form an2 + bn and finally sequences of the form an2 + bn + c. Problems that involve finding whether a number is a term of a quadratic sequence or finding the position of a particular term can be explored if appropriate. Students may need reminding of methods for solving quadratics prior to attempting these problems.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Sequences and proof_7_Represent numbers algebraically
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use algebra to represent types of numbers. Begin by guiding students to recognise that even numbers are all multiples of 2 and can be written as 2n, where n is an integer. Then encourage students to make connections between odd and even numbers, recognising that odd numbers are 1 more than even numbers and can be written as 2n + 1 Progress to exploring the results of performing the four operations on combinations of odd and even numbers, for example odd + odd = even. Using multilink cubes can support students to build and visualise number structures. Students will also be required to represent consecutive numbers algebraically, so it is vital they understand the concept of consecutive integers. Students should have the opportunity to discuss representations of multiples. For example, 6a (where a is an integer) being a multiple of 6 but also of 2 and 3

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Sequences and proof_8_Algebraic arguments and proof
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of representing numbers algebraically to complete algebraic proofs. Students will need to be comfortable with algebraic manipulation, so begin by recapping simplifying expressions and expanding and factorising quadratics. Draw attention to the need for rigorous algebraic proof rather than repeated numerical examples. Explore factorisations of expressions to highlight specific properties, for example, 12m + 6 can be shown to be a multiple of 2, 3 or 6 depending on the chosen factorisation. Students will require the knowledge that if n is an integer, n + 1 represents the next consecutive integer, and that 2n will be even and 2n + 1 will be odd.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Set notation and Venn diagrams_1_Set notation
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and build on learning from Year 9, identifying and representing sets using set notation. Students need to be able to identify elements of a set given a description and describe a set given the elements. Students will also identify and interpret the region of a Venn diagram that represents the intersection or union of two or more sets. Students may need reminding that set A includes the intersection A ∩ B, in a standard Venn diagram. Using colour or shading to highlight these areas is useful to support students understanding. Students should also consider more advanced notation such as A ∩ B’ or (A ∪ B)’.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Set notation and Venn diagrams_2_Venn diagrams
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students create and interpret Venn diagrams and work out probabilities from them. It is important for students to understand that the large rectangle around the circles represents all possible elements, denoted by the universal set, ξ. By understanding the structure of a Venn diagram, students will be able to sort and interpret information efficiently, investigating whether sets intersect or whether they are mutually exclusive. Emphasise the difference between questions such as “Who plays hockey?” compared with “Who plays only hockey”? Ensure that students practise writing probabilities of each region in the Venn diagram, and as confidence develops, move on to Venn diagrams with three circles.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Set notation and Venn diagrams_3_Solve problems with Venn diagrams
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on learning from previous steps in the block to solve problems with Venn diagrams. The problems appear in different forms, incorporating other topics such as solving equations and fractions of amounts. It may be worth investing time to read through questions as a class and explore useful strategies before students work on these independently. Goal-free problems are an excellent way to take the emphasis away from finding an answer, instead encouraging students to focus on what can be worked out and how. It may be beneficial to start with problems that are broken down into individual steps, before progressing to more complex problems. If appropriate, more advanced set notation such as P(A ∪ B)’ can be used.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Set notation and Venn diagrams_4_Conditional probability (Venn diagrams and two-way tables (E)
small_step_name: Conditional probability (Venn diagrams and two-way tables (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students interpret conditional probabilities from Venn diagrams and two-way tables. Recapping constructing and interpreting Venn diagrams and two-way tables may be useful for students. It is important that students identify which set of outcomes they are selecting from and that they understand the key concept that “given” means that only one set of outcomes are relevant when selecting the event. Emphasise the difference between questions such as “What is the probability that they chose red?” compared with “Given that they are in Year 11, what is the probability that they chose red”?

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Similarity and congruence_1_Find unknown lengths and angles in similar shapes
small_step_name: Find unknown lengths and angles in similar shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate unknown angles and lengths in similar shapes, building on learning from Key Stage 3. Ensure students identify corresponding sides correctly, drawing attention to examples where the shapes have different orientations. Model strategies to calculate the scale factor of enlargement between the shapes, ensuring students can work with both integer and fractional scale factors, for example the scale factor from shape A to B and shape B to A. Students should be aware that corresponding angles in similar shapes are equal. Encourage the use of geometric notation when labelling side lengths and angles.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Similarity and congruence_2_Identify similar triangles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their knowledge of angle facts to show that a pair of triangles are similar. Remind students of facts involving angles that are vertically opposite, angles that form a straight line and angles that exist within pairs of parallel lines. Ensure students see a variety of examples including triangles that share a vertex as well triangles embedded within each other, sharing a common angle. Support students to identify the corresponding vertices of two similar triangles. Highlight that if all three angles in one triangle are the same as the angles in another, then the triangles are similar. If necessary, it may be appropriate to encourage students to draw the triangles separately and in the same orientation before working out unknown lengths and angles. Students should also recognise that using side ratios is an equally valid method of establishing similarity.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Similarity and congruence_3_Solve problems with similar shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and extend their understanding of similar shapes. Begin with a mixture of questions from previous steps where students calculate scale factors between similar shapes, determining if two shapes are similar and calculating unknown lengths and angles. Once students are confident, include problems involving similar triangles. Demonstrate strategies to help visualise the problem, such as drawing any shapes with a common vertex or angle as two separate shapes in the same orientation. Students should be encouraged to discuss their approaches and reasoning when solving the problems. It may be appropriate to interleave other topic areas such as Pythagoras’ theorem or trigonometry.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Similarity and congruence_4_Prove a pair of triangles are similar
small_step_name: Prove a pair of triangles are similar
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students explore general examples of similar triangles and use formal proofs to justify their similarity. They may need reminders about angle rules, such as those involving parallel lines, to help identify equal angles within a shape. Emphasise the importance of using correct geometric notation when labelling angles and lengths in their proofs. Demonstrate how to structure explanations clearly and precisely to strengthen their justifications. Reinforce that triangles are similar if all corresponding angles are equal or if their corresponding side lengths are proportional. Where appropriate, challenge students to apply Pythagoras’ theorem within similar right-angled triangles.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Similarity and congruence_5_Areas of similar shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the relationship between the areas of similar shapes. Start by guiding students to spot patterns between the scale factors for lengths and areas, highlighting that the area scale factor is the square of the length scale factor. Note that length scale factor is also known as the linear scale factor. Students should explore identifying area scale factors that are not integers, expressing such examples as a fraction instead of decimal. Once students are confident in identifying the area scale factor, extend their understanding to find unknown areas or lengths in 2-D shapes, including opportunities to practise working with surface areas of 3-D shapes. If appropriate, make the connection to learning from the previous block where students converted between metric units of area.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Similarity and congruence_6_Volumes of similar shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the links between the volumes of similar 3-D shapes. Like the previous step, begin by calculating the volumes of similar cubes and cuboids and draw students’ attention to the relationship between the length scale factor and the volume scale factor. Students should understand the volume scale factor is the cube of the length scale factor. Ensure students are familiar with representing the volumes of similar solids as a ratio. Encourage non-integer scale factors to be expressed as fractions instead of decimals. Once students are confident with identifying the volume scale factor between two similar solids, have them use it to calculate unknown volumes and lengths of 3-D shapes. Calculating unknown volumes from given surface areas of similar solids is covered in the next step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Similarity and congruence_7_Solve problems with area and volume in similar shapes
small_step_name: Solve problems with area and volume in similar shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of calculating areas and volumes in similar shapes from previous steps. Students will also apply their understanding of calculating surface area and volumes of more complex 3-D shapes from the previous step. Begin by recapping calculating area and volume scale factors before using this to find the areas and volumes of similar shapes. Students should also explore problems where the surface area or volume of both shapes is given. Encourage students to use ratios to calculate any unknown lengths, areas or volumes. Scaffolding multi-step problems can help students access these types of problems.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Similarity and congruence_8_Prove a pair of triangles are congruent
small_step_name: Prove a pair of triangles are congruent
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students will identify and establish the difference between congruent and similar shapes, using conditions of congruence. Remind them that congruent shapes are identical, while similar shapes are proportional to each other. It may be necessary to remind students of the conditions of congruence such as SSS, ASA, SAS and RHS. Begin by defining each condition of congruence, highlighting the key features and showing various examples and non-examples for each. Students should understand the minimum information needed to establish congruence. Ensure students see examples that are commonly mistaken as congruent but do not present enough information, for example, two triangles with three angles in common are similar, but more information would be needed to determine if the triangles are congruent.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Standard form_1_Numbers in standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students recap and consolidate their knowledge of standard form from Key Stage 3. They will revisit conversion to and from standard form, as well as comparing and ordering numbers in standard form. Students may need reminding of the correct form A × 10n, where 1 ≤ A < 10 and n is an integer. Students need to able to recognise when a number is or is not in standard form; using non-examples such as 0.6 × 10–5 is encouraged. Using numbers in context, such as diameters of planets, helps to highlight why standard form is an efficient way to write large numbers. This step also includes opportunities to adjust a number to standard form.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Standard form_2_Four operations with numbers in standard form
small_step_name: Four operations with numbers in standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students perform the four operations on numbers written in standard form. Mental, written and calculator methods should all be explored. Recapping index laws may be helpful before multiplying and dividing numbers written in standard form. For adding and subtracting, students should begin with calculations where the power of 10 is the same in each number before progressing to those where they differ. Encourage students to discuss whether they prefer to convert to ordinary numbers before adding or subtracting, or whether they are comfortable using their knowledge of place value. Throughout this step, ensure students adjust answers to correct standard form where needed. Calculations involving a combination of operations will be covered in the next step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Standard form_3_Solve problems with standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their prior knowledge to solve more complex problems involving numbers in standard form. Begin with simple calculations involving a mix of operations before progressing to worded problems. Give students the opportunity to solve problems that include working with numbers written in standard form within, for example, percentage, average and substitution problems. Students should explore “show that” and “spot the mistake” questions to develop reasoning and error-spotting skills. Ensure students are given the opportunity to round to significant figures with numbers written in standard form. Students may find it helpful to use a calculator where required.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Vectors_1_Solve problems with vectors
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This small step provides an opportunity to revisit problems with vector geometry, which were covered in Year 10. Students may need to be reminded of different ways to represent vectors, such as numerical column vectors or algebraic expressions. Remind students that the numbers in a column vector represent the horizontal and vertical direction and that multiplying these by a scalar will affect both. For example, 2 × 4 3   = 8 6   Students should also understand that if one vector is a multiple of another, then the vectors are parallel, ensuring that they can apply this to both numerical column vectors and vectors represented as algebraic expressions.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Vectors_2_Ratios with vectors (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding of ratio to vector geometry. Representing vectors on a grid is a useful strategy to allow students to see relationships between vectors and the ratios linking different points. Highlight proportional relationships when ratios are used in combination with vectors. For example, if AN : NB = 1 : 2, then the point AN must be 1 3 along the line AB. It can be beneficial for students to practise expanding brackets such as 2 3(4a + b). Students should also be given the opportunity to explore vector journeys, allowing them to reinforce prior knowledge. Scaffolding questions where appropriate will help students to formulate strategies which will be useful for more complex problems.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Vectors_3_Collinear points using vectors (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students make links between vectors and collinear points. Students will need to be confident in identifying parallel vectors before attempting this step. They will often be asked to “prove” or “show that” three points are collinear. Highlight that if two vectors are parallel and share a common point, then the three points are collinear and form a straight line. Students must state both of these conditions as part of a full explanation to demonstrate collinearity. Stem sentences can be useful to encourage students to explain how they know three points are collinear. Using vectors on a grid is an effective strategy to introduce the concept, before eventually working with more abstract diagrams.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Autumn_Higher_Vectors_4_Geometric arguments and proofs using vectors (E)
small_step_name: Geometric arguments and proofs using vectors (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Students use this small step to consolidate and deepen their understanding of the previous steps in this block, with a focus on vector journeys. Students should be able to write vectors when given information about the ratio of distances between points on a line. If appropriate, isolate key learning points using straight lines first, before moving on to vectors in shapes such as triangles or parallelograms. Model how to construct an algebraic proof or argument, working through problems in stages, and encourage students to discuss what each new piece of information represents. “Show that” problems are useful to encourage students to reason with vector problems.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Angles bearings and trigonometry_10_Solve problems with bearings using Pythagoras and trigonometry (E)
small_step_name: Solve problems with bearings using Pythagoras and trigonometry (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students use their prior knowledge of Pythagoras’ theorem and trigonometry to solve problems with bearings. Students may need to draw north lines and auxiliary lines on diagrams to form right-angled triangles. Encourage students to draw separate triangles if necessary and label all relevant information to help them identify which method is the most appropriate and efficient. Students may need support to form diagrams from worded questions, but scaffolding should be removed as students become more confident. Encourage them to annotate their work by showing angle rules and trigonometric reasoning.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Angles bearings and trigonometry_1_Angles in lines and shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students review basic angle rules in lines and shapes. Ensure they are confident identifying when angles sum to 180° or 360°, and that vertically opposite angles are equal. Start with angles on a straight line or around a point before calculating unknown angles in triangles and quadrilaterals. Encourage students to explain how they identify and calculate angles, using correct vocabulary and angle rules. If appropriate, provide sentence stems to help them structure their reasoning. Once students are confident, present diagrams that require multiple rules to work out unknown angles. Ensure students are confident identifying and labelling angles, especially when using notation such as ∠ABC. Calculators can be used if appropriate.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Angles bearings and trigonometry_2_Solve problems with angles in parallel lines
small_step_name: Solve problems with angles in parallel lines
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit angles in parallel lines, building on their understanding of alternate, corresponding and co- interior angles. This step provides opportunities to deepen their understanding through a mixture of fluency practice and problem-solving. Use both examples and non-examples of parallel lines to help students accurately identify when lines are parallel and which angle rules can be applied. Extend the challenge by introducing angles in algebraic form, requiring students to set up and solve equations to find unknown angles.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Angles bearings and trigonometry_3_Solve problems with angles and algebra (E)
small_step_name: Solve problems with angles and algebra (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students combine their knowledge of angle facts with forming algebraic expressions and equations. Bar models can be a useful tool to help students model, form and solve equations, particularly when angles are equal or form a known total, for example, 180°. Ensure students are confident in solving equations with unknowns on both sides, as this will be needed when forming equations from a set of angles given in algebraic form that are equal.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Angles bearings and trigonometry_4_Compass points and related angles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit their understanding of angles and apply this to compass points in preparation for later steps on bearings. Students should be familiar with the major compass points and directions. Explicit links should be made between these and angle measurements, for example, north to east represents a 90° turn clockwise. This step allows students to deepen their understanding by investigating the degrees of turn between the different cardinal directions. Students can be challenged to use a map or scale drawing to describe the positions of different places in relation to one another.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Angles bearings and trigonometry_5_Understand and represent bearings
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to bearings as a specific type of angle, always measured clockwise from north and expressed using three figures. Use real-world contexts such as giving directions, map reading or aviation problems to illustrate the concept. Provide both examples and non-examples to deepen understanding. Highlight the wording “bearing of A from B”, as this can often cause confusion, and emphasise identifying the angle carefully with a range of start and end points. Discuss the convention that the north line is typically drawn vertically up the page, regardless of the actual direction of north. Throughout this step, make regular connections to angles within compass points.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Angles bearings and trigonometry_6_Measure and read bearings
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn how to measure and read bearings in a variety of problem-solving contexts with a focus on interpreting diagrams and using protractors. Students may need to refer to the rules of bearings from the previous step throughout when practising this new skill. Ensure that students are proficient in using a protractor to measure angles before this step. Include examples of bearings that are greater than 180° and those whereby auxiliary lines need to be drawn to indicate north. Students may need support in identifying which bearing to measure depending on the wording of the questions. For example, “the bearing of A from B” and “the bearing of B from A”. A visualiser is useful for modelling and to support students’ understanding.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Angles bearings and trigonometry_7_Scale drawings using bearings
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn how to measure and read bearings in practical situations and tackle more complex problems that involve drawing scale diagrams. To encourage precise use of a protractor, it may be helpful to avoid square paper. Students should be confident with scale drawings and able to interpret scales presented in different forms, such as 1 cm represents 2 m or 1 : 200 Emphasise the importance of accuracy, with students producing neat, clearly labelled diagrams using a ruler and protractor where required. As in previous steps, a visualiser is useful for modelling and to support students’ understanding.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Angles bearings and trigonometry_8_Calculate bearings using angles rules (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students compare multiple methods of problem solving with bearings by using their prior knowledge of angle rules and facts. Students may need reminding that north lines are parallel. Therefore, they should be encouraged to draw auxiliary lines so that the rules for angles in parallel lines can be used. Emphasise the importance of reading the question carefully, noting where to measure the bearing from. Encourage students to annotate diagrams, adding known bearings, unknown angles and reasoning as they work.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Angles bearings and trigonometry_9_Choose appropriate methods to solve problems with right-angled triangles
small_step_name: Choose appropriate methods to solve problems with right-angled triangles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students select and apply Pythagoras’ theorem or trigonometric ratios effectively. To ensure they are confident making this choice, it may be useful to first revisit each method individually and consolidate fluency with the different calculations. Give students a range of mixed questions, where they will need to identify which method is the most appropriate. Emphasise to students that, in some cases, either method can be used. Students can be challenged with multi-step problems requiring them to perform trigonometric ratio calculations more than once. Remind students to ensure their calculators are set to degrees when calculating with trigonometric ratios.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Constructions and Loci_10_Solve loci problems
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply the skills they have developed throughout the block to solve loci problems. Provide a range of problems to help them refine their decision-making when creating constructions. Include tasks that combine previous constructions and involve drawing to scale. “Spot the mistake” activities are useful for encouraging discussion, identifying misconceptions, and reinforcing correct methods. Emphasise the importance of leaving construction lines visible. As in earlier steps, using a visualiser is recommended to model the process of solving loci problems.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Constructions and Loci_1_Use geometrical equipment
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use protractors and compasses to construct and measure accurately. This step allows pupils to practise using geometrical equipment in preparation for accurately drawing polygons and completing constructions. Remind students how to measure angles correctly with a protractor, drawing attention to the two scales and explaining when each should be used. Provide opportunities for them to practise using a pair of compasses by drawing circles and arcs before progressing to circles with a specified radius or diameter. Demonstrate how to measure the required radius by aligning the compass points with a ruler to ensure accuracy. If possible, use a visualiser to model correct compass technique, emphasising the need to maintain the set distance on the compass while drawing.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Constructions and Loci_2_Accurately draw triangles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students accurately draw triangles. Students accurately draw triangles when given two angles and a side length between them (ASA), two side lengths and an angle between them (SAS) or three side lengths (SSS). Begin by modelling how to accurately draw ASA and SAS triangles with a ruler and a protractor, before modelling how to use a pair of compasses to construct an SSS triangle. Highlight that students should keep any construction lines on a completed drawing. Encourage students to measure angles and/ or sides to check their drawings are accurate. Use of a visualiser is recommended when accurately drawing triangles.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Constructions and Loci_3_Accurately draw polygons (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students refine their construction skills, using geometrical equipment to accurately draw polygons other than triangles. Remind students of properties of different shapes such as trapeziums or parallelograms, drawing attention to key features such as equal angles or parallel side lengths. Encourage students to discuss which part of a given diagram may be the best to draw first, and ensure they have strategies to check any drawings by measuring side lengths and angles. As with the previous step, the use of a visualiser is recommended to model how to accurately draw different polygons.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Constructions and Loci_4_Construct an angle bisector
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students bisect an angle using a pair of compasses, a skill that will later be applied to solving loci problems within this block. Students may have encountered constructing angle bisectors in Year 9 Explain that bisecting an angle divides it into two equal parts. Use a visualiser to model the process of constructing an angle bisector with a pair of compasses. Once students are confident with bisecting a single angle, extend their understanding by asking them to bisect angles within polygons. Encourage them to check the accuracy of their constructions by measuring the resulting angles with a protractor. Later in the block, students will connect angle bisectors to the concept of a locus that is equidistant from two lines.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Constructions and Loci_5_Construct a perpendicular bisector
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use a pair of compasses to construct the perpendicular bisector of a line segment, a skill they may have previously encountered in Year 9. This step provides an opportunity to revisit and practise the technique before applying it to loci problems. Explain that a perpendicular bisector meets a line at a 90° angle and divides it into two equal parts. Use a visualiser to demonstrate how to construct a perpendicular bisector accurately. Emphasise that the compass width should be set to more than half the length of the line segment so that the arcs intersect. Remind students that both arcs must be drawn with the same radius to ensure the line segment is bisected precisely.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Constructions and Loci_6_Construct a perpendicular from or to a point
small_step_name: Construct a perpendicular from or to a point
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students construct a perpendicular of a line passing through a specific point, which may have been previously covered in Year 9. Highlight that the shortest distance from a point to a line is along the perpendicular. Emphasise the importance of setting the pair of compasses to a distance that ensures the construction arcs intersect the line at two points, allowing for an accurate perpendicular to be drawn. Compare constructing a perpendicular bisector to constructing a bisector from a specific point on a line to highlight key differences between the two skills. Use a visualiser to model constructing intersections of a given line before drawing a perpendicular.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Constructions and Loci_7_Locus of distance from a point or a straight line
small_step_name: Locus of distance from a point or a straight line
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn that a locus is a set of points that share a common property, such as all points 4 cm from a given point. Emphasise that the locus of points a fixed distance from a point forms a circle and discuss how the wording of a question determines whether it refers to points inside, on, or outside the circle. For example, how points within the circle represent distances less than the given radius. Encourage students to explore and label these regions clearly. Students also investigate the locus of points a fixed distance from a line, and can extend this concept by constructing loci around other shapes, such as squares.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Constructions and Loci_8_Locus equidistant from two points
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of perpendicular bisectors to identify a locus equidistant from two points. Before using formal modelling, students could be encouraged to find different points that are equidistant from two different points, leading to the use of a pair of compasses to identify a relevant locus. Highlight that the locus equidistant from two points is the perpendicular bisector of the line segment formed between the two points. Use of a visualiser is recommended to model how to identify the locus equidistant from two points.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Constructions and Loci_9_Locus equidistant from two lines
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of loci by exploring points equidistant from two lines using angle bisectors. Encourage students to explore multiple points that are equidistant from the two lines forming an angle. Highlight that all points equidistant from the two lines lie along the angle bisector of the angle formed by the lines. Using a visualiser is recommended to clearly demonstrate how to construct and identify the locus. If appropriate, this step can be combined with constructing an angle bisector.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Equations and formulae_1_Solve equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This small step provides students with opportunities to consolidate learning of solving equations from Year 10. Students solve a mixture of 1- and 2-step equations, including those involving fractions. Representations such as function machines can support students with inverse operations, while bar models are useful for solving equations that have unknowns on both sides. Ensure students are confident in solving equations with fractional and/or negative solutions, and discuss strategies to check if solutions are correct. If appropriate, challenge students to solve equations with unknowns on both sides involving fractions, for example, x + 3 2 = 2x – 9 The use of calculators is encouraged, if appropriate, to focus students’ attention on applying correct operations.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Equations and formulae_2_Form and solve equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use a variety of geometry and worded contexts to form and solve 1- and 2-step equations. Remind students how to collect like terms, as this will be a key step before solving an equation. Encourage students to discuss the information they know about a given context, for example, angles in a triangle sum to 180°. Bar models can be a useful representation to support understanding of forming and solving equations. Note that students will form and solve equations with unknowns on both sides in the next extend step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Equations and formulae_3_Form and solve equations with unknowns on both sides (E)
small_step_name: Form and solve equations with unknowns on both sides (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students further develop their understanding of forming and solving equations. Ensure students are given opportunities to discuss information presented in a context, focusing on when two pieces of information are equal in value, for example, the opposite side lengths of a rectangle. Model strategies to check solutions by substituting values and evaluating both sides of a formed equation. Varied contexts can provide an opportunity to revisit topics such as angle facts. Remind students to check the question to ensure they have fully answered it, as finding the unknown may not be the final required step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Equations and formulae_4_Solve inequalities
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on prior learning from Year 10 to solve inequalities. Remind students of the differences between an equation and an inequality. Emphasise that solutions to an inequality are given as a range of values using inequality symbols. Encourage students to discuss possible values that will satisfy an inequality to ensure understanding. Students can apply similar strategies previously used when solving equations, paying careful attention to the symbol used in any given inequality. Students should be given opportunities to solve inequalities of various forms, including those with unknowns on both sides. Where appropriate, challenge students to solve compound inequalities, such as 20 < 2x < 30

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Equations and formulae_5_Form and solve inequalities (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students form and solve inequalities. Begin by encouraging students to form and solve simple 1- and 2- step inequalities before progressing to forming inequalities with unknowns on both sides. Highlight that if no unknown is specified within a question, any letter could be used to form an initial inequality. Ensure that students are also given opportunities to discuss solutions in context. For example, if n represents the number of people, then the minimum or maximum number of people should be an integer.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Equations and formulae_6_Form and solve more complex equations and inequalities (E)
small_step_name: Form and solve more complex equations and inequalities (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students solve equations and inequalities in different forms, such as equations involving fractions or compound inequalities. Support students when forming and solving compound inequalities by modelling how to combine two inequalities, for example writing 2x > 12 and 2x < 20 as 12 < 2x < 20 Provide opportunities for students to discuss when an inequality can be represented as a compound inequality due to the context. For example, if (x – 30)° represents the size of an angle that must be less than 50° this can be written as x – 30 < 50, so x < 80 However, since an angle cannot be 0° or negative, x must be greater than 30. Therefore, the complete inequality is 30 < x < 80

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Equations and formulae_7_Solve simultaneous equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This small step provides a useful opportunity to revisit solving simultaneous equations. Remind students of key strategies for isolating variables, such as subtracting or adding equations to eliminate a variable. Model examples requiring rearranging or scaling equations before elimination and encourage exploration of when addition may be more efficient than subtraction. Support students in using substitution to check their solutions, and where appropriate, challenge them to consider which problems are best solved by substitution. Note that solving simultaneous equations by substitution is an extend step in Year 10 and may be unfamiliar to some students.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Functions and graphs_1_Function machines
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit and consolidate using function machines with numerical inputs and outputs, before progressing to working with algebraic expressions in subsequent steps. This is an ideal opportunity for students to practise applying the four operations and using directed numbers. Have students build confidence by determining outputs from given inputs, then move on to finding an input from a given output using inverse operations. When ready, progress to function machines that have multiple inputs and outputs and where students need to deduce the underlying functions. Include examples featuring two-step function machines as well as squaring, cubing and rooting. At this stage, it is not necessary to explore algebraic inputs or outputs.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Functions and graphs_2_Write an expression or formula from a function machine
small_step_name: Write an expression or formula from a function machine
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of numerical function machines to write algebraic expressions using function machines. They will explore one- and two-step function machines with both numerical and algebraic inputs, outputs and functions. Highlight the importance of the order of two-step function machines using an algebraic input, for example, × 3, + 2 and how it differs from + 2, × 3 in producing the expressions 3x + 2 and 3(x + 2) respectively. Give students the opportunity to find inputs for given functions and outputs and explore the links to rearranging formulae. As confidence builds, progress to more complex scenarios involving multiple inputs and outputs, where students must deduce the underlying functions.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Functions and graphs_3_Quadratic graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of quadratic graphs. Students will use completed tables to generate and plot coordinate pairs for quadratic functions. When plotting, model how to draw a smooth curve through the points rather than connecting them with straight lines. Progress to identifying turning points from graphs and reading or estimating roots. Begin with simple quadratic functions such as y = x² or y = x² + 1 and guide students to the solutions to a quadratic equation, for example x² – 5 = 0 being the points where the graph y = x² – 5 crosses the x-axis. Once students are confident, introduce the concept of plotting an additional straight line on a graph to estimate solutions to a new equation, for example, drawing the line y = 4 to solve x² + 3x + 2 = 4

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Functions and graphs_4_Cubic graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of cubic graphs. Begin by recapping drawing graphs by completing tables of values. Draw attention to the solutions of a cubic equation being the points where the graph crosses the x-axis. Remind students the x-axis is also the line y = 0. Next, guide students to identify turning points and points of inflection by examining changes in the gradient. Once students are confident, introduce the concept of plotting an additional straight line on a cubic graph to estimate solutions to a new equation, for example, drawing the line y = 5 to solve x3 + x + 2 = 5. In this foundation step, the additional lines are all of the form y = a. Dynamic graphing software can be useful to support student understanding.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Functions and graphs_5_Reciprocal graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore graphs of reciprocal functions. Dynamic graphing software can be helpful to support understanding. Allow students to explore reciprocal functions using calculators, then discuss why division by zero is undefined and what this means for the graph. Draw students’ attention to the behaviour of the graph as the values of x become very large or very small. Highlight the presence of vertical and horizontal asymptotes. Use this as an opportunity to introduce the concepts of positive and negative infinity, explaining how the graph approaches but never touches these lines. Provide students with opportunities to complete tables of values to plot reciprocal graphs. Include graphs of negative reciprocal functions and discuss how these differ from positive reciprocal graphs. Extend understanding from previous steps by drawing additional lines to estimate values of x given a value of y and vice versa.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Functions and graphs_6_Recognise and sketch graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of linear, quadratic, cubic and reciprocal graphs to recognise graph shapes and match them to equations. Discuss the shapes of the different graphs, making reference to key features such as turning points, asymptotes and straight lines versus curves. Explore simple changes to graphs by looking at the effect of adding or subtracting constants, for example, comparing y = x and y = x + 2 Provide opportunities for students to investigate the difference between negative and positive graphs, for example, y = x2 and y = –x2. Students may need to be reminded of positive and negative gradients as linear graphs were last encountered in Year 10. In this foundation step, it is not necessary to explicitly discuss transformations of graphs.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Rates_1_Convert between hours and minutes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This small step provides the opportunity for students to work with measures of time that are not an integer number of hours. This will support students when calculating with speed, distance and time later in the block. Remind students of simple facts related to time, for example half an hour is 30 minutes. Show students how to convert decimal hours to minutes, for example 2.5 hours = 150 minutes because 2.5 × 60 = 150. Students should also be able to convert minutes to hours by considering the inverse of this process, for example 150 ÷ 60 = 2.5 Provide students with strategies to represent hours and minutes in minutes, for example multiplying the number of hours by 60, then adding the number of minutes to the result, again ensuring that they can do this process in reverse. Calculator use is encouraged and if appropriate, students could explore calculator functions that convert units of time.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Rates_2_Speed, distance and time
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and build upon their understanding of problems involving speed, distance and time covered in Key Stage 3. Remind students that units of speed represent the distance covered for each unit of time, for example “miles per hour”. Students can be provided with formulae linking speed, distance and time, but should be encouraged to use a logical approach to solve problems related to speed, distance and time. For example, if the time is doubled, then the distance covered will also double if the speed is constant. Students could also inspect units given in a problem to decide on a strategy to solve it. For example, a speed in m/s can be calculated by dividing the distance (metres) by the time (seconds).

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Rates_3_Interpret distance-time graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students link their understanding of speed, distance and time, by interpreting distance–time graphs. Ensure that students are confident with interpreting the gradient of line segments before interpreting speed from distance–time graphs. Remind students that on a distance–time graph, the horizontal axis represents time, and the vertical axis represents distance. Give students plenty of opportunity to identify key features of the graphs, such as a horizontal line segment representing where an object is stationary, or a diagonal line segment representing an object moving at a constant speed. Use of a calculator is recommended to allow students to focus on distance–time graphs rather than the arithmetic.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Rates_4_Draw distance-time graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding from the previous step to draw distance–time graphs. It may be useful to recap how to interpret key points on a completed distance–time graph first, for example the distance travelled, time taken, or how long a “rest” was. Begin by providing students with information to draw a distance–time graph, for example “a car travelled 40 miles in 1 hour”. Students could also work out distances or times when given information about the speed, for example “a cyclist travels at a speed of 50 km/h for 30 minutes”. Throughout this step, students should be encouraged to both draw and interpret different pieces of information related to distance–time graphs, including calculating speed. Practice should include distance–time graphs for both single and return journeys.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Rates_5_Density and pressure
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of density, as well as formally studying pressure in maths for the first time. Remind students how to calculate density (d), mass (m) or volume (V) using the formula d m V = . Ensure students understand the units used to measure pressure (for example, N/cm2) and that this is calculated by dividing the force exerted by an object on a surface, by the area of the object in contact with the surface. As students become more confident, introduce problems such as calculating the volume of an object before calculating the density. Where appropriate, challenge students to consider the total density of a combination of two materials with different densities.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Rates_6_Convert compound units (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn how to convert compound units of measure, such as converting speeds in metres per second to kilometres per hour. Ensure students are confident in converting units of length, for example metres to kilometres, and units of time, for example hours to seconds, before converting compound units of measure. Model how to convert compound units of measure in stages, for example converting from metres per second to metres per minute, then converting this to metres per hour. Encourage students to reason whether their answers are reasonable, for example if a speed is given in metres per second. Students could also explore converting speeds in mph to km/h.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Transformations_1_Enlargement (positive scale factor)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students enlarge shapes using positive integer scale factors, building on their understanding of similarity. Start with enlargements on a grid and highlight that scale factors greater than 1 produce larger images. As students progress, introduce enlargements with a given centre of enlargement. Encourage students to count distances from the centre of enlargement to each vertex of the shape and multiply it by the scale factor to determine the corresponding vertices of the image. Provide practice using centres that are not at the origin. Students should also work out the scale factor of an enlargement, and identify the centre of enlargement by drawing ray lines through the corresponding vertices of the object and image. The point where the ray lines meet is the centre of enlargement. Encourage students to use a ruler for accuracy and extend lines beyond the image if necessary.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Transformations_2_Enlargement (fractional scale factor)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of enlargements, to work with scale factors smaller than 1 Introduce positive fractional scale factors that are between 0 and 1 to show how these scale factors create reduced images. Emphasise that distances from the centre of enlargement are still multiplied by the scale factor, but the image moves closer to the centre of enlargement and becomes smaller. This step focuses on unit fractional scale factors only, such as 1 2 or 1 3 Progress to using centres of enlargement given as coordinates, including those away from the origin. Students should also determine fractional scale factors from given enlargements and identify centres of enlargement using ray lines.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Transformations_3_Line symmetry and rotational symmetry
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of rotational symmetry and lines of symmetry, reinforcing their prior knowledge from Year 8. They revisit how to determine the order of rotational symmetry, recognising that all shapes have rotational symmetry of at least order 1. Tracing paper can be used to support students to identify the order of rotational symmetry. Review line symmetry by identifying single lines of symmetry such as horizontal, vertical or diagonal lines before progressing to shapes with multiple lines of symmetry. Students could use practical methods such as folding paper shapes to check their answers. Comparing rotational and line symmetry helps students identify key differences, including that the number of lines of symmetry does not always match the order of rotational symmetry.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Transformations_4_Reflection
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their Key Stage 3 understanding of reflection, by reflecting shapes in mirror lines and accurately describing the transformation. Recap reflections in a drawn mirror line, emphasising that the object and image are congruent, and that each vertex is equidistant from the line of reflection. Once students are confident reflecting in drawn lines, introduce reflections defined by an equation. Remind students how to draw the equations of vertical, horizontal and diagonal lines such as y = x or y = –x. For diagonal reflections, model rotating the diagram 45° so that the mirror line becomes vertical or horizontal. Students should also be able to describe a reflection by identifying the line of reflection through corresponding points, finding their midpoints and stating the equation of the line.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Transformations_5_Rotation
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit their Year 9 understanding of rotation by rotating shapes about a given centre and accurately describing the transformation. Recap rotations of 90°, 180° and 270° clockwise and anti-clockwise about a marked centre, highlighting that the object and image remain congruent while orientation changes. Once students are secure rotating shapes about a marked centre of rotation on a grid, introduce rotations about the origin and other coordinates. Encourage students to mark the centre and use 90° increments to track movement, using tracing paper to support. Explore rotating the same shape from different centres, such as inside the shape or on a vertex, to observe how the image’s position changes. Students should also be able to describe a rotation fully by identifying the centre, angle and direction when comparing the object and image.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Transformations_6_Translation
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students recap their Year 9 learning of translations by translating shapes and describing translations using column vectors. Begin by describing column vectors in words, before applying the column vector to the concept of translation. Emphasise that a translation involves moving a shape in a specific direction without changing its size, shape or orientation. Draw students’ attention to the importance of translating each vertex of the shape, highlighting that the vector of translation is determined by the distance between corresponding vertices, not the distance between the shapes themselves. Explain that students must specify the movement in both the horizontal and vertical directions to describe a translation fully, and remind them of the importance of the positive and negative signs in column vectors.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Transformations_7_Describe a transformation
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn to describe any transformation accurately, building on their learning from previous steps. Students begin by identifying the type of transformation: translation, reflection, rotation or enlargement. Encourage students to observe the differences between the object and the image, and consider whether the shape is in the same orientation (translation), mirrored (reflection), rotated (rotation) or changed size (enlargement). Students should focus on key properties such as size and orientation, to rule out incorrect options. Tracing paper can be helpful for physically testing transformations. Stem sentences can also support students in structuring their responses.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Foundation_Transformations_8_Find the result of a series of transformations (E)
small_step_name: Find the result of a series of transformations (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn to describe a series of transformations as a single transformation. Begin by having students perform two successive transformations, such as a rotation followed by a reflection, and ask them to compare the original and final shapes. Encourage them to look for a direct link between the start and end positions, identifying whether the combined effect is equivalent to a single transformation. Discuss common patterns, such as two successive reflections across parallel lines being equivalent to a translation, or two rotations around the same point combining into a single rotation. Stem sentences can be useful to support structured explanations. Encourage students to justify their reasoning and test their ideas by reversing transformations to check their conclusions.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Angles bearings and trigonometry_1_Understand and represent bearings
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to bearings as a specific type of angle, always measured clockwise from north and expressed using three figures. Use real-world contexts such as giving directions, map reading or aviation problems to illustrate the concept. Provide both examples and non-examples to deepen understanding. Highlight the wording “bearing of A from B”, as this can often cause confusion, and emphasise identifying the angle carefully with a range of start and end points. Discuss the convention that the north line is typically drawn vertically up the page, regardless of the actual direction of north. Throughout this step, make regular connections to angles within compass points.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Angles bearings and trigonometry_2_Measure and read bearings
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn how to measure and read bearings in a variety of problem-solving contexts with a focus on interpreting diagrams and using protractors. Students may need to refer to the rules of bearings from the previous step throughout when practising this new skill. Ensure that students are proficient in using a protractor to measure angles before this step. Include examples of bearings that are greater than 180° and those whereby auxiliary lines need to be drawn to indicate north. Students may need support in identifying which bearing to measure depending on the wording of the questions. For example, “the bearing of A from B” and “the bearing of B from A”. A visualiser is useful for modelling and to support students’ understanding.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Angles bearings and trigonometry_3_Scale drawings using bearings
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn how to measure and read bearings in practical situations and tackle more complex problems that involve drawing scale diagrams. To encourage precise use of a protractor, it may be helpful to avoid square paper. Students should be confident with scale drawings and able to interpret scales presented in different forms, such as 1 cm represents 2 m or 1 : 200 Emphasise the importance of accuracy, with students producing neat, clearly labelled diagrams using a ruler and protractor where required. As in previous steps, a visualiser is useful for modelling and to support students’ understanding.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Angles bearings and trigonometry_4_Calculate bearings using angles rules
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students compare multiple methods of problem solving with bearings by using their prior knowledge of angle rules and facts. Students may need reminding that north lines are parallel. Therefore, they should be encouraged to draw auxiliary lines so that the rules for angles in parallel lines can be used. Emphasise the importance of reading the question carefully, noting where to measure the bearing from. Encourage students to annotate diagrams, adding known bearings, unknown angles and reasoning as they work.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Angles bearings and trigonometry_5_Choose appropriate methods to solve problems with right-angled triangles
small_step_name: Choose appropriate methods to solve problems with right-angled triangles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit prior learning on mathematical rules within right-angled triangles. Students may need to be reminded of the formulae for Pythagoras’ theorem and the trigonometric ratios. Provide students with a range of mixed questions, where they will need to identify which method is the most appropriate. Emphasise to students that, in some cases, either method can be used. Challenge students further with multi-step problems requiring them to perform trigonometric ratio calculations more than once to work out an unknown value. Remind students to ensure their calculators are set to degrees when calculating with trigonometric ratios.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Angles bearings and trigonometry_6_Solve problems with bearings using Pythagoras and trigonometry
small_step_name: Solve problems with bearings using Pythagoras and trigonometry
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use their prior knowledge of Pythagoras’ theorem and trigonometry to solve problems with bearings. Students may need to draw north lines and auxiliary lines on diagrams to form right-angled triangles. Students should identify right-angled triangles within diagrams and label all relevant information to help them identify which method is the most appropriate and efficient. Encourage students to form diagrams from worded questions. Challenge students further through multi-step problems, whereby trigonometric ratios should be used more than once to work out unknown values. Remind students to ensure their calculators are set to degrees when calculating trigonometric ratios.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Angles bearings and trigonometry_7_Sine and cosine rules
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit prior learning on sine and cosine rules. It may be useful to look at each rule in isolation before deciding which rule is appropriate. Allow time for fluency practice of each variation before giving students mixed questions where they need to identify the appropriate rule to work out unknown information. Students can then be challenged with problem-solving and reasoning questions. Ensure students correctly label diagrams and draw representations when a diagram is not provided. Remind students to ensure their calculators are set to degrees mode for their trigonometric calculations.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Angles bearings and trigonometry_8_Solve problems with bearings using the sine and cosine rules (E)
small_step_name: Solve problems with bearings using the sine and cosine rules (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use their prior knowledge of the sine and cosine rules to solve problems with bearings. Students may need to draw north lines and auxiliary lines on diagrams to form triangles. After using sine or cosine rules to find an interior angle, explicitly show students how to turn that into a bearing. Remind students to label diagrams with relevant information to help them identify which method is the most appropriate and efficient. Encourage students to form diagrams from worded questions. Challenge students further through multi-step problems whereby the sine and cosine rules may be used more than once to work out unknown values. Remind students to ensure their calculators are set to degrees when calculating with trigonometric functions.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Constructions and Loci_1_Accurately draw triangles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students accurately draw triangles. Students accurately draw triangles when given two angles and a side length between them (ASA), two side lengths and an angle between them (SAS) or three side lengths (SSS). Begin by modelling how to accurately draw ASA and SAS triangles with a ruler and a protractor, before modelling how to use a pair of compasses to construct an SSS triangle. Highlight that students should keep any construction lines on a completed drawing. Encourage students to measure angles and/ or sides to check their drawings are accurate.
