Use of a visualiser is recommended when accurately drawing triangles.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Constructions and Loci_2_Accurately draw polygons
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students refine their construction skills, using geometrical equipment to accurately draw polygons other than triangles. Remind students of properties of different shapes such as trapeziums or parallelograms, drawing attention to key features such as equal angles or parallel side lengths. Encourage students to discuss which part of a given diagram may be the best to draw first, and ensure they have strategies to check any drawings by measuring side lengths and angles. As with the previous step, the use of a visualiser is recommended to model how to accurately draw different polygons.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Constructions and Loci_3_Construct an angle bisector and a perpendicular bisector
small_step_name: Construct an angle bisector and a perpendicular bisector
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use a pair of compasses to construct angle bisectors and perpendicular bisectors, revisiting skills they may have previously covered in Year 9 Explain that an angle bisector divides an angle into two equal parts, and model the process using a visualiser before students practise on single angles and angles within polygons. Encourage them to check accuracy with a protractor and later connect this to loci problems that are equidistant from two lines. Remind students that a perpendicular bisector meets a line at 90° and divides it equally. Demonstrate the method, highlighting that the compass radius must be set to more than half the line length, and kept consistent for both arcs to ensure accuracy.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Constructions and Loci_4_Construct a perpendicular from or to a point
small_step_name: Construct a perpendicular from or to a point
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students construct a perpendicular of a line passing through a specific point, which may have been previously covered in Year 9. Highlight that the shortest distance from a point to a line is along the perpendicular. Emphasise the importance of setting the pair of compasses to a distance that ensures the construction arcs intersect the line at two points, allowing for an accurate perpendicular to be drawn. Compare constructing a perpendicular bisector to constructing a bisector from a specific point on a line to highlight key differences between the two skills. Use a visualiser to model constructing intersections of a given line before drawing a perpendicular.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Constructions and Loci_5_Locus of distance from a point or a straight line
small_step_name: Locus of distance from a point or a straight line
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn that a locus is a set of points that share a common property, such as all points 4 cm from a given point. Emphasise that the locus of points a fixed distance from a point forms a circle and discuss how the wording of a question determines whether it refers to points inside, on, or outside the circle. For example, how points within the circle represent distances less than the given radius. Encourage students to explore and label these regions clearly. Students also investigate the locus of points a fixed distance from a line, and can extend this concept by constructing loci around other shapes, such as squares.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Constructions and Loci_6_Locus equidistant from two points
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of perpendicular bisectors to identify a locus equidistant from two points. Before using formal modelling, students could be encouraged to find different points that are equidistant from two different points, leading to the use of a pair of compasses to identify a relevant locus. Highlight that the locus equidistant from two points is the perpendicular bisector of the line segment formed between the two points. Use of a visualiser is recommended to model how to identify the locus equidistant from two points.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Constructions and Loci_7_Locus equidistant from two lines
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of loci by exploring points equidistant from two lines using angle bisectors. Encourage students to explore multiple points that are equidistant from the two lines forming an angle. Highlight that all points equidistant from the two lines lie along the angle bisector of the angle formed by the lines. Using a visualiser is recommended to clearly demonstrate how to construct and identify the locus. If appropriate, this step can be combined with constructing an angle bisector.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Constructions and Loci_8_Solve loci problems
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply the skills they have developed throughout the block to solve loci problems. Provide a range of problems to help them refine their decision-making when creating constructions. Include tasks that combine previous constructions and involve drawing to scale. “Spot the mistake” activities are useful for encouraging discussion, identifying misconceptions, and reinforcing correct methods. Emphasise the importance of leaving construction lines visible. As in earlier steps, using a visualiser is recommended to model the process of solving loci problems.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Equations and formulae_10_Solve a pair of simultaneous equations involving algebraic constants for a third unknown (E)
small_step_name: Solve a pair of simultaneous equations involving algebraic constants for a third unknown (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students solve simultaneous equations consisting of one linear and one non-linear equation. Note that this may have been covered as an extend step in Year 10 Before attempting to solve simultaneous equations of this form, ensure students are confident in expanding quadratic expressions and solving quadratic equations. Dynamic graphing software is recommended to show how intersections of linear and non- linear graphs represent solution pairs, before moving to algebraic methods. Model substituting an expression from a linear equation into a non-linear equation and rearrange it into a familiar form. To build confidence, begin with linear equations that do not need to be rearranged, before moving on to those that do. For example, providing two equations with y as the subject first.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Equations and formulae_1_Form and solve equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use a variety of geometry and worded contexts to form and solve 1- and 2-step equations. Remind students how to collect like terms, as this will be a key step before solving an equation. Encourage students to discuss the information they know about a given context, for example, angles in a triangle sum to 180°. Bar models can be a useful representation to support understanding of forming and solving equations. Note that students will form and solve equations with unknowns on both sides in the next extend step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Equations and formulae_2_Form and solve inequalities
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students form and solve inequalities. Begin by encouraging students to form and solve simple 1- and 2- step inequalities before progressing to forming inequalities with unknowns on both sides. Highlight that if no unknown is specified within a question, any letter could be used to form an initial inequality. Ensure that students are also given opportunities to discuss solutions in context. For example, if n represents the number of people, then the minimum or maximum number of people should be an integer.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Equations and formulae_3_Form and solve equations and inequalities with unknowns on both sides
small_step_name: Form and solve equations and inequalities with unknowns on both sides
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students further develop their understanding of forming and solving equations and inequalities with unknowns on both sides. Provide opportunities to discuss contextual problems where two expressions are either equal or have a defined relationship, for example, where one angle is greater than another. Model strategies to check solutions by substituting values and evaluating both sides of an equation or inequality, emphasising the impact of inequality symbols on solution sets. Students will form and solve more complex equations and inequalities, including fractional and compound, in the following extend step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Equations and formulae_4_Form and solve more complex equations and inequalities (E)
small_step_name: Form and solve more complex equations and inequalities (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students solve equations and inequalities in different forms, such as equations involving fractions or compound inequalities. Support students when forming and solving compound inequalities by modelling how to combine two inequalities, for example writing 2x > 12 and 2x < 20 as 12 < 2x < 20 Provide opportunities for students to discuss when inqualities can be represented as a compound inequality due to the context. For example, if (x – 30)° represents the size of an angle that must be less than 50°, this can be written as x – 30 < 50, so x < 80 However, since an angle cannot be 0° or negative, x must be greater than 30. So the complete inequality would be 30 < x < 80

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Equations and formulae_5_Quadratic equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of solving quadratic equations introduced in Year 10. Begin by revisiting equations of the form (x + a)(x + b) = 0, emphasising that to solve the equation, either bracket can be equal to zero. Include a variety of equations such as those involving the difference of squares, perfect squares, equations that need to be rearranged and expressions that factorise into a single bracket. Students should also be given the opportunity to solve equations with a coefficient of x2 greater than 1, such as 2x2 + 7x + 3 Provide opportunities to use the quadratic formula, as well as solving by completing the square, which may have been introduced as an extend step in Year 10. Encourage students to make links to graphical representations of equations.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Equations and formulae_6_Quadratic inequalities (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the links between quadratic inequalities and graphs of quadratic functions in order to solve quadratic inequalities. Remind students that the roots of a quadratic are the solutions to the quadratic equation, and the points on its graph where y = 0 Dynamic graphing software can be useful to highlight the parts of a quadratic graph where the equation is less than or greater than 0, and to make links between graphical representations and solution sets for quadratic inequalities. From here, students can begin to make generalisations. For example, when an inequality is in the form x2 + bx + c < 0 or x2 + bx + c ≤ 0, then the solution set will be represented with a compound inequality. Students should be encouraged to sketch graphs, identifying the roots throughout this step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Equations and formulae_7_Understand iterative processes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore iterative processes and the notation used to describe them. Linking iterative processes to sequences will help students understand the meaning of the notation that is commonly used, such as Un, Un + 1 or U1 Introduce students to descriptions of rules for sequences using notation and discuss their meanings, for example, Un + 1 = Un + 5 means “the next term of the sequence is generated by adding 5 to the previous term.” Once students are confident interpreting notation, they could explore other problems involving repeated percentage change, growth or decay. Students will estimate solutions to equations by iteration in the next step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Equations and formulae_8_Solve simultaneous equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding from the previous step to estimate solutions to equations by iteration. Use graphs to show students that a change of sign will verify that a root lies between two given values, as it indicates the graph has intersected the x-axis. Model how to find estimates for solutions, highlighting the use of the “ANS” function to retrieve the previous answer on a calculator. Students will also be required to rearrange formulae in this step. Be mindful that students may focus on changing the subject of the formula instead of correctly rearranging to create an iterative formula, such as writing x2 + 2x – 1 = 0 as x = x 1 – 2

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Equations and formulae_9_Solve simultaneous equations (one linear, one non-linear) (E)
small_step_name: Solve simultaneous equations (one linear, one non-linear) (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This small step provides a useful opportunity to revisit solving simultaneous equations. Remind students of strategies for isolating variables, such as subtracting one equation from another to eliminate a variable. Model solving pairs of simultaneous equations where one or both equations need to be rearranged or scaled before eliminating a variable. Provide opportunities for students to explore when adding equations might be more efficient than subtraction. Students should also solve simultaneous equations where substitution is the most effective method, and they should be given opportunities to form and solve simultaneous equations. Support students in using substitution to check their solutions.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_10_Reflection of a graph
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore reflections of graphs and make generalisations. Encourage understanding of why –f(x) is a reflection in the x-axis and f(–x) is a reflection in the y-axis, rather than relying on students memorising rules. This can be done initially by students observing patterns with individual points, for example comparing (–1, 3) with (1, 3) shows that negating the value of x causes symmetry through the y-axis. Dynamic graphing software can be useful to support understanding. Students should be able to sketch a graph mapped by the transformation, as well as describe the transformation. Exploring functions that are symmetrical in the x-axis or y-axis is also useful. It is not necessary to introduce transformations of the form f(–ax) or –af(x) within this step.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_11_More complex transformations of the graph of a function (E)
small_step_name: More complex transformations of the graph of a function (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore more complex transformations of the graph of a function. Combine translations to give functions of the form y = f(x + a) + b, allowing students to both sketch graphs and explore the effects on coordinates of points. Then introduce functions such as y = –f(x + a) in which reflections are combined with translations. Apply combined transformations to graphs of trigonometric functions. If appropriate, introduce transformations of the form y = af(x) and how the graph “stretches” vertically by scale factor a. Give students the opportunity to deduce transformations from coordinate mappings. Dynamic graphing software is useful to support student understanding.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_1_Function notation
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students encounter formal function notation for the first time. Encourage students to use precise mathematical language when describing functions, for example, reading f(x) as “f of x”. Build on their existing understanding of substitution by drawing links between evaluating expressions and using function notation, for example, finding the value of x + 4 when x = 3 versus finding the value of f(3) when f(x) = x + 4. Use a variety of letters to represent different functions, like g(x) or h(x), within questions to reinforce the general concept. Once students are confident evaluating functions for given values of x, progress to solving equations written in function notation. Introduce the idea of equating two functions to solve such equations.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_2_Composite functions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to composite functions. Begin by using numerical inputs, for example, finding fg(3). Break down these problems by first calculating g(3) and substituting that result into the function f. Use these numerical examples to build a strong conceptual understanding that fg(x) means that the output of g(x) is the input of the function f. Encourage the use of precise mathematical language with students reading fg(x) as “f of g of x”. Take time to explore the difference between fg(x) and gf(x), as understanding the order of composition is essential. Ensure students are given the opportunity to work with repeated functions such as ff(x). Once students are confident with evaluating composite functions, extend their understanding by introducing questions that require them to solve equations involving composite functions.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_3_Inverse functions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to inverse functions. It may be helpful to review changing the subject prior to this step, as this skill underpins the concept of finding an inverse function. Begin with simple functions, for example, those of the form f(x) = x + 2, making explicit links to inverse operations. Working backwards through function machines can be used to support understanding. Clearly distinguish between the reciprocal x–1 and the notation for an inverse function f–1(x). Provide opportunities for students to solve equations involving inverse functions. Once confidence is built, problems that combine inverse and composite functions should be explored, including those that lead to simultaneous equations.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_4_Quadratic and cubic graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of quadratic and cubic graphs. Begin by revisiting how to complete tables of values in order to plot these graphs accurately. Remind students that additional lines can be drawn on graphs to estimate solutions to equations. Include examples where the equation of the extra line must be extracted from a new equation. Explore turning points, asking students to read them from graphs. Explore using given coordinates of turning points in combination with knowledge of completing the square to write equations of quadratics. Similarly, reinforce how the roots of a quadratic function can be used to determine its equation. Dynamic graphing software can be useful to support understanding.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_5_Reciprocal graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore graphs of reciprocal functions. Dynamic graphing software can be helpful to support understanding. Allow students to explore reciprocal functions using calculators, then discuss why division by zero is undefined and what this means for the graph. Draw students’ attention to the behaviour of the graph as the values of x become very large or very small. Highlight the presence of vertical and horizontal asymptotes. Use this as an opportunity to introduce the concepts of positive and negative infinity, explaining how the graph approaches but never touches these lines. Provide students with opportunities to complete tables of values to plot reciprocal graphs. Include graphs of negative reciprocal functions and discuss how these differ from positive reciprocal graphs. Extend understanding from previous steps by drawing additional lines to estimate values of x given a value of y and vice versa.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_6_Exponential graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their knowledge of drawing graphs and growth by plotting and interpreting exponential graphs. Students may need to revisit negative powers and powers of fractions prior to this step. Introduce students to exponential graphs, drawing particular attention to common features such as the overall shape and the y-intercept at (0, 1) for graphs of the form y = px. Begin by completing tables of values to plot graphs and using additional lines to estimate values of x or y. Progress to using simultaneous equations to find the values of a and b in the equation y = abx, given two coordinates that lie on the curve. This step can be extended to allow students to make links between exponential graphs and contextual growth and decay problems. Dynamic graphing software can be useful to support student understanding.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_7_Trigonometric graphs (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students learn how to recognise, draw and use graphs of the trigonometric functions y = sin (x), y = cos (x) and y = tan (x). These graphs will be new to students, so it may be useful to begin with plotting and sketching these from a table of values. Spend some time discussing the shape of the graphs and recognise key coordinates for each one. Students can then investigate the properties of the asymptote in y = tan (x). Once students are confident recognising different trigonometric graphs, they can start to use them to read off values and solve trigonometric equations. Ensure that students realise that the graphs are periodic and repeat an infinite number of times. Encourage students to use the symmetry of the graphs to work out different values of x that give the same values of y.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_8_Recognise and sketch graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use their knowledge of linear, quadratic, cubic and reciprocal graphs to recognise graph shapes and match them to equations. Discuss the shapes of the different graphs, making reference to key features such as turning points, asymptotes and straight lines versus curves. Explore simple changes to graphs by looking at the effect of adding or subtracting constants, for example, comparing y = x and y = x + 2 Provide opportunities for students to investigate the difference between negative and positive graphs, for example, y = x2 and y = –x2. Students may need to be reminded of positive and negative gradients as linear graphs were last encountered in Year 10. In this step, it is not necessary to explicitly discuss transformations of graphs.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Functions and graphs_9_Translation of a graph
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore translations of graphs and make generalisations. Begin by introducing vertical translations, discussing both the impact on the position of the overall graph and on the individual points. Allow students to observe that y = f(x) + a translates y = f(x) by the vector 0 a   . Next, progress to translations of the form y = f(x + a). Highlight the difference in the impact on the coordinates of points between, for example, y = f(x + 1) and y = f(x) + 1 Translations of trigonometric graphs are also explored within this step. Dynamic graphing software is useful to support students in making connections. Combined translations can also be explored, for example, y = f(x + a) – b.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Rates_1_Speed, distance and time
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and build upon their understanding of problems involving speed, distance and time covered in Key Stage 3. Remind students that units of speed represent the distance covered for each unit of time, for example “miles per hour”. Students can be provided with formulae linking speed, distance and time, but should be encouraged to use a logical approach to solve problems related to speed, distance and time. For example, if the time is doubled, then the distance covered will also double if the speed is constant. Students could also inspect units given in a problem to decide on a strategy to solve it. For example, a speed in m/s can be calculated by dividing the distance (metres) by the time (seconds).

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Rates_2_Distance-time graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students link their understanding of speed, distance and time, by drawing and interpreting distance–time graphs. Provide opportunities for students to identify and discuss key features of the graphs, such as a horizontal line segment representing where an object is stationary, or a diagonal line segment representing an object moving at a constant speed. When modelling how to draw a distance–time graph, provide information related to time and distance, for example “a car travelled 40 miles in 1 hour”, before moving on to problems involving speed to work out a time/distance, for example “a cyclist travels at a speed of 50 km/h for 30 minutes”. If appropriate, students could calculate the average speed of a journey whilst moving, by considering the total distance travelled and the total time taken whilst moving.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Rates_3_Velocity-time graphs (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students interpret velocity–time or speed– time graphs. Ensure that students understand that velocity is a measure of speed in a given direction, whereas speed does not indicate the direction. Students will learn that the gradient at a specific time on a velocity–time graph represents a change in velocity (acceleration). Begin with graphs involving only straight lines, highlighting that positive and negative gradients represent constant acceleration and deceleration, respectively. For curved graphs, model how to draw and calculate the gradient of a tangent at a point, discussing why the accuracy of a tangent may affect answers. Note that students will only look at motion in a single direction, therefore “velocity” and “speed” can be used interchangeably.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Rates_4_Density and pressure
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of density, as well as formally studying pressure in maths for the first time. Remind students how to calculate density (d), mass (m) or volume (V) using the formula d m V = . Ensure students understand the units used to measure pressure (for example, N/cm2) and that this is calculated by dividing the force exerted by an object on a surface, by the area of the object in contact with the surface. As students become more confident, introduce problems such as calculating the volume of an object before calculating the density. Where appropriate, challenge students to consider the total density of a combination of two materials with different densities.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Rates_5_Convert compound units
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn how to convert compound units of measure, such as converting speeds in metres per second to kilometres per hour. Ensure students are confident in converting units of length, for example metres to kilometres, and units of time, for example hours to seconds, before converting compound units of measure. Model how to convert compound units of measure in stages, for example converting from metres per second to metres per minute, then converting this to metres per hour. Encourage students to reason whether their answers are reasonable, for example if a speed is given in metres per second. Students could also explore converting speeds in mph to km/h.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Transformations_1_Enlargement (positive scale factor)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students enlarge shapes using positive integer scale factors, building on their understanding of similarity. Start with enlargements on a grid and highlight that scale factors greater than 1 produce larger images. As students progress, introduce enlargements with a given centre of enlargement. Encourage students to count distances from the centre of enlargement to each vertex of the shape and multiply it by the scale factor to determine the corresponding vertices of the image. Provide practice using centres that are not at the origin. Students should also work out the scale factor of an enlargement, and identify the centre of enlargement by drawing ray lines through the corresponding vertices of the object and image. The point where the ray lines meet is the centre of enlargement. Encourage students to use a ruler for accuracy and extend lines beyond the image if necessary.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Transformations_2_Enlargement (fractional scale factor)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of enlargements, to work with positive fractional scale factors. Introduce positive fractional scale factors that are between 0 and 1, to show how these scale factors create reduced images. Emphasise that distances from the centre of enlargement are still multiplied by the scale factor, but the image moves closer to the centre of enlargement and becomes smaller. This step will initially begin with unit fractional scale factors before progressing to non-unit fractions and those greater than 1 Progress to using centres of enlargement given as coordinates, including those away from the origin. Students should also determine fractional scale factors from given enlargements and identify centres of enlargement using ray lines.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Transformations_3_Enlargement (negative scale factor)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of enlargement by using negative scale factors. Begin with scale factors of –2 and –3, with a marked centre of enlargement on a grid. Guide them to count distances from the centre to each vertex, multiplying these distances by the scale factor to locate the corresponding vertices of the image. Explain to students that, unlike positive enlargements, applying a negative scale factor means the distance from the centre of enlargement is measured in the opposite direction. Highlight that a negative scale factor rotates the image 180° while also changing its size. As students develop, encourage them to apply this concept on a coordinate grid, where the centre of enlargement is given as a pair of coordinates. To deepen understanding, ensure students practise enlargements from various centres, including those away from the origin.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Transformations_4_Reflection
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their Key Stage 3 understanding of reflection, by reflecting shapes in mirror lines and accurately describing the transformation. Recap reflections in a drawn mirror line, emphasising that the object and image are congruent, and that each vertex is equidistant from the line of reflection. Once students are confident reflecting in drawn lines, introduce reflections defined by an equation. Remind students how to draw the equations of vertical, horizontal and diagonal lines such as y = x or y = –x. For diagonal reflections, model rotating the diagram 45° so that the mirror line becomes vertical or horizontal. Students should also be able to describe a reflection by identifying the line of reflection through corresponding points, finding their midpoints and stating the equation of the line.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Transformations_5_Rotation
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit their Year 9 understanding of rotation by rotating shapes about a given centre and accurately describing the transformation. Recap rotations of 90°, 180° and 270° clockwise and anti-clockwise about a marked centre, highlighting that the object and image remain congruent while orientation changes. Once students are secure rotating shapes about a marked centre of rotation on a grid, introduce rotations about the origin and other coordinates. Encourage students to mark the centre and use 90° increments to track movement, using tracing paper to support. Explore rotating the same shape from different centres, such as inside the shape or on a vertex, to observe how the image’s position changes. Students should also be able to describe a rotation fully by identifying the centre, angle and direction when comparing the object and image.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Transformations_6_Translation
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students recap their Year 9 learning of translations by translating shapes and describing translations using column vectors. Begin by describing column vectors in words, before applying the column vector to the concept of translation. Emphasise that a translation involves moving a shape in a specific direction without changing its size, shape or orientation. Draw students’ attention to the importance of translating each vertex of the shape, highlighting that the vector of translation is determined by the distance between corresponding vertices, not the distance between the shapes themselves. Explain that students must specify the movement in both the horizontal and vertical directions to describe a translation fully, and remind them of the importance of the positive and negative signs in column vectors.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Transformations_7_Describe a transformation
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn to describe any transformation accurately, building on their learning from previous steps. Students begin by identifying the type of transformation: translation, reflection, rotation or enlargement. Encourage students to observe the differences between the object and the image, and consider whether the shape is in the same orientation (translation), mirrored (reflection), rotated (rotation) or changed size (enlargement). Students should focus on key properties such as size and orientation, to rule out incorrect options. Tracing paper can be helpful for physically testing transformations. Stem sentences can also support students in structuring their responses.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Transformations_8_Find the result of a series of transformations
small_step_name: Find the result of a series of transformations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn to describe a series of transformations as a single transformation. Begin by having students perform two successive transformations, such as a rotation followed by a reflection, and ask them to compare the original and final shapes. Encourage them to look for a direct link between the start and end positions, identifying whether the combined effect is equivalent to a single transformation. Discuss common patterns, such as two successive reflections across parallel lines being equivalent to a translation, or two rotations around the same point combining into a single rotation. Stem sentences can be useful to support structured explanations. Encourage students to justify their reasoning and test their ideas by reversing transformations to check their conclusions.

small_step_id: Year 11_15-16_Spring_Higher_Transformations_9_Invariant points (E)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of invariant points and lines by observing which parts of a shape remain unchanged during different transformations. Begin by demonstrating a reflection where the shape touches the mirror line, and ask students to identify points that have not moved. Highlight to students that points on the mirror line are invariant. Repeat with other transformations, highlighting that invariant points occur in rotations only when the centre lies on a vertex or edge of the shape. Similarly, in enlargements, this occurs only when the centre of enlargement is on the shape itself. When discussing enlargements, note special cases such as a scale factor of 1, where all points are invariant. Emphasise that translations have no invariant points, as every point moves the same distance and direction, unless the shape remains unchanged.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_10_Addition problems
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This small step brings together the learning from the previous steps, as children start to answer addition problems that are not isolated to a specific structure. As this is the first time that they are likely to have explored multiple structures within different contexts, this can initially be overwhelming for children. The use of manipulatives and realistic situations can support children to understand what is happening. While concrete resources and visual representations are useful, children should move towards working in the abstract. This is an excellent opportunity to reinforce learning on number bonds, from earlier in the block. Children should start to use these bonds to find answers to additions rather than always relying on counting.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_11_Find a part
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Now that children have looked at addition in detail, in this small step they begin to think about subtraction by finding a part. The focus of this small step is on the knowledge and use of number bonds to identify missing parts, rather than formal subtraction and the subtraction symbol. A practical way to introduce this to children is through games. If you tell them that you have 5 counters altogether, and show them 2 in one hand, they can use their knowledge of bonds and their earlier learning to work out how many are in the other hand. Children then begin to work more abstractly and use their earlier learning to identify what is missing. Questions will be presented in the form 3 + = 5, rather than 5 − 3 = . They will be introduced to the subtraction symbol formally in the next step.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_12_Subtraction – find a part
baseline_ss_wr_desc: are formally introduced to the subtraction symbol for the first time. As in the previous step, the structure of all the questions is partitioning. The only difference is the way in which children represent their findings. They are still required to use their knowledge of number bonds to find parts, but represent them using the subtraction symbol. To begin, children focus on the meaning of the subtraction symbol rather than having to identify missing values. They are given a completed part-whole model and write the related subtractions using the numbers in the part-whole model to start to build their understanding. As children become more secure in this, and understand what the subtraction symbol represents, they then use it to answer missing number problems similar to the ones they saw in the previous step.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_13_Fact families – the eight facts
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Now that children have been exposed to both addition and subtraction, in this small step they build on their knowledge of addition fact families to find all eight facts within a fact family. An example of such a fact family is: 3 + 5 = 8 8 = 3 + 5 5 + 3 = 8 8 = 5 + 3 8 − 5 = 3 3 = 8 − 5 8 − 3 = 5 5 = 8 − 3 Initially, the focus is on identifying the facts from a completed part-whole model or number sentence. Once children are secure in this, they can start to use questions in similar structures to those they have seen previously, to complete a calculation and find its related fact family.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_14_Subtraction – take away/cross out (How many left?)
small_step_name: Subtraction – take away/cross out (How many left?)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: are introduced to the structure of subtraction that is “taking away”. This is the first time within this block that they will have seen such questions. In the same way as they were introduced to partitioning, this is done within this step without the use of the subtraction symbol. Use of the subtraction symbol follows formally in the next small step. Taking away is often the structure of subtraction that children are more familiar and comfortable with, as they can physically take things away to support their understanding. They can then move on to crossing out on diagrams and pictures. In each question, children are required to find out how many are left. In later steps, children will use number sentences for this type of problem. Although physically taking away can aid initial understanding, moving towards crossing out can help children to relate the numbers in the number sentences to the question and understand what each number represents.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_15_Subtraction – take away (How many left?)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: formalise their learning from the previous step. They again focus on subtraction questions that require them to take away, but this time record their findings in a number sentence. The use of “first, then, now” stories can aid understanding and help children to relate the question to the number sentence. For example, for the story “First there were 5 birds in a tree. Then 2 of the birds flew away. Now there are 3 birds in the tree”, the related subtraction sentence is 5 − 2 = 3. Encourage children to recognise that the 5 represents the number of birds at the start, the 2 represents the number of birds that flew away and the 3 represents the number of birds that are left. Initially, children simply form the subtraction sentences for a given scenario. Then they move on to questions where they need to work out how many are left. Use of concrete resources and pictorial representations is useful throughout.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_16_Subtraction on a number line
baseline_ss_wr_desc: look at subtraction on a number line for the first time. Children use the method of “counting back” to find the answers to subtraction calculations. As they did when adding more, they start from the “first” number and then count back to find the answer. These questions can be linked to examples and scenarios they have used in earlier steps in this block. This allows children to first focus their attention on how the number line helps with the calculation, before they move on to work more abstractly to complete subtractions by counting back. As in the previous step, encourage children to think about each number within a calculation, what it represents and how it is shown on the number line. For example, in 5 − 3 = 2, 5 is the number they start at, 3 is the number of jumps back they do and 2 is the number they land on.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_17_Add or subtract 1 or 2
baseline_ss_wr_desc: focus on adding 1 or 2 in a variety of different contexts. They combine all the methods and approaches they have seen so far in this block. The main difference between this learning and the previous learning is that children need to decide for the first time whether the question is an addition or a subtraction. So far, they have only seen each skill in isolation. Encourage children to make connections between adding/ subtracting 1 and adding/subtracting 2. It is important that they recognise that adding 2 is the same as adding 1 twice, and similarly subtracting 2 is the same as subtracting 1 twice. This will help children to be secure in their understanding of the composition of 2

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_1_Introduce parts and wholes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: begin to think about parts and wholes. While this reinforces and reminds children of what they have learned in Reception, they are unlikely to have been formally introduced to the language of “parts” and “whole”. Ensure time is spent identifying the parts and the whole during activities. Allow children to explore and notice different compositions; for example, 5 can be composed of 2 and 3 or 1 and 4 or 1 and 1 and 3. Encourage children to recognise that numbers can be composed of two or more parts. At this stage, children should be given the opportunity to explore this concept through play and physical activities. The part-whole model is introduced in the next step.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_2_Part-whole model
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Now that children have explored parts and wholes, in this small step they are introduced to the part-whole model. This is sometimes referred to as a “cherry model”. The main teaching point is for children to see that a whole group of objects can be composed of two or more parts and that they can represent this using a part-whole model. The group can be split in a variety of different ways. Draw children’s attention to the fact that the parts cannot be bigger than the whole group. Provide children with laminated part-whole models, so that they can experiment with physical objects – either drawing or placing pictures on the part-whole model. Encourage them to describe what they do by saying full sentences aloud. Children should be comfortable describing the parts and wholes in a variety of ways, sometimes starting with the whole and at other times with a part.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_3_Write number sentences
baseline_ss_wr_desc: learn that the addition symbol (+) can be used to represent combining two or more parts and the equals symbol (=) can be used to show the equivalence between the whole and the sum of the parts. At this stage, children consider a specific order to the number sentence (a + b = c). They focus on the language associated with this number sentence, for example 7 apples plus 3 apples is equal to 10 apples. Once understanding is established, children explore number sentences written in a different order, such as 4 = 1 + 3 “First, then, now” stories are a great way to link real-life situations to the number sentences and part-whole models.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_4_Fact families – addition facts
baseline_ss_wr_desc: build on their learning about writing number sentences by looking at addition fact families. Children recognise that the order of an addition sentence can be varied, and they begin to discover that addition is commutative. For example, 3 + 2 = 5 2 + 3 = 5 5 = 3 + 2 5 = 2 + 3 Continue to use concrete resources and pictures to support children’s understanding – ten frames and counters and cubes are particularly useful. small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_5_Number bonds within 10
baseline_ss_wr_desc: combine their knowledge of the part-whole model and addition facts to explore number bonds within 10 Starting with the whole, children break numbers into parts and explore how many different ways a number can be partitioned. Double-sided counters and ten frames are useful concrete resources, together with dot patterns. Children will see numbers made from dot patterns differently, for example some may see 6 as being made up of 5 and 1, while others may see it as being made up of two 3s. Exploring patterns is a good way to encourage discussion and expose children to different ways of thinking. Throughout this step, continue to look at number sentences written with the symbols in different places and talk about the commutative nature of the calculations, for example 3 + 1 = 4 is the same as 1 + 3 = 4

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_6_Systematic number bonds within 10
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Now that children have explored number bonds within 10, in this small step they start to work systematically to identify all the number bonds. Some children may have started to do this naturally, whereas others will need to be exposed to this way of thinking. It is important that children learn to work systematically to ensure that they organise their thinking and consider all the possibilities in a problem. Double-sided counters are extremely useful in this step, as children can clearly see the pattern formed when they work systematically to find number bonds. If they start, for example, with 5 counters all showing the same colour, they can turn 1 over to show that 1 + 4 = 5, turn another over for 2 + 3 = 5 and so on to find all the number bonds in a systematic way.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_7_Number bonds to 10
baseline_ss_wr_desc: move on from number bonds within 10 to number bonds to 10 Initially, allow children to explore finding the number bonds. They could use two different colour cubes to build towers of 10 and represent their tower in a number sentence. For example, if their tower is made up of 2 blue cubes and 8 red cubes, they have 10 cubes altogether, so 2 + 8 = 10 As children become more comfortable in finding these bonds to 10, encourage them to use their earlier learning to work systematically to find all the number bonds. Ten frames and double-sided counters can support them with their thinking. This is essential learning that forms the basis of our number system, so time should be spent ensuring that children are comfortable with finding and recognising these bonds.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_8_Addition – add together
baseline_ss_wr_desc: begin to formalise the idea of addition as bringing two or more parts together to create a whole. This is a more formal way of looking at the learning they have covered earlier in this block. At this stage, the focus should be on bringing two parts together, rather than adding more, which will be covered explicitly in the next step. When representing their additions, encourage children to use correct mathematical language to explain, for example “3 cubes plus 5 cubes is equal to 8 cubes.” The use of “is equal to” rather than “makes” will support children in later learning. Ten frames, counters and Rekenreks are useful manipulatives to support this learning, and part-whole models can be used to represent additions.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Addition and subtraction within 10_9_Addition – add more
baseline_ss_wr_desc: build on their understanding of addition as they explore the structure of “adding more”. The focus is on increasing one quantity by a given amount, while continuing to work within 10 As in the earlier steps, classroom items and concrete resources can be used to support children’s learning and “first, then, now” stories can help to build their understanding. For example, “First Rosie has 3 pencils. Then she is given 2 more pencils. How many pencils does she have now?” While exploring with physical pencils will help children with initial understanding, moving towards representations such as ten frames and counters and Rekenreks will support when working in the abstract. A number line can also support children in finding how many there are. When working on a number line, they should start from the “first” number, and draw jumps to find the total.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_10_Compare groups by matching
baseline_ss_wr_desc: match one object with another to compare groups. This is sometimes referred to as one-to-one correspondence, where children check if, for example, there are enough presents for everyone to have one each. Children should be exposed to situations where there are too many, not enough or just the right amount. Children should be encouraged to move physical objects or draw lines between pictorial representations to support them in matching. At this stage, children do not need to know the exact difference between the groups if there is a difference.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_11_Fewer, more, same
baseline_ss_wr_desc: compare numbers of objects. It is important to ensure that children have clear understanding of new vocabulary such as “fewer”, “more” and “same”. They need to practise using the words in a variety of contexts in the same way that they need to practise working with numbers in a variety of contexts. In particular, the word “fewer” can be tricky, as many adults tend to incorrectly use the word “less” instead. “Fewer” is used when talking about a number of things or objects, whereas “less” is used when talking about values. For example, “There are fewer blue cars than red cars” is correct, not “There are less blue cars than red cars.”

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_12_Less than, greater than, equal to
small_step_name: Less than, greater than, equal to
baseline_ss_wr_desc: move on from describing whether there are “fewer”, “more” or the “same” number of objects to comparing numerical values using the vocabulary “less than”, “greater than” or “equal to” alongside the symbols <, > and =. Number tracks are particularly useful in this step and children will begin to see that smaller numbers are to the left of greater numbers. Concrete resources can also be used, but make sure that children do not get confused with the previous step, where they were using words to describe sets of objects. It needs to be clear that they are now comparing the numbers not the objects.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_13_Compare numbers
baseline_ss_wr_desc: build on their learning from earlier in the block to compare pairs of numbers within 10 Children can use their knowledge of counting to support them, for example because they would say 6 after 5, they know that 6 is greater than 5. Children can also use their knowledge of representing numbers using objects to help them identify which of a pair of numbers is greater or less than the other. In the previous steps, children were introduced to the language of “greater than”, “less than” and “equal to” alongside the corresponding inequality symbols >, < and =. They use these throughout this step when comparing numbers. It is important that children use all the symbols, in order to reinforce their meaning. In order to bring in other learning from this block, children could also compare numbers written as words.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_14_Order objects and numbers
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Now that children are confident counting and comparing numbers to 10, in this small step they move on to ordering three groups of objects. Expose children to different methods for ordering, such as comparing two groups initially, and lining groups up. Children should use the language they learnt in the previous steps and be introduced to the vocabulary “most” and “fewest” and begin to use it. Alongside the objects, introduce numbers so that children can begin to order a set of three numbers. They will need introducing to the language of “greatest” and “smallest” and should begin to use it. At this stage, it is not necessary for children to order more than three numbers, although children who are confident with three numbers can be challenged to do this.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_15_The number line
baseline_ss_wr_desc: are introduced to a number line for the first time. So far, children have only used number tracks, so they may be tempted to label the numbers in between the divisions on the number line. Careful explanation will be needed to avoid this. All number lines will count in 1s. The number line can be used to practise and consolidate the skills learnt so far in this block. Children recap counting from zero to 10 forwards when labelling a number line and can also practise counting backwards if they read from right to left. They can clearly see that 1 more is the next number to the right on the number line, while 1 less is the previous number. The number line can also be used to consolidate comparison of numbers using both words and inequality symbols, as well as being used to order numbers. A number line is a good opportunity to count from zero, as children do not do this when counting objects.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_1_Sort objects
baseline_ss_wr_desc: learn that collections of objects can be sorted into sets based on attributes such as colour, size or shape. Sorting enables children to consider what is the same about all the objects in one set and how they differ from the objects in other sets. Children need to understand that the same collection of objects can be sorted in different ways and should be encouraged to come up with their own criteria for sorting objects into sets. Practical activities should be used to support the learning in this step and ideas are suggested in Key learning. The concept of sorting can also be reinforced during daily activities such as lining up. Children could be asked to line up based on certain criteria, for example whether they have a sister.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_2_Count objects
baseline_ss_wr_desc: The aim of this small step is for children to be able to fluently count to 10 when counting objects. Focus on the five counting principles when assessing children’s ability to count accurately. The one-to-one principle: Children assign one number name to each object that is being counted. The stable-order principle: When counting, the numbers have to be said in a certain order. The cardinal principle: The final object in a group is the total number of objects in that group. The abstraction principle: Anything can be counted, including things that cannot be touched, such as sounds and movements, for example jumps. The order-irrelevance principle: The order in which they count a group of objects is irrelevant. There will still be the same number.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_3_Count objects from a larger group
small_step_name: Count objects from a larger group
baseline_ss_wr_desc: continue to count objects, but this time they are asked to count a specific number of objects from a larger group. This requires children to be more organised and careful when counting. From a larger group, children select a given number of objects and count them out. When asked “How many?”, they should be able to recall the final number they said. Children who have not grasped the cardinal counting principle will recount the whole group again. To support children, it may be useful to ask them to count the objects onto a mat or into a container before moving on to pictorial representations.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_6_Count on from any number
baseline_ss_wr_desc: count on from any number while staying within 10. For example, they may be given a starting number of 4 and asked to continue “5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10”. Ten frames and number tracks are useful tools to support children with this concept. When used side by side, they help children to continue to link a representation to the numeral and/or the word. Note that children have not yet been formally introduced to the number line, so using this representation at this stage could be confusing. Being able to count on is an important skill to develop in preparation for addition, where children can start with an amount and count on to get the total.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Place value within 10_9_1 less
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Once children are confident counting backwards and placing numbers on a track, the language of “1 less” can be introduced. need to know that 1 less is the number before and they should use their counting skills or a number track to help them. It is important to make references back to previous learning on finding 1 more, so that children understand that finding 1 less is the opposite of finding 1 more. Cubes are a useful manipulative to show the concept of “1 less”, as children can link this to the everyday activity of walking down the stairs.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Shape_1_Recognise and name 3-D shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This small step is the first in a block of learning on shape. Children start by looking at 3-D shapes, as these are tangible shapes that they can touch and feel to help understand their identifying features. Children are required to name simple 3-D shapes such as cubes, cuboids, cylinders, pyramids, cones and spheres. While some questions require children to write the names of the shapes, at this point the focus should be more on verbally naming and matching. Encourage children to make links to previous learning and to start to think about the 2-D faces on a 3-D shape, as this will support them later on when they look at 2-D shapes in detail.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Shape_2_Sort 3-D shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: start to sort 3-D shapes. They should be given the opportunity to explore similarities and differences between shapes as they play, and to sort them according to what they notice. While they may have naturally started to sort 3-D shapes already, in this step children sort and group 3-D shapes more formally according to simple properties, including type, size and colour. As well as sorting shapes themselves, children also identify how given groups of shapes have been sorted. Encourage children to explain in detail what they notice about groups of shapes and to consider whether they could have been sorted another way. Children should think about the key features of each 3-D shape. Encourage them to consider questions such as “Will they stack, or will they roll?” as another method for sorting.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Shape_3_Recognise and name 2-D shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Now that children have looked in detail at 3-D shapes, they begin to look at 2-D shapes. They will have experience of 2-D shapes and may already know some of the names. Children are required to name simple 2-D shapes, such as triangles, squares, rectangles and circles. While some questions require children to write the names of the shapes, at this point the focus should be on verbally naming and matching. As 2-D shapes cannot be physically explored in the same way as 3-D shapes, they can be difficult to introduce to children in a practical way. 3-D shapes can be used as a way of exploring 2-D shapes, where children focus on the faces of the 3-D shapes to identify which 2-D shapes they are made up of. They can also draw around 3-D shapes or use them to make prints of 2-D shapes. It is essential that children recognise that 2-D shapes are completely flat.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Shape_4_Sort 2-D shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: start to sort 2-D shapes. While they may have naturally started to sort 2-D shapes already, in this step they sort and group 2-D shapes more formally according to simple properties, including type, size and colour. As well as sorting shapes into groups themselves, children also identify how given groups of shapes have been sorted. Encourage children to explain in detail what they notice about groups of shapes and to consider whether they could have been sorted another way. They should think about what is the same and what is different about shapes, while also recognising that the orientation of a shape does not affect its properties. Take time to explore the similarities between squares and rectangles, so that children see the connection.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Autumn__Shape_5_Patterns with 2-D and 3-D shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: create patterns with 2-D and 3-D shapes. They should experience both repeating patterns (ABAB) and symmetrical patterns (ABBCBBA), but do not need to know the names of these types of patterns. Children use both 2-D and 3-D shapes to complete and make simple patterns, focusing on different shapes, sizes and colours. Encourage children to say the patterns aloud, consolidating their previous learning on naming shapes. Use shapes in different orientations to reinforce children’s recognition of 2-D and 3-D shapes. Children should be able to recognise the rule within a pattern and use this to continue it in any direction.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Addition and subtraction within 20_10_Missing number problems
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this final small step, children explore missing number problems. They use the idea of inverse operations to see that if they start with a number and add 2 to it, then to “undo” that they need to subtract 2. Bar models and part-whole models are useful representations for this. “First, then, now” stories can be particularly helpful for children to act out the problems and visualise what is happening. Use of counters and ten frames, as well as number lines, supports children in their understanding of a missing number problem, helping them to discuss what the numbers in a problem represent. With the missing number problem 3 + = 5, a common mistake is to add 3 and 5 and get 3 + 8 = 5. Children need to spot that this does not make sense, as 8 is greater than 5

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Addition and subtraction within 20_1_Add by counting on within 20
baseline_ss_wr_desc: build on their learning from earlier in the year as they explore addition by counting on from a given number within 20 The use of ten frames and counters or cubes is particularly useful, together with bar models. Children should begin to understand that addition is commutative (although they do not need to formally know the word), and that it is more efficient to start from the greater number than the smaller number. For example, when working out 1 + 13, it is quicker to add 1 to 13 than to add 13 to 1. A number line is a particularly useful tool to exemplify this point, as children see the benefit of drawing just 1 jump rather than drawing 13 jumps. It is important that children see that they are not just counting the total of two separate numbers or items; rather, they are adding to what they already have.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Addition and subtraction within 20_2_Add ones using number bonds
baseline_ss_wr_desc: use number bonds and related facts when adding within 20, as an alternative to counting on. This is a more efficient method because, for example, if they know that 4 and 2 are a bond to 6, they can use this fact to see that 14 and 2 are a bond to 16, as are 4 and 12 Using counters and ten frames and base 10 enables children to see the links between related facts, noticing that, for example, 11 + 6 is 10 more than 1 + 6 Children can also explore missing number problems such as 5 + = 17 using the knowledge that 5 and 2 are a number bond to 7

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Addition and subtraction within 20_3_Find and make number bonds to 20
small_step_name: Find and make number bonds to 20
baseline_ss_wr_desc: explore number bonds to 20. They have already learnt about number bonds to 10 and should be confident with these. It is essential that children are fluent in their number bonds as they are used frequently throughout the curriculum. Children use their knowledge of number bonds to 10 to find number bonds to 20. Using examples such as 7 + 3, 17 + 3 and 7 + 13 encourages children to see the link between bonds to 10 and bonds to 20, as well as reinforcing their understanding of place value. They see that working systematically helps them to find all the possible number bonds to 20 Representations such as ten frames, counters, Rekenreks and part-whole models, among others, can be used to support children’s understanding.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Addition and subtraction within 20_4_Doubles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: learn about doubles, with a focus on adding the two equal quantities together as opposed to multiplying by 2 Give children opportunities to build doubles using real objects and mathematical equipment. Building numbers using the pair-wise patterns on ten frames helps them to see the doubles. Mirrors and barrier games are a fun way for children to see doubles as they build and begin to explore symmetry. Encourage children to say the doubles as they build them, for example “Double 2 is 4” Provide examples of doubles and non-doubles for children to sort and explain why they have sorted in the way they have. Dominoes are a great resource for this activity. At this point, children only explore doubles up to double 10

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Addition and subtraction within 20_5_Near doubles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Building on the previous step, in this small step children use doubles to help work out near doubles. For example, they can use the double fact that 6 + 6 = 12 to work out 6 + 7 by adding 1 more. They should see that this is a more efficient method than counting on. As in the previous step, building numbers in a pair-wise pattern on ten frames can help children visualise that to work out 3 + 4, they can do 3 + 3 plus 1 more. Children can also explore finding near doubles through subtraction, for example 3 + 4 is equal to 4 + 4 minus 1. This can be useful for children who are more confident with certain doubles than others. For example, if a child is not confident with doubling 7, they may struggle with 7 + 8, but if they can double 8, they can use this fact instead.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Addition and subtraction within 20_6_Subtract ones using number bonds
baseline_ss_wr_desc: begin subtracting within 20. Earlier in the year, children subtracted within 10 by counting back and using number lines. They now subtract within 20 using their knowledge of number bonds. For example, if they know the number bond 7 – 5 = 2, then they know that 17 – 5 = 12 By completing these calculations side by side using ten frames, counters, part-whole models or base 10, children see that the second subtraction will have an answer that is 10 greater than the first subtraction. At this stage, none of the subtractions cross 10, so children can focus on using their number bond knowledge rather than counting back, which is covered in the next step.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Addition and subtraction within 20_7_Subtraction – counting back
baseline_ss_wr_desc: build on the language of subtraction, recognising the subtraction symbol from earlier learning and using it within 20 Children use the counting back strategy for numbers within 20, including subtractions that cross 10. The use of zero is important, so children know that when nothing is taken away, the start number remains the same, or when the whole group is taken away, there will be nothing left. Crossing out and using a number line are particularly useful for counting back to work out subtractions. This can also be linked with “first, then, now” stories.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Addition and subtraction within 20_8_Subtraction – finding the difference
baseline_ss_wr_desc: formally learn about finding the difference for the first time and explore it as a form of subtraction. Children often struggle with this concept as they are not required to physically take away or count back a specified amount as they have previously experienced. Instead, they are making comparisons between two amounts. In some cases the question will be worded as “How many more …?” Up until now, they have only encountered the word “more” when thinking about addition. Children can use their skills of counting back and counting on to help them find the difference. Alternatively, they can make both amounts and visually see how many more or less a number is.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Addition and subtraction within 20_9_Related facts
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Now that children have spent some time exploring addition and subtraction separately, in this small step they look at how they relate to each other, considering the addition and subtraction fact families for numbers within 20 Children use both concrete resources and pictures to find links between the addition and subtraction sentences. Highlight that addition and subtraction are inverse operations. As well as finding the four related facts, children can write the sentences with the “=” at either the end or the start. Throughout this step, the idea of commutativity should be reinforced, and children should be able to verbalise that addition can be done in any order, whereas subtraction cannot. It is not necessary for children to use the word “commutative” at this stage.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Length and height_1_Compare lengths and heights
baseline_ss_wr_desc: compare lengths and heights of objects using language such as “longer than”, “shorter than” and “taller than”. Children understand that height is a type of length and that the language they use changes, depending on what type of length they are describing and comparing. Children should also be exposed to objects that have the same length or height and use the language of “is the same” or “is equal to” to compare. At this stage, children only compare the lengths and heights of pairs of objects. Ordering lengths and heights is covered later in Key Stage 1

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Length and height_2_Measure length using objects
baseline_ss_wr_desc: begin to measure the lengths and heights of objects, using non-standard units of measure such as cubes or paper clips. As in the previous step, they explore both lengths and heights. It is important that children know that in order to measure the length of something they need to use a consistent unit of measure. They should see that it is not useful to measure the length of something using a range of objects, for example a combination of cubes and paper clips. Similarly, the chosen unit of measure should be equal in size, for example all the paper clips must be the same. Learning from the previous step is consolidated, as children make comparisons of lengths they have measured. They should see that for accurate comparisons they must use a consistent unit of measure, for example cubes for both items.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Length and height_3_Measure length in centimetres
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Building on the previous step, children measure the lengths and heights of objects using a ruler and a standard unit of measure: centimetres. They are introduced to the abbreviation “cm”, so that they do not have to write the full word. Discuss with children why it is helpful to have a standard unit of measure that can be used around the world. Model how to align a ruler with the object being measured. Also show how to look to the nearest whole centimetre when measuring objects that are not an exact number of centimetres. Learning from the first step is consolidated, as children make comparisons of lengths they have measured.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Mass and volume_1_Heavier and lighter
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this block, children are formally introduced to mass for the first time. They may have some understanding of describing something as heavy or light from their own experience or from previous learning in Reception. Children begin by holding objects to compare them, using the language of “heavier” or “lighter”. They then use balance scales to check their comparisons. They need to understand that the heavier object is lower on the balance scale. At this stage, children do not need to measure the actual mass of objects in order to compare them. Children may assume that larger objects are heavier than smaller objects or that objects that are the same size/shape have the same mass. Comparing the mass of a large inflated balloon and a small ball of modelling clay, and comparing the mass of an inflated and a water-filled balloon should help to overcome these misconceptions.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Mass and volume_2_Measure mass
baseline_ss_wr_desc: use a variety of non-standard units, such as cubes or bricks, to measure the mass of an object. Building on the previous step, children should understand that when a scale is balanced, objects have the same mass. On a balanced scale, the number of non-standard units on one side tells them the mass of the object on the other side. Highlight the importance of choosing the same non-standard unit to measure the mass. Measuring the mass of an object using an assortment of different non-standard units, such as a number of cubes, pencils and wooden bricks, makes it difficult to record the object’s mass. Children may find it difficult to balance objects exactly. If an object does not balance exactly, encourage them to use the closest number or to try a different non-standard unit.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Mass and volume_3_Compare weight
baseline_ss_wr_desc: compare the weights of two objects, still using non-standard units of measure. Children should know that if, for example, an apple has the same weight as 6 cubes and a banana has the same mass as 4 cubes, then the apple is heavier than the banana, provided the cubes have the same mass. Children use their knowledge of “heavier” and “lighter” from earlier in the block to compare the masses of objects. It is important that children are also exposed to examples of objects that have the same mass as each other. Once children are confident comparing two objects, they can begin to order the masses of more than two objects and to use the language of “heaviest” and “lightest”.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Mass and volume_4_Full and empty
baseline_ss_wr_desc: are introduced to volume and capacity for the first time. They begin by exploring practically the idea that capacity is the maximum amount that something can hold. Ensure that they experience a range of different sizes and shapes of containers and begin to make basic comparisons to see which has the greater capacity. Children then explore the concept that volume is the amount of something inside a container. They describe the volume in a container using phrases such as “empty”, “nearly empty”, “nearly full” and “full”. At this stage, no formal measurements of volume or capacity, such as litres, are used.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Mass and volume_5_Compare volume
baseline_ss_wr_desc: develop their understanding of volume further and start to compare volumes using the language of “more than” and “less than”. Initially, children make simple visual comparisons between identical containers, using the language introduced in the previous step. They should still be exposed to a range of different size and shape containers. Children then compare and order more than two glasses. This can include following instructions to show a certain volume, for example showing more than half full, but less than nearly full. Challenge children to also compare volumes in containers with different capacities. For example, if glasses are the same height but different widths and the level of the water is the same, then the wider glass must have a greater volume of water inside. Practical explorations of these types of problems will be key.

small_step_id: Year 1_5-6_Spring__Mass and volume_6_Measure capacity
baseline_ss_wr_desc: measure the capacity of different containers using non-standard units of measure.
