Recognising prisms is covered later in this block and properties of circles will be explored later in Year 8

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Area volume and density_2_Area of a 2-D shape
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit Year 7 learning and calculate the areas of rectangles, parallelograms, triangles and trapezia. Encourage students to explore the links between the areas of different shapes. For example, a parallelogram can be transformed into a rectangle by rearranging its sections or can be cut in half to show the area of a triangle. Using dynamic geometry software can help to visually demonstrate these relationships. Students may need help to identify the perpendicular height of shapes. Model working with shapes in different orientations and shapes with more than two dimensions labelled. Students can use calculators to enable them to focus on choosing and using the correct formulae. If appropriate, challenge students to find the areas of shapes that require unit conversions.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Area volume and density_3_Area of a compound shape
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate the areas of compound shapes. Start by splitting a compound shape into two smaller shapes and working out the area of each, then using these to work out the total area of the compound shape. Introduce examples that include all dimensions and those that require splitting into more than two shapes. Students may need support with identifying unknown lengths in compound shapes. Provide a range of examples that require dimensions to be added as well as subtracted.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Area volume and density_4_Recognise prisms
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students deepen their understanding of 3-D shapes and identify the defining features of prisms. Model and encourage the use of key mathematical language, emphasising the differences between the vocabulary used to describe 3-D and 2-D shapes, such as comparing “edges” with “sides”. Establish that prisms have a constant cross-sectional area along their length. 3-D models or dynamic software can help to illustrate the properties of a prism. Show prisms in different orientations to prevent misconceptions, noting that the length of the prism can sometimes be referred to as its height. Explore non-examples such as pyramids, cones, cylinders and spheres, to help distinguish prisms from other shapes.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Area volume and density_5_Volume of cubes and cuboids
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate the volumes of cubes and cuboids. Clarify that volume measures the space inside a 3-D object and is expressed in cubic units, while area measures the space in two dimensions. Use physical models such as interlocking cubes to help students visualise how volume fills a 3-D shape. Begin by counting layers of cubes before introducing the formula volume = length × width × height. Discuss how the three dimensions can be used interchangeably, and that the word “depth” may also be used. Model looking at cuboids from different orientations to identify the dimensions. Remind students that cubes are special cuboids, in which the dimensions are all equal, so the formula simplifies to the cube of the side length. If appropriate, challenge students to calculate the volumes of cuboids that require unit conversions.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Area volume and density_6_Convert metric units of mass and capacity
small_step_name: Convert metric units of mass and capacity
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students understand and use metric units of mass and capacity. Define the key units and their relationships. For example, 1000 mg is equivalent to 1 g, 1000 g is equivalent to 1 kg and 1000 kg is equivalent to 1 tonne. For capacity, focus on millilitres (ml), centilitres (cl) and litres (l), highlighting that 1000 ml is equivalent to 100 cl and 1 l. Emphasise that metric prefixes remain consistent, with “milli-” meaning one-thousandth and “kilo-” meaning one thousand, reinforcing conversions based on powers of 10 If appropriate, introduce 1 cm3 being equal to 1 ml, explaining how capacity is a measurable volume. Alongside conversion calculations, encourage discussion on selecting appropriate units for different items.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Area volume and density_7_Understand the units of density, mass and volume
small_step_name: Understand the units of density, mass and volume
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore density, mass and volume, developing a deeper understanding of the units involved. Introduce density through familiar comparisons, such as looking at how the same mass of different materials occupies different amounts of space. For example, ask if a tonne of bricks takes up the same volume as a tonne of feathers. For objects of similar size, discuss why their masses differ, reinforcing the link between mass, volume and density. Emphasise the importance of using the correct units and introduce the concept of “per” (mass per unit volume) by relating it to familiar concepts such as mph or km/h. Examine the density of various materials, including solids, liquids and gases, across different volumes and encourage discussion on selecting appropriate units for different objects.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Area volume and density_8_Solve problems with density, mass and volume
small_step_name: Solve problems with density, mass and volume
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on the previous step to solve problems with density, mass and volume. Reinforce that density is the mass per unit of volume and introduce the formula, density = mass volume . It can be helpful to model the calculation of density using a range of common materials, for example wood, metal and plastic, asking students to consider if they think the material will be more or less dense than the previous one. Once students are confident with calculating the density of an object, they can use the formula to calculate the volume or mass of an object. Calculators can be used for support. Encourage students to check that their answers and units make sense for the given context.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Equations and inequalities_10_Solve inequalities with unknowns on both sides E
small_step_name: Solve inequalities with unknowns on both sides E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students develop their understanding of solving equations with unknowns on both sides to solving inequalities. Before introducing inequalities, recap strategies to solve equations with unknowns on both sides, highlighting which unknown has the smaller coefficient. Ensure that students pay attention to inequality symbols used and encourage discussions about integer values that satisfy each inequality. Practice should include examples of inequalities with brackets or of different forms, for example 3 + x < 5x − 1 If appropriate, students could also build on their understanding from the previous step, forming and solving inequalities with unknowns on both sides.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Equations and inequalities_1_Solve 1- and 2-step equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students develop understanding of solving 1- and 2-step equations, building on learning from Year 7 This step provides opportunities to consolidate understanding of arithmetic with directed number, solving equations such as c + 5 = −2 and 3p + 17 = 11, introduced earlier in Year 8, where students used directed number with algebra. They also solve equations in different forms, for example 10 = 5k and 19 = 2x + 1, and practise writing non-integer solutions as both decimals and fractions. Throughout the step, refer to letters as “unknowns” rather than “variables”, as there is only one possible solution for these types of equations.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Equations and inequalities_2_Solve more complex equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students solve equations with brackets and negative coefficients of an unknown. Start with strategies to solve equations involving brackets, ensuring that students are confident expanding single brackets. Discuss other strategies, for example dividing by the coefficient of a bracket as a first step. Students can then explore equations with negative coefficients of an unknown, for example 10 − 2x = 4. Encourage them to add 2x to both sides of the equation as a first step. Discuss other strategies, for example dividing both sides by the negative coefficient of the unknown. Questions should include non-integer and negative solutions.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Equations and inequalities_3_Solve fractional equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students solve equations involving fractions. So far, the 2-step equations that students are likely to be most familiar with are in the form ax + b = c. Discuss the differences between 2-step equations such as x 2 + 3 = 15 and x + 3 2 = 15, ensuring that students understand the order of inverse operations that should be applied. Include practice question sets with a mixture of these, to avoid reliance on repetitive procedures. Once students are confident, move on to more complex equations involving more than two steps, such as 2x + 1 5 = 8 If appropriate, equations with brackets or negative coefficients of an unknown could also be introduced. Ensure practice includes equations written in different forms, as well as non-integer or negative solutions.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Equations and inequalities_4_Form and solve equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students combine their understanding of forming expressions and solving equations to form and solve equations. Ensure that students are confident forming expressions, covered earlier in Year 8. Where appropriate, recap topics to allow a variety of contexts in which to form equations, for example area and perimeter of shapes or angle facts. Start with forming and solving 1-step equations, before moving on to more complex equations such as those covered in Steps 2 and 3. Model how to form an equation from given information, for example equating an expression to a known value. Forming equations with unknowns on both sides is covered in the next step.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Equations and inequalities_5_Solve equations with unknowns on both sides
small_step_name: Solve equations with unknowns on both sides
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students extend their knowledge of solving 2-step equations to equations with unknowns on both sides. Bar models are useful to introduce the strategies required to solve these equations. Encourage students to identify which of the two coefficients of the unknown is smaller. Discuss why subtracting this from both sides is a useful first step, highlighting that this reduces the equation to a 1- or 2-step equation. Ensure that students are comfortable representing solutions in multiple forms, for example x = 3 or 3 = x. Once students are confident, include questions with non-integer and negative solutions. If appropriate, students could also explore equations in which adding an unknown to both sides is an efficient first step, for example 10 − x = 2x + 1

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Equations and inequalities_6_Understand and use inequalities
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn how to interpret and use inequalities. Students may be familiar with inequality symbols from Key Stage 2 when comparing values, for example 5 < 9 Establish that an inequality describes a range of values. Highlight the difference between similar inequalities, for example x < 5 and x ≤ 5, explaining that the range of values for the second inequality includes the value of 5. Encourage students to discuss possible values that satisfy an inequality. Students should also explore compound inequalities, for example 3 < n < 7, again discussing possible values that satisfy the inequality.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Equations and inequalities_7_Inequalities on a number line
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on the previous step to represent inequalities on a number line. Students learn that inequalities using the symbols < or > are represented by an unshaded circle above the number given, and inequalities using the symbols ≤ or ≥ by a shaded circle above the number. Providing students with varied practice can be an effective way to highlight the differences between representations on a number line, for example representing x > 5 and x ≥ 5 Students also represent and interpret compound inequalities on number lines, understanding that this is represented with two circles joined by a straight line segment.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Equations and inequalities_8_Solve inequalities
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students combine their understanding of solving equations and interpreting inequalities to solve inequalities. Start by discussing the similarities and differences between solving equations and inequalities, highlighting that an equation has one solution, whereas an inequality has a set of solutions. Students can apply similar strategies to those they used to solve equations earlier in the block, paying careful attention to the symbol used in any given inequality. This step also provides opportunities to revisit interpreting inequalities and representing inequalities on number lines. Encourage students to check solutions by substituting values into expressions.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Equations and inequalities_9_Form and solve inequalities
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding by forming and solving inequalities. Encourage students to form and solve simple 1- and 2-step inequalities. Contexts such as perimeter and area, as well as everyday problems, can be effective to highlight why forming and solving inequalities is useful. Provide students with variables to use, for example n to represent the number of people in a class, but make it clear that if no variable is specified within a question, they can use any letter. Ensure that students are given opportunities to discuss solutions in context, for example if n represents a number of people, then the minimum/maximum number of people should be an integer.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Indices_1_Add and subtract expressions with indices
small_step_name: Add and subtract expressions with indices
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students add and subtract expressions with indices, reinforcing their understanding of collecting like terms from earlier in the year. Begin with variables with powers up to 3, as students are already familiar with squares and cubes. As confidence develops, introduce higher powers. It may be helpful to recap the meaning of “like” and “unlike” terms. Students may also need a reminder about the meaning of “coefficient” and the convention of omitting 1 as a coefficient. Encourage students to use the identity symbol (æ). Using manipulatives, such as algebra tiles, can effectively illustrate why, for example, 2x² + 3x² æ 5x², not 5x4

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Indices_2_Multiply and divide expressions with indices
small_step_name: Multiply and divide expressions with indices
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students multiply and divide expressions with indices, building on their understanding of simplifying expressions from earlier in the year. Remind students of the conventions for expressions such as 3 × a, b × 4 and c × c, as well as the use of fractions to represent division. Students explore powers, for example recognising that x5 is equivalent to x × x × x × x × x. Encourage them to write calculations out in full to help develop understanding, for example 33 32 = 3 × 3 × 3 3 × 3 . This will help to prepare them for higher powers and the laws of indices later in the block. Also remind students to express answers in fractional form, such as y 2 , rather than using decimals (0.5y).

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Indices_3_Addition law for indices
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the addition law for indices. Recap what a power represents, for example 24 = 2 × 2 × 2 × 2. Introduce questions such as 24 × 23, encouraging students to initially write the calculation out in full before identifying the resulting index. Prompt them to recognise and generalise a rule for multiplying terms with the same base, leading to am × an = am + n. Emphasise that this rule applies only to expressions with the same base, for example 23 × 34 ≠ 67. Students practise working with positive and negative indices in both numerical and algebraic forms, including expressions involving coefficients. Discuss why a variable written without an exponent, such as x, is understood to have a power of 1

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Indices_4_Subtraction law for indices
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding from the previous step and explore the subtraction law of indices. Encourage students to write out the multiplications indicated by the index notation, for example 35 33 = 3 × 3 × 3 × 3 × 3 3 × 3 × 3 , and then to identify terms that can be cancelled out. Reinforce that terms are cancelled because 3 3 = 1. Encourage students to generalise the rule for dividing terms with the same base, leading to am ÷ an = am − n. Remind them that, as for the addition law of indices, this rule only applies when the bases are the same. Throughout this step, students work with both numerical and algebraic expressions.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Indices_5_Addition and subtraction laws for indices
small_step_name: Addition and subtraction laws for indices
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding from the previous two steps by applying both the addition and subtraction laws of indices. If necessary, remind students of the general rules am × an = am + n and am ÷ an = am − n. They should identify which rule to apply and work with expressions that include a combination of multiplication and division. Emphasise that these laws only apply when the bases are the same. Students also explore powers of zero, reasoning why t2 t2 is equal to t0 and 1. Challenge them to extend their thinking with questions involving multiple bases and operations, for example a5 × b3 ÷ a2 × b6

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Indices_6_Powers of powers E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students explore powers of powers, building on their prior knowledge of the laws of indices. Start with examples such as (54)3, showing that it can be rewritten as 54 × 54 × 54. Using their understanding of the addition law of indices, students should then deduce that (54)3 = 512, leading to the general rule (ax)y æ axy. Students practise applying this rule with both numerical and algebraic bases. Once confident, they could extend their understanding to expressions involving coefficients, for example (2x4)5. Remind students of the order of operations to ensure accurate calculations.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Indices_7_Negative indices E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students explore negative indices, building on their knowledge of reciprocals and negative powers of 10, which may have been covered as an extend step in Year 7 A useful way to develop this concept is by identifying a pattern when decreasing the power, for example 102 = 100, 101 = 10, 100 = 1, 10−1 = 1 10 . This illustrates that each time the power decreases, the value is divided by the base, which can be expressed as a fraction. Once students are confident in their understanding of a power of −1, they move on to exploring powers of −2 and lower. Emphasise that the power still applies to the numerator and denominator once the number is expressed as a fraction, for example 5−2 = 12 52 = 1 25. This helps to reinforce the pattern that negative exponents represent the reciprocal of the base raised to the corresponding positive power.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Indices_8_Fractional indices E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students explore fractional indices. Start with unit fractional indices with numerical bases. Explain that the denominator of a fractional exponent indicates the root, for example 9 1 2 = 9 and 27 1 3 = 27 3 Once students are confident in evaluating fractional indices, they explore non-integer bases, focusing on fractions and decimals that comprise square or cube numbers, for example 25 64 and 0.81. Students do not need to work with non-unit fractional indices in this block, as these are covered in Year 10

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_10_Percentage change
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of expressing one value as a percentage of another, now focusing on percentage change. Ensure that students are confident finding the difference and identifying the original amount before writing the fraction in the form difference original . Remind them that if the denominator is a factor or multiple of 100, they can scale the fraction accordingly, whereas for other cases they can use the general formula difference original × 100. It is also beneficial to examine problems that can be solved using both calculator and non-calculator methods, encouraging students to choose the most efficient approach.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_11_Find the original value given a percentage
small_step_name: Find the original value given a percentage
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students determine the original amount from a given percentage. This may have been introduced as an extend step in Year 7, but this step also includes percentages greater than 100%. Emphasise that the original value represents 100%. Start with percentages that are easy to scale, for example 50%, 20% and 10%. Bar models are a useful representation to help students understand the percentage that the amount represents. Students could then explore other percentages such as 80%, 15% and 125%. They could use a scaling method or inverse operations with multipliers to find the original amount. For example, if 80% of £200 is calculated by 200 × 0.8 = 160, then 160 ÷ 0.8 = 200

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_12_Choose appropriate methods to solve percentage problems
small_step_name: Choose appropriate methods to solve percentage problems
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their learning about percentages by applying their knowledge to solve problems. There should be an emphasis on choice of strategy rather than arithmetic, so the use of calculators is encouraged, if appropriate. Model reading questions, asking students what they know and what they need to find out, while highlighting key words. Students can continue to use bar models to represent the problems, allowing them to identify what information is given and what needs to be worked out. Prompt students to check whether their answer is reasonable by considering it in the context of the question.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_1_Percentage of an amount
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students review their understanding of calculating percentages of amounts from Year 7, using calculator and non-calculator methods. Knowledge of multipliers is not required at this stage, as it will be introduced later in the block. Recap methods for finding common percentages such as 1%, 5%, 10%, 25%, 50% and 100% (the whole amount) without a calculator. Using bar models can support students’ understanding, where appropriate. Once they are confident, introduce more complex percentages, for example 17%. Discuss how to combine common percentages to form 17%, for example 10% + 5% + 1% + 1%. Comparing this approach to a calculator method can help to reinforce the idea that once students determine 1%, they can find any percentage.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_2_Convert between percentages and decimals
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students convert between decimals and percentages, building on their Year 7 understanding of fractions, decimals and percentages. This step will build the foundational knowledge required for calculating with multipliers later in the block. Remind students that a percentage means “number of parts per 100” and emphasise the connection to place value and fractions, for example 0.34 = 34 hundredths = 34% = 34 100 . Students convert decimals to percentages and vice versa, focusing on percentages less than 100%. A hundred square can be a useful visual aid to reinforce understanding.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_3_Use multipliers to calculate percentages
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of calculating percentages and converting between decimals and percentages from previous steps. They now use multipliers to find a percentage of an amount, focusing on percentages less than 100%, as percentages greater than 100% will be covered in later steps. Percentage increases using multipliers will be introduced later in the block. Start by reinforcing the relationship between decimals and percentages, for example recognising that 34% is equivalent to 0.34. Discuss how the wording of questions can guide the necessary calculations. For example, when finding 34% of 500, the word “of” in mathematics often signifies multiplication, so the calculation can be written as 34% × 500 or 500 × 0.34

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_4_Convert between percentages and decimals greater than 1
small_step_name: Convert between percentages and decimals greater than 1
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their prior knowledge of converting between percentages and decimals, now focusing on values greater than 1. This step prepares students to calculate percentage increases using a multiplier. Reinforce the understanding that a percentage means “number of parts per 100”. If necessary, connect this concept to partitioning, for example 130% = 100% + 30%, and relate this to its decimal equivalent of 1.3 = 1 + 0.3. Encourage students to explore whether percentages greater than 100% make sense in different contexts, for example receiving 25% extra time in an exam means being given 125% of the standard time. Compare situations where such percentages are possible and where they are not.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_5_Percentage increase using a multiplier
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use multipliers to increase a value by a given percentage, building on their understanding of converting decimals and percentages greater than 1, as well as using multipliers. Recap students’ understanding from Year 7 that a percentage increase is the addition of a percentage of the original amount to the original amount. Remind them that percentages can be written as decimals, and that calculating a percentage increase involves multiplying by the corresponding decimal, known as a multiplier. From there, introduce a more efficient approach by calculating, for example, 120% of the original value directly using the decimal multiplier 1.2. A bar model can be an effective visual tool to illustrate this relationship.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_6_Percentage decrease using a multiplier
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use multipliers to decrease a value by a given percentage. Building on students’ understanding of percentage increase, they now determine the appropriate multiplier for a percentage decrease. Remind them that the original value is 100%, meaning that a 20% decrease leaves 80% of the original. This leads to the more efficient approach of using multipliers, for example multiplying by 0.8 for a 20% decrease. Bar models can be a helpful visual tool to illustrate this reduction, allowing students to see the remaining percentage and to identify the correct multiplier.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_7_Percentage increase and decrease using a multiplier
small_step_name: Percentage increase and decrease using a multiplier
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their learning from the previous steps and calculate percentage increase and decrease using multipliers. They practise a mixture of questions to ensure that they are thinking carefully and not just following a procedure. Discuss the multipliers for increasing or decreasing by, for example, 60%, 6% and 0.6%. Drawing bar models to identify whether the problem represents a percentage increase or decrease and to determine the correct multiplier can be useful. Discuss the key vocabulary to help students recognise increases and decreases, such as a car depreciating in value.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_8_Express one number as a fraction or a percentage of
small_step_name: Express one number as a fraction or a percentage of
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students express one number as a fraction or percentage of another. Start by writing one number as a fraction of another, ensuring that students are confident with this before moving on to percentages. Initially, focus on fractions where the denominator is a factor or multiple of 100, encouraging students to use their knowledge of equivalent fractions to scale the denominator to 100 by multiplying or dividing before converting to a percentage. They should also work with fractions that, when simplified, have a denominator that is a factor of 100. Where appropriate, students could express values such as 1 out of 8 as a percentage, building on their prior knowledge of converting eighths to percentages from Year 7

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Percentages_9_Express one number as a fraction or a percentage of
small_step_name: Express one number as a fraction or a percentage of
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students combine calculator skills with understanding of how to express one number as a fraction or percentage of another, focusing on fractions where the denominator is not a factor or multiple of 100 Start by converting fractions to decimals, before converting them to percentages, then explore how this can be completed in a single calculation. Introduce the general method of a b × 100 to convert a fraction into a percentage. Encourage students to explore cases where the result is not an exact value and requires rounding to a suitable degree of accuracy. Include examples that require unit conversions and everyday contexts, such as calculating 25 days out of 1 year as a percentage.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Positive and negative powers of 10_1_Numbers greater than 1 in standard form
small_step_name: Numbers greater than 1 in standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore positive and negative powers of 10, building the foundations for standard form. It may be useful to start by recapping multiplication and division by 10, 100 and 1000, as well as power notation, for example 102 = 10 × 10. Encourage students to make the link between increasing and decreasing powers of 10 before discussing 100 and negative powers of 10. Highlight the connection between, for example, 102 = 100 and 10 −2 = 1 100 Discuss the benefits of writing numbers in powers of 10, considering numbers such as 1 billion = 1 000 000 000 = 109, while also exploring numbers written in words.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Positive and negative powers of 10_2_Numbers between 0 and 1 in standard form
small_step_name: Numbers between 0 and 1 in standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students write numbers greater than 1 in standard form (also known as standard index form). Introduce students to the correct form, A × 10 n, where 1 ≤ A < 10 and n is an integer. Explain the difference between numbers written in standard form and numbers written as ordinary numbers. Examples of standard form numbers in context are helpful to explain the reason for writing numbers in this way. It is important for students to see non-examples of standard form, such as 0.6 × 107 or 56 × 104, to strengthen understanding. Refer to place value and discourage students from counting/adding zeros.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Positive and negative powers of 10_3_Standard form on a calculator
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students now explore standard form for numbers between 0 and 1 Students learn that a number written in standard form with a negative index has a value between 0 and 1. Remind them that numbers in standard form are in the form A × 10n, where 1 ≤ A < 10 and n is an integer. Recap the effect of multiplying numbers by negative powers of 10, making links to equivalent calculations, for example 4 × 10 −2 = 4 × 0.01. Links with fractional equivalents could also be useful, for example 4 × 1 100 , discussing strategies to calculate, for example 4 ÷ 100. Encourage discussion about examples in context, to highlight why standard form is an efficient way to write small numbers.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Types of data_1_Averages and range
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students differentiate between discrete, continuous, quantitative and qualitative data. Explain that discrete data consists of countable values (for example, the number of tickets sold at a cinema), while continuous data can take any value within a given range and can include decimals and fractions (for example, temperature). Establish that quantitative data is numerical (for example, the number of goals scored in a football match), whereas qualitative data is non-numerical (for example, eye colour). It is important for students to understand how the type of data affects the choice of graphs or averages used in analysis. Encourage them to discuss why qualitative and discrete data are often represented using bar charts, pie charts or pictograms, while line graphs and scatter graphs are typically used to represent continuous data.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Types of data_2_Outliers and errors
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate averages and the range from a list of data, building on learning from Year 7 Recap how each type of average and the range is calculated. Emphasise that the mean, median and mode represent an “average” in different ways, while the range measures the spread of the data. Remind students to consider the context of the data and to give their answer using the correct context, for example “The mean of the heights is 162 cm.” Once students can fluently calculate and differentiate between the averages and range, introduce more complex problems, for example calculating a missing value from a list when given the mean.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Types of data_3_Choose the most appropriate average
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students identify and understand outliers and errors in data sets. Discuss that outliers are numbers that are very different from the rest of the data. Highlight that outliers could be real, unusual values or errors in the data collection. Show examples of data from everyday life and ask students to consider reasons for the outliers. They could also link this step to outliers they have seen in scatter diagrams, improving their ability to notice data that does not follow a pattern. Emphasise the effects that outliers have on averages and range, and discuss the effect of calculating these without including an outlier.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Types of data_4_Compare distributions using averages and the range
small_step_name: Compare distributions using averages and the range
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students continue to develop their understanding of averages, considering when an average (mean, median or mode) for a given data set is appropriate. Discuss the characteristics and limitations of each type of average and when each one is the most suitable representation of “typical” in a set of data. This approach draws students’ attention to a complete data set rather than just performing calculations. Everyday applications, such as wages, demonstrate that the median or mode can provide a more representative summary than the mean, particularly when outliers are present.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Types of data_5_Averages from an ungrouped frequency table
small_step_name: Averages from an ungrouped frequency table
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students continue developing their understanding of averages and range by comparing data sets using these measures. Draw students’ attention to the range as a measure of spread, helping them to understand that it indicates how consistent the data is. When comparing data sets, considering both the average and the range together provides a fuller picture of the data set’s typical values and variability. Examples in context, such as comparing sports teams’ performance statistics, can help students to see how different averages together with the range offer insights into consistency and variation across data sets.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Types of data_6_Represent and interpret grouped discrete data
small_step_name: Represent and interpret grouped discrete data
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate the mean, median and mode from ungrouped frequency tables. When calculating the mean, begin by organising raw data into a table, listing data values together with how often they occur. Model how to find the total of each sub-category, before adding all the products together and dividing by the total number of data values. To find the modal value, encourage students to consider the values within a category that occur most often. Support students finding the median from a table by initially writing the data from the frequency table in a list. Highlight the connection between the median from the list and its location in the table, before modelling how to find the median directly from the table using the cumulative frequency.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Types of data_7_Represent and interpret continuous data grouped into
small_step_name: Represent and interpret continuous data grouped into
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students represent and interpret grouped discrete data in frequency tables, without inequalities. The use of inequality notation will be covered in the next step. Discuss when it is appropriate to use grouped data, focusing on simplifying large data sets. Explain that each data point should fall into one distinct group, so the groups should be structured in a way that avoids any ambiguity, such as using the intervals 0–10, 11–20 and so on. It can be helpful to discuss sensible group intervals. For example, for a data set with a range of 100, two intervals of 0–50 and 51–100 may be less appropriate than smaller intervals such as 0–20 and so on. Students calculate the frequency for each interval and the cumulative frequency of multiple intervals.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Spring__Types of data_8_Mean and mode from a grouped frequency table E
small_step_name: Mean and mode from a grouped frequency table E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students represent and interpret continuous data using grouped frequency tables with equal class intervals. Recap interpreting inequalities, for example 5 < x ≤ 10, and emphasise that continuous data encompasses a range of values within a given interval. Highlight why < and ≤ symbols are necessary to avoid overlaps between intervals and discuss which interval borderline values should be put into. A number line can be useful to illustrate how boundaries separate each interval. Also discuss how to interpret the grouped data, for example identifying intervals with the greatest frequencies. Address the limitations of grouped data, such as the loss of individual data values.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Summer__Basic angle rules and notation_10_Prove simple geometric facts  E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on learning from the previous steps to find the sizes of unknown angles in regular polygons. Start by discussing the term “regular”, as students often misunderstand this to mean only equal lengths, rather than equal lengths and equal angles. Compare different methods to work out the size of one interior angle. Once students are confident in working out unknown angles in a single polygon, move on to examples of compound shapes made from regular polygons. Encourage students to give reasons for each stage of their workings. This step provides an opportunity for students to determine and apply the most suitable method for each question. Calculators may be used throughout this step, so that the focus remains on the method rather than the arithmetic.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Summer__Basic angle rules and notation_1_Angles in parallel lines
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students review the basic rules of angles and notation covered in Year 7 Start by looking at each rule in isolation: the sum of angles on a straight line, angles around a point and angles in triangles and quadrilaterals. Students should be able to identify when angles sum to 180° or 360° and when angles are equal. Once they are confident in identifying the correct rule, move on to diagrams where more than one rule may need to be applied. Remind students of the three-letter angle notation, which is used throughout the block. Encourage them to explain and justify how they identify and calculate angles, using correct vocabulary and angle rules.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Summer__Basic angle rules and notation_2_Alternate and corresponding angles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students investigate the properties of parallel lines, and the angles formed between them and an intersecting transversal. Ensure that students are confident in identifying when lines are parallel and when they are not, using examples and non-examples. Once students are confident in the properties of parallel lines, introduce the transversal. Students measure the angles formed and discover the relationship between the angles. Encourage them to use the correct vocabulary when describing angles. The terms “alternate” and “corresponding” are introduced in this step to describe pairs of angles and their relative positions.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Summer__Basic angle rules and notation_3_Alternate, corresponding and co-interior angles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students begin formally calculating the sizes of alternate and corresponding angles between parallel lines. They build on their learning from the previous step, understanding that in parallel lines, alternate angles are always equal and corresponding angles are always equal. Use examples and non-examples to aid understanding. As with previous steps, ensure that students use the correct vocabulary and angle notation when describing angles and the appropriate language when justifying their reasoning, for example stating “because alternate angles are equal” rather than “because they are alternate”.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Summer__Basic angle rules and notation_4_Solve problems with angles in parallel lines
small_step_name: Solve problems with angles in parallel lines
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their knowledge of alternate and corresponding angles and begin to explore co-interior angles in parallel lines. As in previous steps, explore examples and non-examples using parallel and non-parallel lines to make connections to the fact that co-interior angles in parallel lines sum to 180°. Emphasise that they lie on the same side of the transversal, with both angles between the parallel lines. Initially, encourage students to measure co-interior angles to highlight the relationship between them, discussing how they are different from alternate and corresponding angles, not just in position but also in their properties. Include vertical parallel lines and sets of more than two parallel lines with the same transversal.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Summer__Basic angle rules and notation_5_Properties of special quadrilaterals and their diagonals
small_step_name: Properties of special quadrilaterals and their diagonals
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use the angle facts covered in the previous steps to solve more complex, multi-step problems. Model the thought processes involved when approaching problem-solving questions. Verbalise each step to help students understand how to break down a problem, identify key information and decide which mathematical strategy to apply. Throughout this step, encourage students to label their diagrams, show their workings clearly and justify their answers using correct angle notation and vocabulary. Ensure that students check their answers throughout.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Summer__Basic angle rules and notation_6_Find unknown lengths and angles in special quadrilaterals
small_step_name: Find unknown lengths and angles in special quadrilaterals
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students investigate the properties of quadrilaterals and their diagonals. A Frayer model can be useful to explore the characteristics of quadrilaterals, as well as providing examples and non-examples. Start by focusing on special quadrilaterals such as squares, rectangles, trapezia, rhombi and parallelograms. Encourage students to consider their similarities as well as how they differ, for example in terms of how many sets of parallel lines or right angles they have. Students can then discuss the fact that diagonals join opposite vertices of a quadrilateral and can sometimes be vertical or horizontal in orientation.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Summer__Basic angle rules and notation_7_Exterior angles of a polygon
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use the properties of special quadrilaterals to work out unknown sides and angles. Throughout this step, students apply their knowledge of angle facts, particularly that angles in a quadrilateral sum to 360° and the properties of angles in parallel lines, to make deductions and justify their answers. Start with quadrilaterals in isolation before moving on to compound shapes and more complex diagrams involving quadrilaterals. Encourage students to discuss and label the information on the diagrams and to use correct angle notation and vocabulary to give reasons for their answers. Calculators can be used to ensure that the focus is on angle reasoning rather than arithmetic.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Summer__Basic angle rules and notation_8_Interior angles of a polygon
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the exterior angles of a polygon. Discuss the definition of a polygon, using examples and non-examples to support understanding. Ensure that students are familiar with exterior angles, understanding that they are formed by extending the sides of a polygon and not that they are the remaining angle around the point at each vertex. Establish the relationship between interior and exterior angles, recognising that the two angles will form a straight line and therefore sum to 180°. Then move on to deduce that the exterior angle sum of any polygon is 360°. Ensure that students understand that the exterior angles will only ever be equal when the polygon is regular.

small_step_id: Year 8_12-13_Summer__Basic angle rules and notation_9_Interior angles of a regular polygon
small_step_name: Interior angles of a regular polygon
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the interior angles of a polygon. Ensure that students are aware that the sum of the interior angle and the exterior angle at a vertex is 180°. Once students are secure in this, move on to calculating the interior angle sum. Use diagrams to explore the relationship between the number of sides a polygon has and the number of internal triangles that can be formed within it. This will lead students to deduce that the interior angle sum is equal to (n − 2 ) × 180°, where n is the number of sides. Challenge students to work out the sizes of unknown angles in a polygon by calculating the interior angle sum.
