Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Area and volume_10_Convert metric units of area and volume E
small_step_name: Convert metric units of area and volume E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students convert metric units of area and volume. Start by exploring a square with a side length of 1 cm and comparing it to a square with a side length of 10 mm, highlighting that the lengths are equivalent. This leads to the key insight that 1 cm2 is equal to 100 mm2. Encourage students to recognise that the area scale factor is the square of the linear scale factor. Students apply the same concept to volume, comparing a cube with a side length of 1 cm to a cube with a side length of 10 mm, working out the volume of each cube as 1 cm3 = 1000 mm3. Establish that the volume scale factor is the cube of the linear scale factor.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Area and volume_1_Nets
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the structure of 3-D shapes by investigating their nets. Begin with cubes and cuboids, encouraging students to practically explore how nets are folded to make 3-D shapes, for example by deconstructing simple boxes. As their confidence develops, move on to nets of other 3-D shapes, such as triangular prisms and pyramids, including different nets of the same 3-D shape. Support students to identify which edges join when folding up a net. This step provides the opportunity to revisit the names of 3-D shapes, as well as the vocabulary to describe the properties of shapes, for example “face”, “edge” and “vertex”.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Area and volume_2_Area of a 2-D shape
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit using formulae to work out the areas of triangles and different quadrilaterals. Encourage students to explore the links between the areas of different shapes. For example, parallelograms can be cut and rearranged to form a rectangle, and both rectangles and parallelograms can be cut in half to show the areas of triangles. The formula for the area of a trapezium can also be explored both visually and algebraically. Encourage students to use full sentences when describing lengths and areas and promote the use of appropriate units. Introducing compound shapes can challenge students, as well as providing a link to working out the surface area of 3-D shapes later in the block.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Area and volume_3_Area and circumference of a circle
small_step_name: Area and circumference of a circle
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their learning from Year 8, applying the formulae for area and circumference of a circle. Students may need reminding that the radius is half the diameter. Emphasise that the area represents the space inside the circle, and the circumference is the distance around it. Ensure that students can use calculators fluently and are also confident when working with exact values of pi (π). Learning could be extended to semicircles, if appropriate.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Area and volume_4_Surface area of cubes and cuboids
small_step_name: Surface area of cubes and cuboids
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of the area of a rectangle to calculate the surface area of cubes and cuboids. Discuss the key properties of cubes and cuboids, highlighting that a cube has six identical square faces, and a cuboid has six rectangular faces, with opposite faces being identical. Students may need support to select the correct dimensions from a diagram to work out the areas of individual faces. Start by revisiting nets to develop understanding of how many faces there are and to help identify the correct dimensions. Ensure that students include the correct units in their answers. Students can use a calculator if required.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Area and volume_5_Surface area of a triangular prism E
small_step_name: Surface area of a triangular prism E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students build on their understanding of the area of rectangles and triangles to find the surface area of triangular prisms. As with the previous step, start by sketching nets to ensure understanding of how many faces there are and to help identify the dimensions. Discuss why a sketch is sufficient, rather than an accurate drawing. Encourage students to organise their workings, as this will help them to check for errors. Include triangles with slanted lengths to encourage students to identify the correct dimensions for the base and perpendicular height needed to calculate the total surface area.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Area and volume_6_Surface area of a cylinder E
small_step_name: Surface area of a cylinder E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students combine their understanding of the circumference and area of a circle with surface area to find the surface area of a cylinder. Students may need support in selecting the correct lengths from a given diagram, together with use of the correct formula. Practical unwrapping of cylinders (for example, labels of tin cans) will help students to understand the link between the circumference and the length of the rectangular face in the net of cylinder. As in previous steps, discuss the units for surface area. Ensure that students are confident using the pi (π) function on their calculators and, if appropriate, they can give answers in terms of pi (π).

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Area and volume_7_Volume of a prism
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students extend their understanding, from learning in Year 8, of the volume of cubes and cuboids to finding the volume of any prism. Demonstrate how constructing a 2-D shape for the cross-section and repeating it in layers can form a 3-D prism. Using this visual approach highlights that the volume is calculated by multiplying the area of the cross-section by the prism’s length. Introduce students to the formula: volume of a prism = area of cross-section × length. Model calculating the volumes of prisms with different cross-sections and in different orientations, highlighting the dimensions needed. Discuss using units such as cm3 or m3. Calculators may be used, if required.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Area and volume_8_Volume of a cylinder
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding of the area of a circle and the volume of a prism to find the volume of a cylinder. A cylinder, by definition, is not a prism but has similar characteristics to a prism, such as a constant cross-section (in this case, a circle). Use visual aids, such as stacks of counters or coins, so that students can visualise how the cross-section is repeated along the cylinder, just like a prism. They may need support in selecting the correct dimensions from a given diagram. Initially, show only the radius and length/height. As their confidence develops, encourage students to explore cylinders of different dimensions and orientations. As in the previous step, discuss the units for volume. The use of calculators is encouraged and students can give answers in terms of pi (π), if appropriate.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Area and volume_9_Volume of cones, pyramids and spheres E
small_step_name: Volume of cones, pyramids and spheres E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students are introduced to formulae of volume that will be studied in greater depth at Key Stage 4 Students should appreciate that the volume of a cone is equal to one-third of the volume of a cylinder with the same dimensions, and the volume of a pyramid is one-third the volume of a prism with the same base and height. Support students to use the appropriate formulae for cones, pyramids and spheres by directly comparing them. Reinforce the specific elements of each formula; for example, when finding the volume of a cone or a pyramid, the coefficient is 1 3 , whereas for a sphere it is 4 3

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Equations inequalities and formulae_1_Solve equations and inequalities
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate and build on their understanding of solving equations and inequalities from Year 8 Remind students that linear equations have a single solution, whereas inequalities can be satisfied by a range of values. Asking students to identify integer solutions to inequalities can help to embed this idea. Discuss how non-integer solutions can be represented as fractions or decimals. The use of a calculator may support students to overcome any potential arithmetic barriers. Encourage students to check solutions by substituting back into the given equation. When appropriate, look at equations expressed in unfamiliar forms, for example 3 + 2m = 9, 15 < c 5 + 1. Students could also solve compound inequalities, such as 4 < 2x < 20

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Equations inequalities and formulae_2_Solve equations and inequalities with brackets
small_step_name: Solve equations and inequalities with brackets
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their learning from Year 8, expanding and solving equations and inequalities with brackets. Ensure that students are confident expanding single brackets. Model different strategies to solve equations or inequalities with brackets. Include equations or inequalities with non-integer solutions, emphasising that these can be left in fractional form where appropriate. Provide a range of equations and inequalities presented in different forms. Encourage students to check solutions by substituting values into equations or inequalities. Students will solve inequalities with negative coefficients in the next step.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Equations inequalities and formulae_3_Inequalities with negative numbers E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students explore and understand that the inequality is reversed when multiplying or dividing by a negative number. Ensure that students are confident with multiplying and dividing by negative numbers, especially knowing that, for example, −(−4) = 4. Begin by exploring how inequalities such as −c > 4 can be rewritten as c < −4. Encourage students to test different values that satisfy the inequality before looking at solving 1-step inequalities such as −2x > 10. Model using a variety of strategies to help students understand what is happening, for example dividing both sides by −2, multiplying (or dividing) both sides by −1 or adding 2x to both sides, subtracting 10 and then dividing by positive 2

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Equations inequalities and formulae_4_Solve equations and inequalities with unknowns on both sides
small_step_name: Solve equations and inequalities with unknowns on both sides
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit solving equations and inequalities from Year 8, solving both equations and inequalities with unknowns on both sides. Using bar models alongside abstract calculations is a useful strategy to support students. Ensure that they encounter different forms of equations and inequalities: where the greater coefficient of the unknown is on the right side; where the smaller of the two given coefficients is negative, for example 15 − 2x = x + 6; and equations of the form ax + b = cx. Encourage students to check solutions by using substitution to assess if the equation or inequality holds true. Calculators may be used if required.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Equations inequalities and formulae_5_Solve problems with equations and inequalities
small_step_name: Solve problems with equations and inequalities
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students form and solve equations and inequalities in different mathematical contexts. A good starting point could be to use basic geometric facts to form and solve equations. Students may need reminding of angle relationships such as the sum of the angles in a triangle being equal to 180°. Highlight the difference between an expression and an equation throughout, ensuring that students eventually form an equation to solve. Encourage students to collect like terms, where appropriate, once they have created an equation or inequality.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Equations inequalities and formulae_6_Substitute into formulae and equations
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore differences between equations and formulae through substitution. Throughout, refer to letters in formulae as variables, rather than unknowns. Start by using substitution to verify solutions to equations: “Show that r = is the solution to ”. Students then substitute values into formulae to produce equations. Introduce the concept of the “subject” of a formula as preparation for changing the subject in the next step. Include familiar or commonly used formulae, for example pressure = force ÷ area. Scientific formulae could also be used throughout; note that in Science, formulae are sometimes referred to as equations. This step is also a good opportunity to practise other key skills, such as general calculator use.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Equations inequalities and formulae_7_Change the subject of a formula (one step)
small_step_name: Change the subject of a formula (one step)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students rearrange simple formulae that involve one step of working to change the subject. Drawing comparisons to solving equations is useful as students begin to explore this concept. Bar models could also be used to build ideas related to changing the subject of a formula, for example if a + b = c, then c − a = b and so on. Ensure that students understand that the subject of a formula is the variable that is isolated and equal to an expression involving other variables. Discuss the fact that the subject of a formula can appear on either the left- or right-hand side of the = sign; for example, r is the subject of both r = m + p and m + p = r. When working with formulae involving squares, highlight that square rooting will give two possible values; for example, if t2 = y, then t = ± y.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Equations inequalities and formulae_8_Change the subject of a formula (two steps)
small_step_name: Change the subject of a formula (two steps)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: Building on the previous small step, students now rearrange simple formulae that involve two steps of working to change the subject. As with the previous step, comparisons between solving 2-step equations and rearranging similar formulae are useful to help students develop their understanding of this concept. Ensure that formulae are given in a variety of forms involving multiplication or division and addition or subtraction. Highlight how the subject of a formula can appear on either side of the = sign. Where appropriate, students could be challenged to rearrange formulae such as x = 3(y − 2) to make y the subject.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Equations inequalities and formulae_9_Change the subject of a complex formula E
small_step_name: Change the subject of a complex formula E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students rearrange more complex formulae that require multiple steps to rearrange. Begin by introducing formulae where grouped terms can be added or subtracted using one step, for example making p the subject of m = p + 2f by subtracting 2f from both sides. Students can then move on to strategies for rearranging formulae where the proposed subject has a negative coefficient, for example making t the subject of m = p − 2t. Students should also be given opportunities to rearrange formulae with squared/cubed terms and/or square/cube roots, discussing the fact that square rooting gives both a positive and a negative root. Familiar formulae from Maths and/or Science could be used throughout.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Fractions_1_Add and subtract fractions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students add and subtract fractions, developing learning from Year 7 Students may need a reminder of how to add and subtract fractions. Representations such as bar models and number lines can be useful to support understanding. Ensure that students practise a variety of questions, including calculating with more than two fractions and working with integers and mixed numbers. When solving contextual problems, it can be helpful to consider the approach that would be used if the numbers were integers, then apply that approach to the fractions. Encourage students to use estimation to establish whether their answers are reasonable and to verify, using a calculator.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Fractions_2_Multiply and divide fractions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate their understanding of multiplying and dividing fractions, building on learning from Year 8. They also multiply and divide mixed numbers. Start with simple abstract calculations, before progressing to more complex problems. Students need to select and perform the correct operations to solve a problem, so give them time to discuss whether the problem requires a multiplication or a division. Pictorial representations such as bar models are useful in supporting students to choose the correct operation. It may be useful to initially show similar problems with integers. Calculators can be used to check answers, if appropriate.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Fractions_3_Fraction of an amount
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of fractions of an amount from Year 7 and solve problems in various forms. Emphasise that finding a fraction of an amount is equivalent to multiplying the amount by the fraction, linking this concept to the previous step. Begin with unit and non-unit fractions of amounts. As students’ confidence develops, move on to problems that involve increasing or decreasing an amount by a fraction or using a fraction to find the whole or other amounts. Using bar models can be helpful for students to understand the structure of the problem and to develop conceptual understanding. Calculators may be used, if required.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Percentages_1_Percentage increase and decrease
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use calculator and non-calculator methods to increase and decrease by a given percentage. Students build on their knowledge of multipliers from Year 8. Although some students may still prefer to find a percentage and then add/subtract to/from the original, understanding multipliers supports efficiency by using a single calculation and supports students with later steps, for example when exploring compound interest. Discuss the multipliers needed for increasing or decreasing by percentages such as 40%, 4% and 0.4%. Drawing bar models to show the relationship with the original amount can be useful. This step also provides an opportunity to incorporate contextual links, such as financial questions.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Percentages_2_Express a change as a percentage
small_step_name: Express a change as a percentage
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students write a change between two amounts as a percentage. Building on their previous knowledge of expressing one amount as a percentage of another, students practise both calculator and non-calculator methods, where appropriate. They become familiar with the general formula change original × 100 to express the percentage change. It is useful to apply contexts in this step, such as changes in population figures or profit and loss calculations.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Percentages_3_Find the original value after a percentage change
small_step_name: Find the original value after a percentage change
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students find original values, given a percentage or a percentage change. Ensure that students understand that the original amount is represented by 100%. Using bar models can be helpful to visualise information, highlighting the percentage that the amount represents and supporting students in deciding the appropriate method to use. Encourage students to apply their knowledge of proportion, fractional equivalents and multiplicative reasoning. They should be proficient in using both calculator and non-calculator methods to determine the original amount.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Percentages_4_Solve problems with percentages (non-calculator)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students consolidate the learning from this block so far, applying their knowledge to reasoning and problem-solving contexts. Students interpret questions and identify an appropriate approach to solve a variety of percentage problems. Bar models are a good representation for percentage problems, useful for visualising the structure of the problem. Model reading questions, asking students what they know and what they need to find out, while highlighting key words such as “increase” and “decrease”. As a non-calculator step, this step offers students a valuable opportunity to reinforce and practise their arithmetic skills.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Percentages_5_Solve problems with percentages (calculator)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students continue to consolidate their learning of percentages by applying their knowledge in problem-solving contexts, now using a calculator. This step focuses on developing students’ ability to problem- solve, not on their arithmetic skills, so there should be an emphasis on choice of strategy rather than arithmetic. Students can continue to use bar models where appropriate to help visualise the problem, as this can help them reason how best to approach the problem. They build on their understanding of increasing and decreasing percentages, expressing changes as percentages and finding the original value.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Percentages_6_Repeated percentage change
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the effect of repeated percentage changes and are guided to understand that these effects are not linear. Students compare calculating repeated percentage increases to the method of using a multiplier such as 1.25n, highlighting the efficiency of the latter method. This will be revisited when studying compound interest, enhancing their understanding further. Using a bar model, students visualise and understand why the sum of individual percentage changes does not equate to their combined effect over time. For example, a 10% increase followed by a 20% increase is not equivalent to a 30% increase of the original value.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Percentages_7_Understand interest
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students are introduced to the term “interest” and develop their understanding of the concept. Students consider different scenarios where interest may be applied, for example understanding how it can add value to a savings account or represent the cost of borrowing money. They build on their previous understanding of percentages of amounts and percentage change and apply this in the context of interest. This learning will build the foundation for simple and compound interest later in the block.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Percentages_8_Simple interest
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore how to calculate simple interest. This step allows students to practise calculating percentages of an amount and percentage increase. Often, they will find the percentage of the amount using non-calculator methods, but calculators can also be used if required. Encourage students to read questions carefully to determine whether they are finding the interest or the total value. Students will also become familiar with terms such as “principal amount” and practise applying simple interest to concepts commonly used in credit agreements.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Percentages_9_Compound interest
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore compound interest, understanding how it differs from simple interest, with the interest added to the current value at the end of each year and the next year’s interest calculated using the new value. Bar models can be used to introduce compound interest, demonstrating how the percentage change compounds on the final amount at the end of each period. This step builds on students’ understanding of percentage increase using a multiplier and repeated percentage change. Students look at compound interest in the context of various financial scenarios, such as savings accounts and loans.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Properties of number_1_Factors, multiples and primes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of identifying and distinguishing between factors, multiples and primes, building on learning from previous years. Encourage systematic listing strategies, identifying factor pairs to ensure that students list all the factors of a given number. Include square numbers, so that students are aware that the numbers in a factor pair may not always be different. Ensure that they understand the key differences between factors and multiples, linking multiples to times-table facts. They can find factors of a given number to decide whether the number is prime or not. Counters and bar models are useful representations to develop understanding of the key concepts.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Properties of number_2_Write a number as a product of prime factors
small_step_name: Write a number as a product of prime factors
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of prime numbers to explore prime factor decomposition. Start with integers that only have two or three prime factors, before increasing the number of prime factors and incorporating index notation. Students may use a prime factor tree as a strategy to organise information and identify the prime factors of a given number. Ensure that they understand the concept behind the procedure and make links to the original number and the product. As their confidence increases, students can develop their reasoning skills to make connections between the prime factor decomposition of related numbers.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Properties of number_3_Use prime factors E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students build on their learning from the previous step, where they expressed numbers as products of prime factors. They develop their understanding of prime factors and use them to further investigate numbers. Start by using the product of prime factors to decide whether a certain number is a factor of a given number. Students can then progress to determining whether a number is a square number or a cube number by exploring its prime factors, before looking at square roots and cube roots. They can also explore simplifying fractions and roots of fractions using prime factors.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Properties of number_4_Highest common factor (HCF) and lowest common multiple (LCM)
small_step_name: Highest common factor (HCF) and lowest common multiple (LCM)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students develop their learning from Year 7 by finding the highest common factor (HCF) and lowest common multiple (LCM) of two or more numbers. This is explored using methods other than Venn diagrams, as using Venn diagrams is explored later in the block. Students covered both concepts separately in Year 7, so as well as recapping the prior learning here, it is important to focus on identifying what is being asked within each question and which method they need to use. This is particularly important when answering questions in context, as students often confuse HCF and LCM when applied to a context.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Properties of number_5_Venn diagrams
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore how Venn diagrams visually represent sets and their relationships. Establish that each circle in a Venn diagram represents a set, with the overlap between circles showing the intersection between sets. Students should understand that elements in the universal set that do not meet the criteria of either set are placed outside the circles. They also interpret information from Venn diagrams. Venn diagrams can be used with different types of numbers, allowing students to revisit multiples, factors and prime numbers from earlier in this block. They should create and interpret Venn diagrams involving words as well as numbers.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Properties of number_6_Use a Venn diagram to calculate HCF and LCM
small_step_name: Use a Venn diagram to calculate HCF and LCM
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use a Venn diagram to determine the highest common factor (HCF) and lowest common multiple (LCM), drawing upon prior knowledge of prime factors and completing Venn diagrams. Students should understand that the intersection of the Venn diagram contains common factors. By multiplying these common factors, they can calculate the HCF. Establish that multiplying all the prime factors in the Venn diagram gives the LCM, since common factors are not duplicated in the intersection.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Properties of number_7_Integers, real numbers and rational numbers
small_step_name: Integers, real numbers and rational numbers
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore types of numbers. They should already be familiar with the word “integer”, and this knowledge is built on when exploring rational, irrational and real numbers. It is useful to link the word “rational” to its derived word “ratio” by noting that, for example, 2 3 can result from the ratio 2 : 1 ( 2 3 of the whole). Highlight that recurring decimals can be written as fractions and therefore they are rational numbers. Students have already met irrational numbers such as π and this step provides the opportunity to formalise this learning and the associated language. Formal proof of irrational numbers is not expected at this stage.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Properties of number_8_Introduction to surds E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students develop understanding that a surd is an irrational number that is expressed as a root. This topic is explored in much more depth at Key Stage 4, with the focus here being on identifying simple surds and manipulation. Comparing strategies for collecting like terms may be useful when adding and subtracting surds, for example comparing 2x + 5x with 2 3 + 5 3. This can be emphasised with language such as “2 lots of 3 add 5 lots of 3 ”. Introduce multiplying surds with roots of square numbers, for example 4 × 9 = 2 × 3 = 6 = 36. Use fact families to establish how to divide surds, for example 3 × 5 = 15 and 15 ÷ 3 = 5 Highlight relationships when multiplying two surds with the same value, for example 7 × 7 = 7

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Rates_1_Speed, distance and time
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students develop their understanding of the relationship between speed, distance and time, building on learning from Year 7 Remind students that speed is a compound measure. For example, express 60 mph as “60 miles travelled for every hour”, as this emphasises the meaning of “per” and why speed is described as a rate. Representations such as double number lines, bar models and ratio tables can be useful to highlight strategies to solve problems. Encourage students to focus on understanding the context of a question by looking at the units given. For example, a speed in km/h can be calculated by dividing the number of kilometres (distance) by the time.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Rates_2_Distance–time graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students interpret and draw journeys on distance–time graphs, consolidating and building on learning from Year 7 Remind students of the key features of distance–time graphs, such as diagonal line segments indicating motion and horizontal line segments representing an object being stationary. Link the gradient on a distance–time graph to speed, emphasising that the steeper a line segment, the greater the speed, and that the average speed is calculated by finding the gradient of the line segment. As students’ confidence increases, they could represent journeys given the average speed, as well as calculating the average moving speed of an entire journey, if appropriate.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Rates_3_Flow problems and their graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore flow problems by comparing how different-shaped containers are filled or drained. Start by comparing the filling of containers in the shape of prisms or cylinders with identical heights but differing bases, highlighting the effect that the shape has on how quickly it fills. Show that the graphs related to the time taken and depth of liquid in each container form a straight line, as the rate of flow is constant. Move on to containers with non-parallel sides, for example a conical or boiling flask, to draw out that the depth of the water will increase more quickly when the container is narrower and more slowly when it is wider. Show that, when plotted, changes in the width of the container are represented by a curve and form a non-linear graph.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Rates_4_Rates of change and their units
small_step_name: Rates of change and their units
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the units involved in rate of change questions. Explain that rates are compound measures, indicating that one unit changes relative to another, for example metres per second, litres per minute. Encourage students to think of rates as “ per ”, where time is often a factor, but not always (for example, cost of fuel per mile travelled). Representations such as double number lines or graphs help students to visualise that the units are directly proportional. Emphasise the importance of interpreting the gradient of a graph in context, linking the concept of rate of change to the gradient.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Rates_5_Convert compound units E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students convert compound measures, for example converting metres per second to kilometres per hour. Encourage students to plan their solution by taking a step-by- step approach, such as converting from metres per second to metres per minute, then to metres per hour, then to kilometres per hour. This process helps to reinforce their understanding of unit conversions. This step also provides opportunities to interleave questions involving speed and flow rates to build connections across different contexts. Additionally, using “goal-free” questions can provide an accessible entry point for students, allowing them to explore and practise conversions, without the need for a specific answer.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Standard form_1_Numbers in standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of standard form from Year 8, revisiting conversion to and from standard form, as well as solving problems with standard form. Remind students of the correct form: A × 10n, where 1 ≤ A < 10 and n is an integer. Students need to be able to recognise when a number is in standard form. This can be encouraged through presenting numbers such as 12.6 × 105 and asking them to convert to standard form. A place value chart can be used to support students’ understanding of standard form, if appropriate. Using numbers in context, such as populations and bacteria, helps to highlight why standard form is an efficient way to write large or small numbers.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Standard form_2_Compare and order numbers in standard form
small_step_name: Compare and order numbers in standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students compare and order numbers given in standard form, ordinary form and words. Discuss strategies for comparing numbers, such as starting by considering the powers of 10. Students should recognise that they do not always need to convert to ordinary form. Instead, they can compare the powers of 10 or, if the two numbers have the same power of 10, they can compare the value of A. Revisiting ordering decimal numbers will help to avoid any misconceptions that may be a barrier to ordering numbers in standard form. Place value charts can be used to deepen students’ understanding.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Standard form_3_Multiply and divide numbers in standard form
small_step_name: Multiply and divide numbers in standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students multiply and divide numbers in standard form. Students were introduced to index laws in Year 8, but this may need recapping. Draw attention to the commutative properties of multiplication and the difference from division, and to the need for brackets in division calculations. Ensure that students are confident with adjusting numbers to correct standard form for dealing with answers such as 30 × 107 Encourage students to develop their mental calculation skills with numbers in standard form. Using a calculator to check their answers can support learning.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Autumn__Standard form_4_Add and subtract numbers in standard form
small_step_name: Add and subtract numbers in standard form
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students add and subtract numbers written in standard form. The idea of “unitising” could be used to support calculations. For example, 3 thousand add 4 thousand is 7 thousand, so 3 × 103 + 4 × 103 = 7 × 103. As students’ confidence develops, explore questions such as 3 × 104 + 8 × 104, which require answers to be adjusted to standard form. Discuss when it is necessary to convert numbers written in standard form to ordinary numbers before adding or subtracting. As with the previous step, mental strategies can be explored, and calculators can be used to check answers.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Algebraic manipulation_1_Expand single brackets and simplify
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students review how to expand and simplify expressions involving single brackets. Students should have prior knowledge of expanding and simplifying expressions of the form 3 ± 4x(x ± 5) and 3(x ± 2) ± 4x(x ± 5). Remind them that each term inside the bracket must be multiplied by the coefficient of the bracket before simplifying. Ensure that they are confident working with both positive and negative terms. Area models and algebra tiles can be a helpful visual tool for representing expressions and supporting understanding. As confidence develops, students can progress to expressions with more algebraic terms, for example a(2a + 3b) − a(4a − 5b).

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Algebraic manipulation_2_Factorise into a single bracket
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students review their understanding of factorising expressions into a single bracket, building on prior knowledge from Year 8 Remind students that factorising is the inverse of expanding. Using an area model and asking students to identify unknown terms when the area is given can help to reinforce the connection between expanding and factorising. Ensure that students are confident working with both positive and negative terms. Start with examples with positive numerical factors, before progressing to negative factors, algebraic factors and a combination of numerical and algebraic factors. Encourage students to check their factorised expressions by expanding.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Algebraic manipulation_3_Expand double brackets
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students expand double brackets of the form (x ± a)(x ± b), sometimes referred to as expanding a pair of binomials. Students may have encountered this learning in Year 8 as an extend step. Using the grid method alongside algebra tiles can help students to make connections when multiplying terms, particularly by reinforcing links with the area model. Start with expressions using only positive terms to build understanding. Once students are confident, move on to brackets involving negative terms. Encourage students to explore unfamiliar forms, such as (x + 5)2 or (x + 3)(4 + x). Introducing and discussing the term “quadratic” will support them with making links to quadratic graphs.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Algebraic manipulation_4_Expand more complex double brackets
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their learning from the previous step to explore double brackets of the form (ax ± b)(cx ± d). Students need a secure understanding of algebraic simplification and operations involving negative numbers. Start with only positive terms within the expression, before introducing negative terms. Recap the use of an area model as a visual prompt to support discussion on expanding double brackets, using algebra tiles if necessary. Expressions in unfamiliar forms such as (3x + 2)2 or (3x + 2)(3 + 5x) could also be explored. As in the previous step, discuss the word “quadratic” to help students make links to quadratic graphs.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Algebraic manipulation_5_Use identities
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of simplifying expressions and expanding single and double brackets by applying this knowledge to algebraic identities. Students learn to identify and use identities to determine unknown values. At this stage, they should begin to recognise that in an identity, the coefficients of matching terms on both sides must be equal, for example if ax + by æ 5x + y, then a = 5 and b = 1. Using a Frayer model can help students explore the key features of an identity and differentiate it from equations and expressions, with non-examples being particularly useful for clarity. Start with simple identities such as 1 2a æ a ÷ 2, then move on to more complex examples that involve simplifying and comparing coefficients to solve for unknowns.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Algebraic manipulation_6_Factorise quadratic expressions
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students factorise quadratic expressions, a concept that may have been introduced as an extend step in Year 8, focusing on expressions of the form x² ± bx ± c. Start with expressions containing only positive terms, to build confidence, before introducing negative terms. Encourage students to find pairs of numbers with a sum of b and a product of c, as this helps them to develop a systematic approach to factorising. The use of algebra tiles can support conceptual understanding by illustrating that factorising is the inverse of expanding. Before introducing negative terms, it may be necessary to revisit students’ understanding of negative factors. If appropriate, challenge students to factorise quadratics in unfamiliar forms, for example 20 + x − x2

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Algebraic manipulation_7_Solve quadratic equations E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students solve quadratic equations of the form x2 + bx + c by first factorising, building on their prior understanding of both factorising quadratic expressions and solving linear equations. Recap how to factorise quadratic expressions with positive and negative terms, ensuring that students are confident with the process. Students can then move on to solve quadratic equations that are equal to zero. Emphasise the key principle that if a product is equal to zero, then at least one of the factors must be zero. Demonstrate this using simple examples such as ab = 0. Reinforce that factorised quadratic expressions typically lead to two solutions and highlight the connection between the factors and the roots of the equation.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Algebraic manipulation_8_Expand triple brackets E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students expand triple brackets of the form (x ± a)(x ± b)(x ± c), building on their prior learning of expanding double brackets. Start with expressions such as (x2 + 4x + 3)(x + 2) to help them apply familiar methods. Students can then move on to expanding three binomials, first expanding two brackets to form a quadratic, then multiplying this by the third expression. The grid method could be used to support students’ organisation, ensuring that all terms are included. Discuss whether the order of multiplication affects the result, and how this might influence their choice of which brackets to expand first. As confidence develops, explore more complex forms such as (ax ± b)(cx ± d)(ex ± f ) and (x ± a)3

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Constructions and congruence_1_Draw and measure angles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit using a protractor to draw and measure angles accurately and use angle notation, which is essential for subsequent steps in this block. Remind students how to line up a protractor correctly when drawing or measuring, ensuring that they use the correct scale. Encourage them to estimate the sizes of angles before measuring and to think about whether they are acute, obtuse or reflex angles. Students also explore strategies to draw or measure reflex angles, such as creating a 300° angle by drawing a 60° angle and indicating that the greater of the two angles created is 300°.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Constructions and congruence_2_Accurately draw triangles using ASA and SAS
small_step_name: Accurately draw triangles using ASA and SAS
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their understanding of drawing and measuring angles to accurately draw triangles. They use a protractor to draw a triangle accurately when given two angles and the side length between them (ASA) or two side lengths and the angle between them (SAS). Model how to accurately draw triangles. Emphasise the importance of accurate protractor placement and ensure that students extend their lines fully from the measured angle in order to draw the triangle accurately. Encourage them to check their drawing with a protractor and a ruler to verify that it matches the given measurements.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Constructions and congruence_3_Accurately draw triangles using SSS
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use a pair of compasses to accurately construct triangles where three side lengths are given (SSS). Model constructing a triangle with three known side lengths. Emphasise correct placement of the compass point and smooth, steady arcs, as well as measuring the compass width accurately using a ruler. Remind students to label the vertices clearly and reinforce the importance of keeping any construction lines that have been created. Encourage them to use a ruler to check the accuracy of their constructed triangle.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Constructions and congruence_4_Construct an angle bisector
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students bisect an angle using a pair of compasses. Establish that bisecting an angle means that an angle is split into two equal angles. For example, bisecting a 60° angle results in two 30° angles. Once students are confident in bisecting a single angle, present them with other problems, for example bisecting an angle in a polygon. They should also be given opportunities to bisect angles in a given context, for example using an angle bisector to identify a region closer to one side than another in a polygon. Ensure that students keep all construction lines when constructing angle bisectors.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Constructions and congruence_5_Construct a perpendicular bisector
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use a pair of compasses to construct the perpendicular bisector of a line segment. Ensure that students understand that if two intersecting line segments are perpendicular, then they intersect at 90°. They also need to understand that the distance between one end point of a line segment and the point of intersection with a perpendicular bisector is half of the total length of the line segment. Remind students to set their compasses to a distance more than half of a given line segment to ensure that construction lines intersect.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Constructions and congruence_6_Construct a perpendicular from or to a point
small_step_name: Construct a perpendicular from or to a point
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use a pair of compasses to construct a perpendicular from a point to a line or a perpendicular to a line through a given point on the line. When constructing a perpendicular from a point to a line, students should recognise that the shortest distance from a point to a line is along the perpendicular. Emphasise the importance of setting the pair of compasses to a distance that ensures the construction arcs intersect the line at two points, allowing for an accurate perpendicular to be drawn. When constructing a perpendicular to a line through a given point on the line, ensure that students position the pair of compasses correctly to create symmetrical intersection points for an accurate construction.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Constructions and congruence_7_Accurately draw more complex polygons
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students practise drawing and measuring angles by creating polygons other than triangles. They also revisit and apply knowledge of properties of polygons, such as opposite angles in a parallelogram being equal. Encourage students to discuss which part of a given diagram may be best to draw first and ensure that they have strategies to check any drawings by measuring side lengths and angles.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Constructions and congruence_8_Identify congruent shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students identify congruent shapes, understanding that they are identical in size and shape. Discuss how the result of rotating or reflecting a shape is another congruent shape. Note that translating a shape will also produce a congruent shape, although students will not formally be introduced to translations until later in the year. Presenting shapes on a grid is a useful starting point for students to begin identifying which shapes are congruent.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Constructions and congruence_9_Congruent triangles
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students identify the properties of congruent triangles, making connections between accurately drawing triangles and congruence. Discuss the conditions that can be used to establish congruency: ASA, SAS and SSS. Also introduce the condition right angle–hypotenuse–side (RHS). Ensure that students have the opportunity to discuss when there may not be enough information to decide whether a pair of triangles is congruent, such as where the triangles have three angles in common. Using triangle congruence to form geometric proofs is not needed at this stage and will be covered in Key Stage 4

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_10_Borrowing (loans)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on learning from earlier in the block to explore various ways in which adults can borrow money, including bank loans, payday loans and credit cards. Discuss examples in context, such as purchasing a holiday with a deposit and loan, establishing how repayments are structured over time. Students touch on APR (annual percentage rate) when looking at payday loans, recognising how high-interest, short-term borrowing can quickly become expensive. They also explore the concept of student loans, developing an understanding of how repayments work and how borrowing for education differs from other types of debt. This step will help students to consider the potential consequences of borrowing, including the impact of missed repayments.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_11_Spending overseas
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This small step focuses on the financial implications of spending overseas, with students exploring exchange rates, currency conversions and different payment options. Key considerations when travelling overseas are sales tax, the difference between paying with cash or card and phone charges. Compare paying in local currency with students’ own currency and how it affects the total cost, depending on bank and vendor exchange rates. Discuss how if no charge is set by the bank for paying in a different currency, then paying in local currency can be more cost effective, but it depends on the exchange rates that are set by both the bank and vendor. It can be helpful to highlight that British pounds (£), Great British pounds (GBP) and sterling all refer to the same currency. Students may see these terms used in different contexts, especially when comparing exchange rates or making payments abroad.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_12_Insurance
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students continue to apply maths in a range of contexts as they explore insurance. Discuss different types of insurance in everyday life, for example car, travel, household and life insurance. Students examine the financial implications of insurance, including learning about insurance premium tax (IPT). IPT is applied to most general insurance policies, except life insurance. Students look at the standard 12% rate for home, car and pet insurance and the higher 20% rate for travel and mechanical repair insurance, such as boiler cover. They explore the idea of excess and consider how a lower premium does not always mean a better deal. It is also important to discuss how some types of insurance, such as car insurance, are mandatory, while others are optional and may depend on individual circumstances or financial situations.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_1_Understand a bank account
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their Key Stage 2 maths and money skills, with new terminology introduced to enhance financial literacy through everyday examples. Introduce students to the layout and key features of a bank statement. Where possible, use genuine bank statements and household bills, as many banks provide sample statements online. Highlight how deposits and withdrawals affect the balance, reinforcing addition, subtraction and place value skills. Discuss credit balances and overdrawn balances, explaining how a positive balance means that money is available, while an overdrawn balance means that the account is in debt. Discuss direct debits and standing orders, explaining how regular payments appear on statements.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_2_Value added tax
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore how the money they spend on goods and services often includes sales taxes in addition to the actual cost. Discuss that tax is paid on most goods or services in the UK and is normally included in the price displayed. Students explore the different rates that are added, as well as the different types of goods and services affected by each rate. They could also consider other taxes that are applied to some specific goods or services, for example alcohol, tobacco and fuel. Students use previous learning on percentages to both increase by a percentage to find the price paid, and find the original price before tax was added.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_3_Ways to pay
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the various payment options available when purchasing goods and services. Discuss different ways of paying for goods and services. Start with schemes involving a deposit with the remaining balance in instalments over time. Students then look at monthly payments without interest, often seen in “buy now, pay later” schemes, where the full cost is split into equal, interest-free payments over a set period. Finally, they consider paying with interest, where the total cost increases due to the interest charged. This step helps students to understand how interest affects cost and how to manage such payments.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_4_Ways to save
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore different ways to save money. Start by considering savings accounts, such as easy-access accounts for flexibility and fixed-term accounts for higher interest rates. Students look at individual savings accounts (ISAs), which allow savings to grow tax-free. Introduce students to stocks and shares ISAs, which offer the potential for greater returns through investments, although these are covered in more detail in Step 6. Finally, students explore saving challenges, helping them to understand the impact of regular saving and the importance of planning for future financial goals.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_5_Jobs and pay
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore jobs and pay. Start by examining payslips to see how deductions such as tax, National Insurance and pension contributions affect take-home pay. Establish that the amount paid in tax and National Insurance depends on an individual’s level of income. Online salary calculators are useful to demonstrate earnings. Students could also explore how income tax funds public services such as schools and hospitals and how National Insurance helps fund state retirement pensions and welfare benefits.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_6_Investments
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore investments such as stocks and shares. It is important to draw students’ attention to the fact that investments can offer the potential to make money, but also carry the risk of losses. Establish that shares are a form of company ownership that can generate profit, with values that rise or fall on the stock market based on market conditions. Students also explore stocks and shares ISAs, looking at how levels of risk affect potential returns. Finally, they consider commodities, which include physical goods, such as gold, oil and agricultural products, that investors trade based on supply and demand.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_7_Borrowing (buying a house)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the concept of borrowing money to buy a house, focusing on mortgages as a common method of financing. While students do not calculate monthly mortgage repayments using a formula, it is useful to explore mortgage calculators online to investigate the impact of changes in interest rates on monthly repayments. Students calculate deposits, loan-to-value (LTV) ratios, maximum borrowing based on income, repayment periods and the total interest paid over the lifetime of a mortgage. They also consider the effect of changes in monthly mortgage repayments on the duration of a mortgage.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_8_Running a house or a business
small_step_name: Running a house or a business
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the financial responsibilities of running a household or business. Introduce students to different types of bills, including for gas, electricity, water, broadband, TV, mobile phone and council tax. They can compare tariffs and explore how costs vary based on usage and provider. Look at smart meters and how they can be used to manage energy usage. Students learn to read bills, considering standing charges, unit costs and fixed versus variable rates. By comparing providers, they can develop financial awareness, making informed decisions about managing money.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Maths and money_9_Budgeting
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore the importance of budgeting and how it supports informed financial decision-making. Start with familiar scenarios, such as managing weekly pocket money to save for a specific item, before moving on to planning a monthly household budget, factoring in rent, bills, food and discretionary spending. Students also assess value for money by comparing prices and identifying the best value. Discuss with students that budgeting is not only about spending wisely, but also about putting money aside as savings.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Ratio and proportion_1_Direct proportion
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students solve problems involving quantities in direct proportion with each other, building on prior learning from Year 8 Revisit topics such as currency conversion, scaling recipes and “best buy” problems to support students to see how these different contexts are all applications of direct proportion. Double number lines and ratio tables are useful to highlight directly proportional relationships between quantities. Encourage students to discuss the efficiency or suitability of different methods, for example comparing the unit cost of two items or scaling the price of two items so that the amounts of each are the same. Students are not required to work algebraically with direct proportion in this step.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Ratio and proportion_2_Direct proportion and conversion graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit graphical representations of directly proportional relationships, building on prior learning from Year 8 Remind students that direct proportion graphs form a straight line from the origin and model how to estimate values from the graph. Encourage students to discuss strategies, such as scaling, to work out values that are not in the range of the data represented on a graph. For example, if a graph shows 5 miles ≈ 8 kilometres, then 50 miles ≈ 80 kilometres. If appropriate, students could solve problems involving multiple conversion/direct proportion graphs.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Ratio and proportion_3_Inverse proportion
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students solve problems with quantities that are inversely proportional. Students learn that if two quantities are inversely proportional, as one amount increases, the other amount decreases at the same rate. Establish the fact that the product of the two quantities is constant as the quantities vary. Throughout the step, encourage students to discuss the assumptions they have made, for example assuming that every person works at the same rate. Students do not need to describe inverse proportion relationships algebraically, for example y = k x, at this stage.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Ratio and proportion_4_Inverse proportion graphs E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students learn to plot and interpret graphs of inversely proportional relationships. Highlight that inversely proportional quantities are an example of a non-linear relationship and so produce a non-linear graph. Graphing software can be used to illustrate key features of inverse proportion graphs, such as showing that the horizontal and vertical axes are asymptotes. Inverse proportion graphs can be used to highlight the constant in an inverse proportion relationship, for example if a = 20 b , then ab = 20 Show students examples and non-examples of inverse proportionality, to develop their understanding that some relationships are neither directly nor inversely proportional.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Ratio and proportion_5_Ratio problems (whole or a part given)
small_step_name: Ratio problems (whole or a part given)
baseline_ss_wr_desc: This step provides an opportunity to revisit ratio problems, building on learning from Year 8 Bar models are a useful representation to enable students to organise the given information and to decide appropriate strategies to solve problems. Ensure that they encounter a mixture of problems and contexts, where they are given the total, one part or the difference between two parts. Bar models can also support students linking ratios and fractions. Comparing questions with the same ratio and numerical value can help to draw students’ attention towards key information. Goal-free, open-ended problems are useful in assessing understanding and reducing student anxiety.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Ratio and proportion_6_Solve problems with ratio and algebra E
small_step_name: Solve problems with ratio and algebra E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students make links between equivalent ratios where one of the ratios contains algebraic expressions. Begin with algebraic ratios equivalent to 1 : 1, for example x + 3 : 7 = 1 : 1. Highlight that this means that the parts are equal, so x + 3 and 7 are equal and an equation can be formed and solved (x + 3 = 7). Move on to algebraic ratios equivalent to other unitary ratios, for example 2x : 24 = 1 : 2, emphasising that 2x 24. Discuss the relationships between the parts in the ratio, for example “The second part of the ratio is double the first part.” As in previous steps, bar models may be a useful support for students to form equations. If appropriate, students could explore algebraic ratios equivalent to non-unitary ratios, for example 2x : x + 8 = 2 : 3

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Recognise enlargement and similarity_1_Work out unknown lengths and angles in similar shapes
small_step_name: Work out unknown lengths and angles in similar shapes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on Year 8 learning to identify similar shapes, determine scale factors and draw enlargements of shapes. Remind students that shapes are similar when all dimensions are multiplied by the same scale factor, emphasising that the interior angles remain the same. Highlight that if all corresponding sides are in the same ratio, the shapes must be similar. Encourage students to explore how scale factors such as 1 2 still represent enlargements, despite the resulting shape being smaller in size. Discuss that if shape A is an enlargement of shape B, then shape B is an enlargement of shape A. Centres of enlargement are covered later in Year 9

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Recognise enlargement and similarity_2_Solve problems with similar triangles E
small_step_name: Solve problems with similar triangles E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students use scale factors to calculate unknown lengths and angles in similar shapes. Students determine the scale factor of enlargement using corresponding lengths before calculating an unknown length. Ensure that they work with a range of scale factors, including decimals and fractions. Encourage students to explore using ratios to calculate unknown lengths, using both the ratio of lengths within a shape and the ratio of corresponding lengths between two shapes. Provide similar shapes in different orientations, discussing strategies for identifying corresponding lengths.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Recognise enlargement and similarity_3_Ratio in right-angled triangles E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students build on their understanding of calculating unknown lengths in similar shapes to solve problems involving similar triangles. Start with problems where the similar triangles are separate shapes, before progressing to cases where they are connected. Remind students how to identify corresponding lengths, encouraging them to use geometric notation when identifying similar lengths and angles. When dealing with connected triangles, support students in redrawing them as separate shapes in the same orientation to help them with calculating unknown lengths and angles. Students also explore using ratios both within individual triangles and between triangles to establish similarity.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Straight line graphs_1_Lines parallel to the axes, y = x and y = −x
small_step_name: Lines parallel to the axes, y = x and y = −x
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students revisit and extend learning of equations of lines parallel to the axes and the lines y = x and y = −x. Start with lines parallel to the axes before moving on to y = x and y = −x, ensuring that students can both plot and recognise the lines. It is important that they understand that the equation of a line describes a relationship between any pair of coordinates on that line. For example, any point on the line y = 3 will have a y-coordinate of 3. Similarly, any point on the line y = −x will have coordinates of the form (−a, a) or (a, −a). Draw attention to the x-axis being the line y = 0 and the y-axis being the line x = 0

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Straight line graphs_2_Explore gradients
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore gradients of straight line graphs and learn that the value of m in the equation y = mx + c represents the gradient of the line. Start with lines of the form y = mx for both positive and negative values of m, before exploring lines of the form y = mx + c. Recap plotting graphs from a table of values before moving on to sketches of lines and comparing gradients. Draw attention to how the value of m affects the steepness of the line. Students should find gradients of lines on coordinate grids in preparation for finding the equation of a line from a graph. In this step, it is not necessary to investigate values of c, as this is explored in the next step. Graph-plotting software can be a useful tool to support student understanding.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Straight line graphs_3_Explore intercepts
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore how the value of c affects a line. Start with lines of the form y = x + c, highlighting that the value of c is the point at which a line intercepts the y-axis, before exploring lines of the form y = mx + c. Establish that the value of c gives the coordinates of the y-intercept, (0, c). Show that in y = mx + c, y = c when x = 0, reminding students that the equation of the y-axis is x = 0. Highlight the special case of lines of the form y = mx and the related y-intercept, (0, 0). Discuss the equivalence of equations such as y = 3x − 2 and y = 3x + −2. Graph-plotting software can be a useful tool to support students’ understanding.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Straight line graphs_4_y = mx + c
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students combine learning from previous steps to interpret equations of lines given in the form y = mx + c, identifying both the gradient, m, and the y-intercept, (0, c). Build students’ confidence in identifying the values of m and c from equations in the form y = mx + c, before introducing non-standard examples such as y = 6 − 2x and 3x + 2 = y. It is not necessary to look at more complex rearrangements of the equation, such as 2y = 3x + 4, as this will be explored in a later step. Students also build on their understanding that lines with the same gradient are parallel and use this knowledge to write equations of parallel lines.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Straight line graphs_5_Rearrange equations to the form y = mx + c E
small_step_name: Rearrange equations to the form y = mx + c E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students apply their knowledge of rearranging formulae to writing equations in the form y = mx + c. Start with formulae that require one step of rearrangement, for example 2y = 6x + 8, before moving on to more complex rearrangements such as 8x + 2y =10. Ensure that students explore examples where they rearrange equations and then write the gradients and y-intercepts. Emphasise the necessity of writing equations in the form y = mx + c in order to identify the gradient and y-intercept. Students could also explore parallel lines in rearranged formulae. At this stage, it is not necessary to look at examples of equations where y is negative, for example 4x = 7 − y.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Straight line graphs_6_Find the equation of a line from a graph
small_step_name: Find the equation of a line from a graph
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students combine their knowledge of finding gradients and y-intercepts to write equations of straight lines from a given graph. Remind students how to calculate the gradient from a graph. Drawing a right-angled triangle between two points on the line and then dividing the vertical distance by the horizontal distance is a useful strategy. Encourage students to examine the scales on axes carefully before calculating gradients. Ensure that they have opportunities to work with fractional and negative gradients and negative y-intercepts.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Straight line graphs_7_Interpret gradients and intercepts of real-life graphs
small_step_name: Interpret gradients and intercepts of real-life graphs
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students apply their knowledge of finding gradients and y-intercepts of lines to finding and interpreting the gradients and y-intercepts of graphs in context. Exemplify the difference between finding the gradient and y-intercept and interpreting them in context. For example, in a graph representing the cost of a taxi journey for a given number of miles, the gradient represents the cost per mile and the y-intercept represents the minimum fare. Students should see examples where the graph demonstrates direct proportion and examples where it does not, understanding that direct proportion graphs start at the point (0, 0). Discuss why the graphs only show positive values of x and y.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Spring__Straight line graphs_8_Graph inequalities E
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students apply their knowledge of lines parallel to the axes and the lines y = x and y = − x to draw and shade inequalities such as x > 3 and y ≤ x. Inequalities on number lines can help to illustrate the connection between open circles, used to represent “less than” or “greater than”, and dashed lines, which indicate that boundary values are not included. Start with regions for single inequalities such as y ≥ 5 before introducing double inequalities, for example −2 < x ≤ 3. Explore the regions of inequalities such as y > x and y > −x, but not more complex inequalities such as y > 2x + 3. Encourage students to test a point to check their solution.
