small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Summer__Solve equations with squares and square roots_1_Identify the hypotenuse
topic: Solve equations with squares and square roots
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their understanding of square numbers and square roots, together with their knowledge of solving equations, to solve equations with square numbers and square roots. Recap calculating square numbers and their square roots. Students apply this knowledge to solve equations that involve squares and square roots, for example x2 = 16 or x = 5. Emphasise the use of inverse operations to solve the equations and highlight why x2 = 16 has two solutions: x = 4 and x = −4. Explain that both 42 = 16 and (−4)2 = 16, so the equation has two values of x that make it true. Therefore, when solving x2 = a, they are looking for all values whose square is a, which includes both the positive and negative values. As students increase in confidence, they move on to solving equations with coefficients that are not 1 and with numbers on both sides of the equation.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Summer__Solve equations with squares and square roots_2_Determine whether a triangle is right-angled
topic: Solve equations with squares and square roots
small_step_name: Determine whether a triangle is right-angled
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students identify the hypotenuse of a right-angled triangle. Start with several right-angled triangles and ask students to measure the length of each side. Encourage them to identify the side that is opposite the right angle and ask what they notice about its length compared to the other sides. Introduce the term “hypotenuse” and emphasise that this is always the longest side in a right-angled triangle and is always opposite the right angle. Include plenty of examples in different orientations, so that they do not rely on position or direction. Include non-examples to challenge misconceptions. As students develop confidence, ask them to identify the hypotenuses within a compound shape made up of right-angled triangles.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Summer__Solve equations with squares and square roots_3_Pythagoras’ theorem (find the hypotenuse)
topic: Solve equations with squares and square roots
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students explore Pythagoras’ theorem and use it to identify right-angled triangles. Start by exploring the areas of the squares on each side of a right-angled triangle. Use concrete or visual representations to support students in discovering that the sum of the areas on the two shorter sides is equal to the area on the side of the hypotenuse. Guide students to notice and articulate this relationship, leading to the formulation of Pythagoras’ theorem, a2 + b2 = c2. Emphasise that the hypotenuse is represented by “c” or “hyp”. Once students understand the connection, ask them to apply the rule to check whether a given triangle is right-angled, using known side lengths to test if the condition is satisfied.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Summer__Solve equations with squares and square roots_4_Pythagoras’ theorem (find any side)
topic: Solve equations with squares and square roots
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students calculate the length of the hypotenuse, given the two shorter sides, building on their knowledge that the hypotenuse is the side opposite the right angle and that it is the longest side in a right-angled triangle. Start with right-angled triangles in a variety of orientations, encouraging students to identify the hypotenuse before substituting values into the formula a2 + b2 = c2. Emphasise the importance of squaring correctly, adding accurately and remembering to take the square root as the final step. Remind students that answers should often be rounded to a given degree of accuracy, for example 1 decimal place. Encourage students to check whether their answer makes sense by comparing it to the lengths of the other sides. As their confidence develops, introduce problems involving compound shapes, where identifying right-angled triangles is required.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Summer__Solve equations with squares and square roots_5_Use Pythagoras’ theorem on coordinate axes
topic: Solve equations with squares and square roots
small_step_name: Use Pythagoras’ theorem on coordinate axes
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students deepen their understanding of Pythagoras’ theorem by applying it to find the length of any side in a right-angled triangle. Remind students how to identify the hypotenuse and to understand that, when the hypotenuse and one shorter side are known, they need to subtract the square of the shorter side from the square of the hypotenuse before taking the square root. Use diagrams and model the reasoning aloud to support students’ understanding so that they do not simply rely on memorised steps. Once they are confident, introduce questions where the unknown side is sometimes the hypotenuse and sometimes a shorter side. This encourages students to think carefully about which side they are finding and helps to secure a deeper conceptual understanding of the theorem.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Summer__Solve equations with squares and square roots_6_Proofs of Pythagoras’ theorem E
topic: Solve equations with squares and square roots
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this small step, students build on their knowledge of coordinates by using Pythagoras’ theorem to find the length of a line segment between two points. Start in the first quadrant, drawing a line segment and counting the horizontal and vertical distances to form a right-angled triangle. Use these to apply Pythagoras’ theorem. As confidence grows, sketch coordinates without a grid and ask students how they could calculate the length without counting squares. Encourage clear diagram labelling, accurate subtraction of coordinates and interpreting results in context. Students can then progress to working with all four quadrants and reason about distances without always using a diagram.

small_step_id: Year 9_13-14_Summer__Solve equations with squares and square roots_7_Pythagoras’ theorem in 3-D shapes E
topic: Solve equations with squares and square roots
baseline_ss_wr_desc: In this extend step, students explore a range of proofs for Pythagoras’ theorem. Algebraic and geometric proofs are examples of proofs that can be explored. It is useful to begin by using a visual or dynamic demonstration to make the result feel intuitive, before introducing a more formal algebraic or geometric proof. Perigal’s dissection was introduced in Step 3, when students were using the theorem to determine whether a triangle is right-angled, and it may be beneficial to refer back to this to support understanding. This step is an opportunity to revisit the proof in a richer context and highlight that Pythagoras’ theorem is not just a formula to memorise, but a result with multiple clear and convincing justifications.
